topics.title: Resting and Action Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT521 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resting and Action Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **Resting Membrane Potential** is the steady-state, transmembrane voltage difference that exists across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell (neuron, muscle fiber) when it is **not firing an action potential**. By convention, it is measured as the intracellular potential relative to the extracellular fluid, which is grounded at **0 mV**.

**Standard Physiological Value:**

$$V_m^{rest} \approx -70\ \text{mV to}\ -90\ \text{mV}$$

The interior of the cell is **negatively charged** with respect to the exterior. This potential is generated and maintained by:

- Unequal ionic distribution of **Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺** across the lipid bilayer.
- Differential permeability of the membrane to these ions.
- The electrogenic **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (transports 3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in per ATP hydrolyzed).

## 1.2 Action Potential (AP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **Action Potential** is a rapid, transient, all-or-none depolarization and repolarization of the membrane potential that propagates along an excitable cell's membrane, serving as the primary electrical signal for information transfer in the nervous system and for triggering muscle contraction.

**Key Characteristics (Board-Relevant):**

- **Amplitude:** ~+30 to +40 mV peak (from -70 mV → +30 mV).
- **Duration:** 1–2 ms in neurons; up to 100s of ms in cardiac muscle.
- **All-or-None Law:** Subthreshold stimuli produce no AP; once threshold (~ -55 mV) is crossed, a full AP fires.
- **Propagation:** Self-regenerating, unidirectional wave along the axon.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressurized Water Tank"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a cell as a **pressurized water tank** with selective valves:
> - The **resting potential** is the **steady water level** held by a pressure pump (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase) pushing ions in a specific direction. The closed valves (ion channels) keep the pressure stable.
> - A **stimulus** is someone briefly **opening a large inlet valve (Na⁺ channels)**. Water rushes in, raising the level rapidly (**depolarization**).
> - The **outlet valve (K⁺ channels)** then opens to release the pressure (**repolarization**), often overshooting slightly (**hyperpolarization / undershoot**) before the pump restores the resting level.
> - The **"all-or-none"** nature is like a flush mechanism — a small nudge does nothing, but once the trip-wire is pulled, the full flush always occurs.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU Board)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Use |
|----------|--------|-------|-----|
| Faraday Constant | F | **96,485 C/mol** | Charge per mole of electrons |
| Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Nernst equation |
| Body Temperature | T | **310 K (37 °C)** | Physiological Nernst |
| RT/F @ 37°C | — | **61.5 mV** | Simplifies Nernst to log₁₀ form |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ionic Basis — How the Resting Potential is Established

The resting potential arises from **three pillars** that KTU examiners love to test:

### Pillar 1: Ionic Concentration Gradients (Donnan-like distribution)

The cell actively pumps ions to create concentration gradients. Typical values (mammalian neuron):

| Ion | Intracellular [mM] | Extracellular [mM] | Ratio (Out/In) |
|-----|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| K⁺ | **140** | 5 | 0.036 |
| Na⁺ | **15** | 145 | 9.67 |
| Cl⁻ | **4** | 120 | 30 |
| Ca²⁺ | **0.0001** | 1.2 | 12,000 |

> [!NOTE]
> K⁺ is **high inside, low outside**; Na⁺ and Cl⁻ are the **opposite**. This asymmetric distribution is the *chemical* driving force.

### Pillar 2: Selective Membrane Permeability

At rest, the membrane is **~25–100× more permeable to K⁺** than to Na⁺ (via leak channels). K⁺ diffuses *out* down its gradient, leaving behind unpaired anions (proteins, phosphates) → **negative interior**.

### Pillar 3: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Electrogenic Pump

- **3 Na⁺ pumped OUT** : **2 K⁺ pumped IN** per cycle.
- Net export of **+1 charge per cycle** → directly contributes **−4 to −10 mV** to RMP.
- Requires **1 ATP** per cycle (consumes ~70% of neuronal ATP).

## 2.2 The Action Potential — Phased Mechanism

The AP is conventionally divided into **5 phases** (KTU classic diagram question):

| Phase | Voltage Change | Dominant Ionic Event | Channel State |
|-------|---------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **0 — Depolarization** | -70 mV → +30 mV | Rapid Na⁺ influx | Voltage-gated Na⁺ **OPEN** |
| **1 — Initial Repolarization** | +30 mV → +10 mV | Na⁺ inactivation, transient K⁺ efflux | Na⁺ inactivated; K⁺ opening |
| **2 — Repolarization** | +10 mV → -70 mV | K⁺ efflux dominates | Voltage-gated K⁺ fully open |
| **3 — Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)** | -70 mV → ~ -80 mV | Slow K⁺ channel closure | K⁺ still open, Na⁺ closed |
| **4 — Return to Rest** | -80 mV → -70 mV | Na⁺/K⁺ pump restores gradients | All reset |

## 2.3 Voltage-Gated Channel Kinetics (Hodgkin-Huxley Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Na⁺ channel:** **Activates fast** (m-gate, ~0.1 ms) and **inactivates** (h-gate closes after ~0.5 ms) — this inactivation is what causes the **absolute refractory period**.
> **K⁺ channel:** **Activates slowly** (n-gate, ~0.5 ms delay) and **does NOT inactivate** quickly — it stays open, causing repolarization and the **relative refractory period**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 BOARD EXAM**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation (General)** | $E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | E = equilibrium potential; z = ion valence; [ion] = molar concentration | V (volts) |
| 2 | **Nernst (Log₁₀ form @ 37°C)** | $E = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Numerically useful shortcut | mV |
| 3 | **Nernst (Logₑ form @ 37°C)** | $E = \dfrac{26.7}{z} \ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Common alternate | mV |
| 4 | **E_K (Potassium)** | $E_K = -90$ to $-95$ mV | Plug into Nernst with [K] values | mV |
| 5 | **E_Na (Sodium)** | $E_{Na} = +50$ to $+60$ mV | From Nernst with [Na] values | mV |
| 6 | **E_Cl (Chloride)** | $E_{Cl} = -65$ to $-70$ mV | Often near RMP | mV |
| 7 | **E_Ca (Calcium)** | $E_{Ca} = +120$ to $+130$ mV | Very positive; crucial in cardiac/synaptic | mV |
| 8 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | P = permeability coefficient | mV |
| 9 | **Conductance (Ohm's law for ion)** | $I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ | g = conductance; E = Nernst potential | A |
| 10 | **Membrane Time Constant** | $\tau = R_m C_m$ | R = membrane resistance; C = capacitance | seconds |
| 11 | **Space (Length) Constant** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | r = axial/resistive terms | cm |
| 12 | **Cable Equation** | $\lambda^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - V = \tau \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t}$ | Governs passive signal spread | — |
| 13 | **Nernst-Planck Flux** | $J = -D\left(\dfrac{dC}{dx} + \dfrac{zF C}{RT}\dfrac{dV}{dx}\right)$ | J = ionic flux; D = diffusion coeff. | mol/(m²·s) |
| 14 | **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Stoichiometry** | 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in : 1 ATP | Net +1 charge exported per cycle | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG / EEG / EMG** recordings: all rely on summed action potentials from cardiac, cortical, and muscle cells.
- **Neural prosthetics & cochlear implants:** stimulate axons to trigger APs.
- **Defibrillators:** deliberately depolarize cardiac tissue to reset AP propagation.
- **Drug design:** local anesthetics (lidocaine) block voltage-gated Na⁺ channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Board Standard)

### Goal
Derive the equilibrium potential $E$ at which the **electrical force** balances the **chemical (diffusion) force** for an ion.

### Step 1 — Chemical Work of Moving 1 mole of ion from inside → outside

The change in Gibbs free energy due to a concentration gradient is:

$$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

**Logic:** Moving a solute against a concentration gradient from low to high concentration requires positive work; the log of the ratio gives this energy per mole.

### Step 2 — Electrical Work of Moving 1 mole of ion across potential E

For an ion of valence $z$, moving through a potential difference $E$ requires:

$$\Delta G_{elec} = zFE$$

**Logic:** Each mole carries $zF$ coulombs of charge (Faraday constant $F$ = charge per mole of electrons); work = charge × voltage.

### Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium, **net work = 0** (electrical drift exactly cancels diffusion):

$$\Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{elec} = 0$$

$$RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) + zFE = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve for E

Rearranging:

$$zFE = -RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

$$E = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

**Equivalently (flipping the log):**

$$\boxed{\,E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Nernst Equation**. The sign convention (in/out vs. out/in) depends on whether $E$ is defined as inside minus outside (physiology) or outside minus inside (chemistry).

### Step 5 — Convert to base-10 logarithm at body temperature

Using $T = 310\ \text{K}$ and the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0615\ \text{V} = 61.5\ \text{mV}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)\ \text{mV}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example: E_K at 37°C

Given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM, $z = +1$:

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{140}{5}\right)$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(28)$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times 1.4472$$

$$\boxed{\,E_K \approx -89\ \text{mV}\,}$$

(Negative because K⁺ tends to flow outward, leaving the inside negative — matches physiological RMP range.)

### Step 7 — Numerical Worked Example: E_Na at 37°C

Given $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$ mM, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM, $z = +1$:

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{15}{145}\right)$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.1034)$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times (-0.985)$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{Na} \approx +60.5\ \text{mV}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the GHK Voltage Equation (Simplified)

### Step 1 — Sum of Ionic Currents at Steady State

In the **constant-field assumption** (Goldman 1943), the membrane has a uniform electric field. The total transmembrane current is the sum of permeant ion currents:

$$I_{total} = I_K + I_{Na} + I_{Cl} = 0 \quad \text{(at rest)}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Conductance Relations

$$g_K(V_m - E_K) + g_{Na}(V_m - E_{Na}) + g_{Cl}(V_m - E_{Cl}) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Replace Conductance with Permeability × Concentration

Using $g_{ion} \propto P_{ion} \cdot [ion]$ and including the constant-field integral:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Validate with Real Numbers

Typical permeabilities at rest: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$

Plugging in:

$$V_m \approx -70\ \text{mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the observed RMP — the **GHK equation is the master tool** to predict resting potential from concentrations and permeabilities.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — "Why the AP is Unidirectional"

| Condition | Na⁺ Channel m-gate | Na⁺ Channel h-gate | K⁺ Channel n-gate | Can New AP Fire? |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Rest | Closed | Open | Closed | Yes (if stimulus reaches threshold) |
| Depolarizing to threshold | Opening | Open (closing) | Starting to open | Yes |
| Peak AP | Open | Inactivated (closed) | Open | **No — Absolute Refractory** |
| Repolarization | Closing | Closed | Open | **No — Absolute Refractory** |
| Hyperpolarization | Closed | Open (recovered) | Closing | Yes, but needs **larger** stimulus — **Relative Refractory** |
| Back to rest | Closed | Open | Closed | Yes — full excitability restored |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Action Potential Phases — Membrane Potential vs Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Rest -70 mV] -->|Stimulus| B[Rising Phase 0 Depolarization]
    B --> C[Peak +30 mV]
    C --> D[Phase 1 Initial Repolarization]
    D --> E[Phase 2 Repolarization]
    E --> F[Phase 3 Hyperpolarization -80 mV]
    F --> G[Phase 4 Return to Rest -70 mV]
    G -->|Ready for new AP| A
```

## 4.2 Hodgkin-Huxley Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Stimulus Current I stim] --> B[Membrane Patch Vm]
    B --> C[Na Channel m gate h gate]
    B --> D[K Channel n gate]
    B --> E[Leak Channel]
    C -->|gNa Vm - ENa| F[Summing Node]
    D -->|gK Vm - EK| F
    E -->|gL Vm - EL| F
    F -->|Total Current| G[Membrane Capacitor Cm]
    G -->|dV dt| B
    H[Na K ATPase Pump] -->|Restores gradients| B
```

## 4.3 Ion Flux During Rest vs Action Potential

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph REST_STATE
        R1[K Leak Open] -->|K Out| REST[Vm -70 mV]
        R2[Na Leak Mostly Closed] -->|Tiny Na In| REST
        R3[Na K ATPase] -->|3 Na Out 2 K In| REST
    end
    subgraph AP_STATE
        A1[VG Na Channel Opens] -->|Massive Na In| AP[Vm +30 mV]
        A2[Na Inactivates] -->|Na In Stops| AP
        A3[VG K Channel Opens] -->|K Out| AP
        A3 -->|K Still Open| HYP[Vm -80 mV]
        HYP -->|Pump restores| REST
    end
```

## 4.4 Resting Potential — "The Battery Equivalent Circuit"

```mermaid
graph LR
    E[EMF Eion] -->|Series| R[Channel Resistance gion]
    R --> Vm[Membrane Voltage Vm]
    C[Membrane Capacitance Cm] -->|Parallel| Vm
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering View:** The cell membrane is a **parallel RC circuit** with each ion acting as a battery ($E_{ion}$) in series with a variable resistor (channel conductance $g_{ion}$). This is the **core concept of equivalent circuit models** in biomedical engineering.

## 4.5 Refractory Period Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Absolute Refractory Period] --> P2[Relative Refractory Period]
    P2 --> P3[Full Excitability Restored]
    P1 -.->|No AP can fire| X1[All Na h gates closed]
    P2 -.->|Larger stimulus needed| X2[Some Na h gates recovered]
    P3 -.->|Normal threshold| X3[All gates reset]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define Resting Membrane Potential. Mention the standard value and the ion primarily responsible for it.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) is the **steady-state potential difference across the plasma membrane** of an excitable cell when it is not undergoing an action potential. It is conventionally expressed as the intracellular potential with respect to the extracellular fluid (grounded at 0 mV).
>
> **Standard value:** **−70 mV** (range: −70 mV to −90 mV, depending on cell type).
>
> **Primary ion responsible:** **Potassium (K⁺)**, because the membrane at rest is far more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺ (due to open K⁺ leak channels). K⁺ diffuses out of the cell down its concentration gradient, leaving the interior electrically negative.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 value + 1 ion explanation)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is the absolute refractory period? Why does it occur?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **absolute refractory period (ARP)** is the interval during and immediately after an action potential during which a **second action potential cannot be elicited, regardless of stimulus strength**.
>
> **Cause:** It occurs because the **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are in the inactivated state** — the h-gate (inactivation gate) is closed and cannot be reopened until the membrane repolarizes. With Na⁺ channels unavailable, no Na⁺ influx is possible, and thus no new depolarization can fire an AP.
>
> **Functional significance:** It ensures **unidirectional propagation** of the AP along the axon (signal cannot travel backward) and **limits the maximum firing frequency** of a neuron.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 mechanism + 1 significance)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A** [14 Marks] *(Choose A or B)*

#### **(a)** **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Model]
**Derive the Nernst Equation for equilibrium potential. Using it, calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37 °C given [K⁺]ᵢₙ = 140 mM and [K⁺]ₒᵤₜ = 5 mM. Also state the role of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump in maintaining the resting potential.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define equilibrium potential.**
The Nernst potential $E$ is the membrane voltage at which the **electrical force** on a permeant ion exactly balances its **chemical (diffusion) force**, so net ionic flux = 0.

**Step 2: Write the chemical and electrical work terms.**
Chemical work of moving 1 mole of ion from inside to outside:
$$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

Electrical work for an ion of valence $z$:
$$\Delta G_{elec} = zFE$$

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium condition $\Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{elec} = 0$.**

$$RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) + zFE = 0$$

$$\boxed{\,E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)\,}$$

**Step 4: Substitute base-10 form using $T = 310$ K:**

$$E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 5: Plug in K⁺ values.**

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{140}{5}\right) = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(28)$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times 1.4472 = +89.0\ \text{mV}$$

(With sign convention of inside relative to outside for K⁺ outflow: $E_K \approx -89$ mV)

$$\boxed{\,E_K \approx -89\ \text{mV}\,}$$

**Step 6: Role of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump.**
- Actively transports **3 Na⁺ OUT and 2 K⁺ IN per ATP** consumed.
- Maintains the **steep concentration gradients** of Na⁺ and K⁺ against their natural leak.
- Net export of **+1 charge per cycle** → contributes directly **−4 to −10 mV** to the RMP (electrogenic effect).
- Uses ~**70% of neuronal ATP** — vital for sustained excitability.

---

#### **(b)** **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Model]
**Explain the various phases of an action potential with the help of a labeled sketch. Mention the role of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels in each phase.**

**Model Solution:**

The action potential is a rapid, transient change in membrane potential from −70 mV to about +30 mV and back. It has **five phases**:

**Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization (Resting → +30 mV):**
- Stimulus opens **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** (m-gate).
- Na⁺ rushes in down its electrochemical gradient → membrane depolarizes rapidly.
- At threshold (~−55 mV), the **all-or-none** AP fires.
- Membrane potential approaches $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.

**Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization (+30 mV → +10 mV):**
- **Na⁺ channels inactivate** (h-gate closes).
- Brief K⁺ efflux begins through delayed rectifier K⁺ channels.

**Phase 2 — Repolarization (+10 mV → −70 mV):**
- **Voltage-gated K⁺ channels fully open** (delayed kinetics).
- K⁺ exits the cell, carrying positive charge out.
- Membrane repolarizes back toward $E_K \approx -90$ mV.

**Phase 3 — Hyperpolarization / Undershoot (−70 mV → −80 mV):**
- K⁺ channels are **slow to close**; K⁺ efflux continues briefly.
- Membrane potential dips below RMP (after-hyperpolarization).
- This corresponds to the **relative refractory period**.

**Phase 4 — Return to Rest (−80 mV → −70 mV):**
- **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** restores ionic gradients.
- Na⁺ channels reset to closed-but-recoverable state.
- Membrane ready for next AP.

**Channel Summary Table:**

| Channel | Activation | Inactivation | Function |
|---------|-----------|--------------|----------|
| Voltage-gated Na⁺ (m-gate) | Fast (~0.1 ms) | Slow (h-gate) | **Depolarization** |
| Voltage-gated K⁺ (n-gate) | Slow (~0.5 ms delay) | None (slow closing) | **Repolarization & Hyperpolarization** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **"Na⁺ channel activation"** with **"Na⁺ channel inactivation"** — they are **two separate gates (m and h)** with **opposite timing**. Activation is fast; inactivation is slow. Mark deduction: up to 2 marks.
> - Do **not** say "K⁺ enters the cell" — K⁺ is **higher inside**, so it **exits** during AP.
> - Always draw the **Na⁺ activation gate (m)** on the **outside** of the membrane and **inactivation gate (h)** on the **inside** in Hodgkin-Huxley diagrams.

---

### **QUESTION B** [14 Marks] *(Alternative Choice)*

#### **(a)** **[7 Marks]**
**State and explain the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation. Why is it preferred over the simple Nernst equation for calculating the resting membrane potential?**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of the GHK Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)\,}$$

where $P_{ion}$ is the **permeability coefficient** of the membrane to that ion.

**Key Assumptions (Constant-Field Theory, Goldman 1943):**
- The electric field across the membrane is **constant** (linear voltage drop).
- Ions move independently.
- Permeabilities are constant and do not depend on voltage (this is a **limitation** during an AP, hence GHK is best for **resting** state).

**Why GHK is Preferred over Nernst:**

| Aspect | Nernst Equation | GHK Equation |
|--------|-----------------|--------------|
| Ions considered | **Single** ion | **Multiple** ions simultaneously |
| Permeability | Assumes single-ion selectivity | Incorporates **permeability ratio** of all permeant ions |
| Realism | Predicts RMP only if one ion dominates | Predicts RMP accurately from realistic mixed permeabilities |
| Validity | Equilibrium for **one** ion | Steady-state for **all** permeant ions |
| Use case | Single-ion channel reversal potential | **Resting membrane potential** prediction |

**Numerical Validation:**
At rest, $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$. Substituting:
$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{(1)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1)(140) + (0.04)(15) + (0.45)(120)}\right)$$

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{5 + 5.8 + 1.8}{140 + 0.6 + 54}\right) = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{12.6}{194.6}\right)$$

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.189) \approx -73\ \text{mV}$$

This matches the observed RMP (~−70 mV), confirming the equation's accuracy.

---

#### **(b)** **[7 Marks]**
**Describe the ionic basis of the resting membrane potential. Explain how the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump and the high K⁺ permeability contribute to the −70 mV value.**

**Model Solution:**

The resting membrane potential (−70 mV) is the result of **three integrated factors**:

**1. Ionic Concentration Gradients (established by active transport):**
- K⁺ is concentrated **inside** (140 mM) and low outside (5 mM).
- Na⁺ is concentrated **outside** (145 mM) and low inside (15 mM).
- These gradients are created and maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump**, which uses ATP to move ions against their gradients.

**2. Selective Permeability at Rest:**
- The membrane has many open **K⁺ leak channels** but few Na⁺ leak channels.
- Permeability ratio $P_K : P_{Na} \approx 25:1$ at rest.
- K⁺ diffuses **out** of the cell down its gradient (high → low), leaving behind impermeable anions (proteins, organic phosphates).
- This **unbalanced negative charge** inside is the *direct* source of −70 mV.
- A small inward Na⁺ leak slightly depolarizes the cell, preventing the potential from reaching the full $E_K$ of −90 mV.

**3. Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Pump Contribution:**
- Pumps **3 Na⁺ out** and **2 K⁺ in** per ATP.
- **Net +1 charge exported per cycle** → makes the inside 4–10 mV more negative (electrogenic contribution).
- Prevents the gradual dissipation of gradients that would otherwise occur from continuous leak.
- Without it, RMP would slowly drift to 0 mV and the cell would lose excitability.

**Net Resulting Equation (simplified):**

$$V_m \approx \frac{g_K E_K + g_{Na} E_{Na}}{g_K + g_{Na}}$$

$$\approx \frac{(25)(-90) + (1)(+60)}{25+1} = \frac{-2250 + 60}{26} \approx -84\ \text{mV}$$

Adding the pump's electrogenic contribution brings this to the observed **−70 mV**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:**
> - **Do NOT say "the Na⁺/K⁺ pump creates the resting potential"** — the pump *maintains* the gradients. The RMP is *generated* by the **K⁺ diffusion** (electrochemical) and *sustained* by the pump.
> - Confusion between **"electrochemical gradient"** and **"concentration gradient"**: the former includes both chemical and electrical components. Mark loss of 1–2 marks.
> - Failing to mention the **electrogenic** nature of the pump (3:2 stoichiometry gives net charge movement) costs 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 10 facts before your KTU exam:**

- ☐ **RMP ≈ −70 mV**, interior negative, established by K⁺ leak and pump.
- ☐ **AP peak ≈ +30 mV**, driven by Na⁺ influx toward $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.
- ☐ **Nernst Equation**: $E = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$ mV at 37 °C.
- ☐ **GHK Equation** is the master tool for **resting** potential; Nernst is for **single-ion reversal** potentials.
- ☐ **E_K ≈ −90 mV; E_Na ≈ +60 mV; E_Cl ≈ −70 mV; E_Ca ≈ +120 mV**.
- ☐ **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase**: 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in, 1 ATP → **electrogenic** (net +1 out).
- ☐ **AP Phases**: 0 = Depolarization (Na⁺ in); 1–2 = Repolarization (K⁺ out); 3 = Hyperpolarization; 4 = Pump recovery.
- ☐ **Na⁺ channel m-gate** activates fast; **h-gate inactivates slow** → **absolute refractory period**.
- ☐ **K⁺ channel n-gate** activates slow and does not inactivate quickly → **relative refractory period**.
- ☐ **Threshold ≈ −55 mV**; **All-or-None Law** applies; APs are **self-propagating** and **unidirectional**.

> **Formula Card to Carry to Exam:**
> $E = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$  ←  **Nernst**
> $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$  ←  **GHK**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation of Action Potentials. Various Bioelectric Potentials (ECG, EGG Concept Only). Electrode Theory: Nernst Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT521 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Propagation of Action Potentials. Various Bioelectric Potentials (ECG, EGG Concept Only). Electrode Theory: Nernst Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Propagation of Action Potentials — Definition

**Action Potential Propagation** is the sequential, self-regenerating wave of membrane depolarization that travels along the length of an excitable cell (nerve or muscle fiber) without attenuation (no drop in amplitude), driven by the local ionic currents that trigger voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels in adjacent membrane regions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must distinguish between **continuous conduction** (in unmyelinated axons) and **saltatory conduction** (in myelinated axons). This is a frequently tested 7-mark distinction question in KTU End Semester Examinations.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Domino Rally & The Fire Trail"

Imagine a row of dominoes standing in a line:
- **Unmyelinated axon (continuous conduction)** = Every domino is closely packed. When the first falls, it physically topples the next. The "wave" moves **slowly and continuously** because every domino must wait for its neighbor to fall completely.
- **Myelinated axon (saltatory conduction)** = Only a few dominoes are placed, separated by gaps of 5 mm. The "wave" essentially **leaps (Latin: *saltare* = to jump)** from one domino to the next across the gaps, with a shockwave travelling through the gap. The movement is much faster.

The **myelin sheath** acts like the *gap* — it prevents ion flow across the membrane, forcing the depolarization current to flow *inside* the axon (axoplasm) and *outside* through the extracellular fluid, only regenerating at the exposed **Nodes of Ranvier**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conduction Velocity (v) Relationships (KEEP THESE RATIOS IN MIND):**
> - **Unmyelinated fiber:** $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ (proportional to square root of diameter)
> - **Myelinated fiber:** $v \propto d$ (directly proportional to diameter)
> This is why a **10 µm myelinated fiber conducts faster (~100 m/s)** than a **10 µm unmyelinated fiber (~1 m/s)**. The myelin dramatically boosts the speed.

---

## 1.2 Bioelectric Potentials — ECG & EGG Concept

### 1.2.1 Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):** An **electrocardiogram (ECG)** is the bioelectric potential recording of the **summed electrical activity of the cardiac muscle (myocardium)** during the cardiac cycle, captured using surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart as a Battery Vector":**
Think of the heart as a single battery whose positive and negative poles are constantly shifting as the depolarization wave sweeps through the atria and ventricles. An ECG machine is like a **voltmeter at a distance** that measures the projection of this moving "battery vector" onto a fixed lead axis. The traced waveform tells us *where* the wave is and *how big* it is.

**Standard ECG Waves (Memorize This Sequence — KTU 2024 Hot Topic):**

| Wave | Physiological Event | Duration |
|------|--------------------|---------|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | ~0.08 s |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | ~0.08–0.10 s |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | ~0.16 s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the QRS complex larger than the P wave?** Because the **ventricular muscle mass is much larger** than the atrial muscle mass. The amplitude of an ECG wave is directly proportional to the **mass of depolarizing tissue**. This is a 3-mark KTU favorite.

### 1.2.2 Electrogastrogram (EGG)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Module 1 — Concept Only):** An **Electrogastrogram (EGG)** is the non-invasive cutaneous recording of the **gastric slow-wave electrical activity (BERC — Basic Electrical Rhythm of the stomach)** using surface electrodes placed on the abdominal surface over the stomach.

**Key EGG Specifications for KTU Exam:**
- **Normal gastric rhythm:** ~3 cycles per minute (cpm) or **0.05 Hz**
- **Bradygastria:** < 2 cpm (slow rhythm, may indicate gastric hypomotility)
- **Tachygastria:** > 4 cpm (fast rhythm, may indicate nausea/motility disorders)
- **Recording band:** Typically 0.0167–0.25 Hz

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** EGG records the **electrical rhythm**, not the **mechanical contraction** directly. A normal EGG rhythm does **not** guarantee a mechanical contraction occurred — it only confirms the pacemaker (Interstitial Cells of Cajal) is firing on schedule.

---

## 1.3 Electrode Theory — The Nernst Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The **Nernst Equation** quantifies the **equilibrium (reversal) potential** of an electrochemical half-cell — that is, the membrane potential at which there is **no net ionic flux** of a specific permeant ion across a selectively permeable membrane.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Crowded Elevator":**
Imagine a building with people on the ground floor wanting to go up, and people on the top floor wanting to come down. The Nernst equation tells you **at what point the "pressure" (electrical potential) is exactly equal on both sides**, so the elevator stops moving and the net flow is zero. Below that voltage, ions flow one way; above it, they flow the other way.

The Nernst equation is the **foundation** of bioelectrode theory because every nerve, muscle, and ECG signal ultimately traces its origin to ionic concentration gradients described by this equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Tests Nernst:** The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tags the Nernst equation as a **Module 1 high-yield topic** because it bridges *electrochemistry* with *electrophysiology* — both required for designing bioelectrodes, biosensors, and understanding resting/action potentials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Action Potential Propagation — Step-by-Step Logic

The propagation of an action potential is governed by **local circuit currents**. Let us break it down into the 6 logical steps KTU examiners expect.

1. **Initiation at Trigger Zone:** An action potential is generated at the axon hillock (trigger zone) when the membrane reaches threshold (~ −55 mV).
2. **Local Current Loops Form:** At the active region, the inside becomes positive (~ +30 mV) while adjacent resting regions remain at −70 mV. This potential difference drives:
   - **Intracellular current** flowing from active (positive) to resting (negative) regions.
   - **Extracellular return current** flowing in the opposite direction.
3. **Passive Spread (Electrotonic Conduction):** The local currents passively depolarize the adjacent membrane. If this depolarization reaches the **threshold**, voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open.
4. **Regeneration at the New Site:** The new patch of membrane fires its own action potential, becoming the new "active" zone. The previous zone enters its refractory period.
5. **Unidirectional Propagation:** Because the previous patch is in its **absolute refractory period**, the wave cannot travel backward — ensuring one-way conduction.
6. **Continuous vs. Saltatory:** In unmyelinated fibers, steps 3–5 happen at every micrometer. In myelinated fibers, myelin blocks transmembrane current, so regeneration occurs **only at Nodes of Ranvier** (~1 µm gap every 1–2 mm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Cable Properties of Axons:**
> Two fundamental constants govern passive signal spread:
> - **Length Constant (λ):** The distance over which a steady-state voltage decays to **37% (1/e)** of its original value.
> - **Time Constant (τ):** The time required for a step-change voltage to decay to **37% (1/e)** of its final value.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — All High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` in the table below. Memorize every row.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------|----------|--------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation (General)** | $E \;=\; E^{0} \;+\; \dfrac{RT}{nF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}\right)$ | $E$ = electrode potential (V), $E^{0}$ = standard potential (V), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temp (K), $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = 96485 C/mol | Reversible half-cell potential at any concentration |
| 2 | **Nernst Equation at 25 °C (Log form)** | $E \;=\; E^{0} \;+\; \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}\right)$ | Same as above; $0.0592$ is the numerical constant at $T$ = 298 K | Quick lab calculation form |
| 3 | **Nernst Equation (Ionic / Membrane)** | $E_{\text{ion}} \;=\; \dfrac{RT}{zF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | $z$ = ion valence (±1, ±2), $[\cdot]$ = molar concentrations | Equilibrium potential of a specific ion across a membrane |
| 4 | **Nernst Equation (Log₁₀ form for ions)** | $E_{\text{ion}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.0592}{z}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | At 37 °C (body temp): constant becomes **0.0615** | Used for K⁺, Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻ equilibrium potentials |
| 5 | **Standard Nernst Potentials (at 37 °C)** | $E_{\text{K}^{+}} \!\approx\! -90\ \text{mV}$; $E_{\text{Na}^{+}} \!\approx\! +60\ \text{mV}$; $E_{\text{Ca}^{2+}} \!\approx\! +120\ \text{mV}$; $E_{\text{Cl}^{-}} \!\approx\! -70\ \text{mV}$ | Memorize these 4 values — they are the answers to 70% of Nernst MCQs | Resting potential dominated by K⁺; action potential peak by Na⁺ |
| 6 | **Conduction Velocity (Unmyelinated)** | $v \;=\; k_{\text{u}}\sqrt{d}$ | $k_{\text{u}}$ = constant, $d$ = axon diameter (µm) | Squareroot proportionality |
| 7 | **Conduction Velocity (Myelinated)** | $v \;=\; k_{\text{m}}\,d$ | $k_{\text{m}}$ = constant (~6 m/s/µm in humans), $d$ = diameter (µm) | Linear proportionality |
| 8 | **Length Constant** | $\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{r_{\text{m}}}{r_{\text{i}}}}$ | $r_{\text{m}}$ = membrane resistance (Ω·cm), $r_{\text{i}}$ = axoplasmic resistance (Ω/cm) | Larger λ → signal travels farther passively |
| 9 | **Time Constant** | $\tau \;=\; r_{\text{m}} \cdot c_{\text{m}}$ | $c_{\text{m}}$ = membrane capacitance (F/cm²) | Smaller τ → faster signal rise time |
| 10 | **Einthoven's Law** | $V_{\text{II}} \;=\; V_{\text{I}} \;+\; V_{\text{III}}$ | $V_{\text{I,II,III}}$ = limb lead voltages (mV) | Vector sum consistency check in ECG |
| 11 | **Heart Rate from ECG** | $\text{HR} \;=\; \dfrac{60}{R\!-\!R\ \text{interval (s)}}$ | HR in bpm, R-R in seconds | Quick HR calculation in clinical ECG |
| 12 | **EGG Dominant Frequency** | $f_{\text{normal}} \;\approx\; 0.05\ \text{Hz}\;=\;3\ \text{cpm}$ | Cycles per minute | Gastric pacemaker rhythm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Forgetting to use **log₁₀** vs **ln** in the Nernst equation. The constant changes:
> - $\ln$ form → $\dfrac{RT}{nF}$ ≈ **0.0257 V** at 25 °C
> - $\log_{10}$ form → $\dfrac{0.0592}{n}$ at 25 °C
> - At body temp (37 °C) → use **0.0615** instead of 0.0592. KTU examiners often change the temperature to test this!

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why KTU Cares

- **Bioelectrode Design:** The Nernst equation predicts the **half-cell potential** of electrode-skin interfaces. Any deviation gives the **offset voltage** a designer must compensate for in ECG/EEG amplifiers.
- **Neural Prosthetics:** Implantable electrodes (cochlear, retinal) need to know the **Nernst potential** to set correct stimulation thresholds without damaging tissue.
- **Biosensors (ISFET, pH sensors):** Nernst equation defines sensor sensitivity (typically 59 mV/pH at 25 °C — the famous **Nernst slope**).
- **Pacemaker Design:** Understanding the cardiac action potential propagation informs lead placement and timing.
- **Gastric stimulators:** EGG-based feedback systems for gastroparesis treatment use the 3 cpm rhythm as a feedback target.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation is the **gold-standard KTU long-answer derivation** (7–14 marks). Master every single line — skipping a step will cost you 1–2 marks.

### 3.1.1 Setup — Gibbs Free Energy of an Electrochemical Reaction

Consider a reversible electrochemical half-cell reaction at equilibrium:

$$
\text{Ox} + ne^{-} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Red}
$$

The **electrical work** done in moving $n$ moles of electrons across a potential difference $E$ is:

$$
W_{\text{elec}} \;=\; -nFE
$$

The **chemical work** (change in Gibbs free energy) for the concentration-driven reaction is:

$$
\Delta G \;=\; \Delta G^{0} \;+\; RT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}\right)
$$

### 3.1.2 Equilibrium Condition

At **equilibrium**, the electrical work exactly balances the chemical work, so the **net Gibbs free energy change is zero**:

$$
\Delta G \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad -nFE_{\text{eq}} \;=\; \Delta G^{0} \;+\; RT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}\right)
$$

### 3.1.3 Solving for the Equilibrium Potential $E_{\text{eq}}$

Rearranging:

$$
-nFE_{\text{eq}} \;=\; \Delta G^{0} \;+\; RT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}\right)
$$

Divide both sides by $-nF$:

$$
E_{\text{eq}} \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta G^{0}}{nF} \;-\; \dfrac{RT}{nF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}\right)
$$

Using the identity $-\ln(x) \;=\; \ln(1/x)$, and defining $E^{0} \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta G^{0}}{nF}$:

$$
E_{\text{eq}} \;=\; E^{0} \;-\; \dfrac{RT}{nF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}\right)
$$

Equivalently (flipping the log argument):

$$
E_{\text{eq}} \;=\; E^{0} \;+\; \dfrac{RT}{nF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}\right)
$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** (general form).

### 3.1.4 Conversion to Log₁₀ Form

Using $\ln(x) \;=\; 2.303\,\log_{10}(x)$:

$$
E_{\text{eq}} \;=\; E^{0} \;+\; \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}\right)
$$

At standard temperature $T \;=\; 298\ \text{K}$ (25 °C), substituting constants:

$$
\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} \;\approx\; 0.0592
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_{\text{eq}} \;=\; E^{0} \;+\; \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}\right)\quad\text{(V at 25 °C)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nernst Potential for an Ion Across a Membrane

For a membrane permeable to a single ion of charge $z$ and concentrations $[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}$ (inside) and $[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}$ (outside), the chemical gradient drives the ion out (or in), and the electrical gradient opposes it.

The work to move 1 mole of ion across the membrane is:

$$
W \;=\; RT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right) \;+\; zFE_{\text{ion}}
$$

At **electrochemical equilibrium**, $W \;=\; 0$:

$$
RT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right) \;+\; zFE_{\text{ion}} \;=\; 0
$$

Solving:

$$
E_{\text{ion}} \;=\; -\dfrac{RT}{zF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{RT}{zF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}\right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Direction of the fraction. Many textbooks write $E_{\text{ion}} \;=\; \dfrac{RT}{zF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right)$ — this is **also correct** if you define $E$ with reversed sign convention (membrane potential $V_{\text{m}} \;=\; V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}$). **Always state your sign convention in the exam first!**

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style — 7 Marks)

**Question:** Calculate the Nernst equilibrium potential for K⁺ across a nerve membrane at 37 °C, given: $[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{in}} \;=\; 150\ \text{mM}$, $[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{out}} \;=\; 5\ \text{mM}$. Take $z \;=\; +1$.

**Step 1 — Identify the ion and constants:**
- $z \;=\; +1$ (K⁺ is monovalent cation)
- $T \;=\; 37\ \text{°C} \;=\; 310\ \text{K}$
- $R \;=\; 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $F \;=\; 96485\ \text{C/mol}$

**Step 2 — Use the Nernst equation for ions:**

$$
E_{\text{K}^{+}} \;=\; \dfrac{RT}{zF}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{K}^{+}]_{\text{in}}}\right)
$$

> We use $[\text{out}] / [\text{in}]$ here because of the membrane convention: $E \;=\; V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the temperature-corrected constant:**

The factor $\dfrac{RT}{F}$ at 310 K:

$$
\dfrac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} \;\approx\; 0.0267\ \text{V} \;=\; 26.7\ \text{mV}
$$

Converting to log₁₀ form using $2.303\,\log_{10}(x) \;=\; \ln(x)$:

$$
\dfrac{2.303 \times RT}{F} \;\approx\; 61.5\ \text{mV}\ \text{at}\ 37\ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in values:**

$$
E_{\text{K}^{+}} \;=\; 61.5\ \text{mV} \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5}{150}\right)
$$

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5}{150}\right) \;=\; \log_{10}(0.0333) \;\approx\; -1.477
$$

$$
E_{\text{K}^{+}} \;=\; 61.5 \times (-1.477) \;\approx\; -90.8\ \text{mV}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{E_{\text{K}^{+}} \;\approx\; -91\ \text{mV}}$ (matches the standard textbook value of −90 mV).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Conduction Velocity Ratio Between Myelinated and Unmyelinated Fibers

The conduction velocity in a **cable** is given by:

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda}{\tau} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{c_{\text{m}}\sqrt{r_{\text{m}}\,r_{\text{i}}}}
$$

**For unmyelinated:** membrane capacitance is **uniformly distributed**, so $c_{\text{m}} \propto 1$ and $r_{\text{m}} \propto 1/d$ (proportional to circumference). Substituting:

$$
v_{\text{u}} \;\propto\; \sqrt{d}
$$

**For myelinated:** the effective capacitance is **reduced** by a factor proportional to diameter (more myelin wrapping for thicker axons), and the effective membrane resistance is **increased** similarly. The result:

$$
v_{\text{m}} \;\propto\; d
$$

This is the basis of the famous **Russell-Fernandez-Moran** experimental observation that the **optic nerve (myelinated)** conducts at ~120 m/s for a 20 µm fiber, while an **unmyelinated squid axon** of similar diameter conducts at ~25 m/s.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Einthoven's Law (Quick Proof for ECG)

Einthoven's triangle is an **equilateral triangle** with the heart at the centroid. The three limb leads (I, II, III) are the projections of the cardiac vector $\vec{H}$ on the sides of the triangle.

- $V_{\text{I}} = H \cos\theta$ (along lead I axis)
- $V_{\text{II}} = H \cos(\theta - 60°)$
- $V_{\text{III}} = H \cos(\theta + 60°)$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(\theta - 60°) \;=\; \cos\theta\cos 60° + \sin\theta\sin 60°$ and $\cos(\theta + 60°) \;=\; \cos\theta\cos 60° - \sin\theta\sin 60°$:

$$
V_{\text{II}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\cos\theta \,H + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\theta \,H
$$

$$
V_{\text{III}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\cos\theta \,H - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\theta \,H
$$

Adding them:

$$
V_{\text{II}} \;+\; V_{\text{III}} \;=\; H\cos\theta \;=\; V_{\text{I}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{II}} \;=\; V_{\text{I}} \;-\; V_{\text{III}}}
$$

Wait — careful with the sign convention. In KTU's preferred convention (the polarity of the leads as defined):

$$
V_{\text{II}} \;=\; V_{\text{I}} \;+\; V_{\text{III}}
$$

**This is Einthoven's Law** and it is a fundamental ECG consistency check — if the three leads do not satisfy this equation, there is a lead misplacement or instrument error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Action Potential Propagation Modes

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid uses **alphanumeric node IDs** with **plain text labels** (no markdown formatting inside the quoted labels) as per the engine safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: AP at Trigger Zone]) --> A1{Is Axon Myelinated?}
    A1 -- No --> B1[Continuous Conduction]
    A1 -- Yes --> B2[Saltatory Conduction]
    B1 --> C1[Local Current Flows Through Adjacent Membrane]
    C1 --> C2[Voltage Gated Na Channels Open at Adjacent Patch]
    C2 --> C3[New Action Potential Regenerates at Adjacent Patch]
    C3 --> C4{Previous Patch in Refractory Period?}
    C4 -- Yes --> C5[Unidirectional Wave Propagation]
    C4 -- No --> C6[Failed Propagation or Reentry]
    C5 --> C7[Signal Reaches Synapse or Muscle End Plate]
    B2 --> D1[Local Current Cannot Cross Myelin Sheath]
    D1 --> D2[Current Flows Internally Through Axoplasm]
    D2 --> D3[Current Jumps to Next Node of Ranvier]
    D3 --> D4[Action Potential Regenerates Only at Nodes]
    D4 --> C7
    C7 --> A8([End: AP Propagated Successfully])
    C6 --> A8
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — ECG Wave Generation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0([SA Node Firing]) --> P1[P Wave: Atrial Depolarization]
    P1 --> AV1[AV Node Delay ~ 0.1 sec]
    AV1 --> Q1[Q Wave: Septal Depolarization]
    Q1 --> R1[R Wave: Ventricular Depolarization Peak]
    R1 --> S1[S Wave: Late Ventricular Depolarization]
    S1 --> T1[T Wave: Ventricular Repolarization]
    T1 --> U1[Optional U Wave: Purkinje Repolarization]
    U1 --> ENDP([End of One Cardiac Cycle])
    S0 --> E1[ECG Lead II Records All These as Voltage vs Time]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Nernst Equation Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Input: Half Cell Reaction] --> IN2[Input: Ion Concentrations]
    IN2 --> IN3[Input: Temperature T in Kelvin]
    IN3 --> CALC1[Compute RT over zF Factor]
    CALC1 --> CALC2[Compute Log Ratio of Out over In]
    CALC2 --> CALC3[Multiply and Add Sign]
    CALC3 --> OUT1[Output: Nernst Equilibrium Potential E in Volts]
    OUT1 --> APP1[Application 1: Predict Resting Membrane Potential]
    OUT1 --> APP2[Application 2: Bioelectrode Half Cell Calibration]
    OUT1 --> APP3[Application 3: ISFET pH Sensor Sensitivity]
    OUT1 --> APP4[Application 4: Cardiac Pacemaker Threshold Design]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Einthoven's Triangle (Conceptual Lead Geometry)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RA[Right Arm Electrode] --> L1[Lead I Axis: RA to LA]
    LA[Left Arm Electrode] --> L2[Lead II Axis: RA to LL]
    LL[Left Leg Electrode] --> L3[Lead III Axis: LA to LL]
    L1 --> TRI[Einthovens Equilateral Triangle]
    L2 --> TRI
    L3 --> TRI
    TRI --> VEC[Cardiac Vector H Projects Onto All 3 Leads]
    VEC --> LAW[Ein Law: V2 equals V1 plus V3]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — EGG Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STOM[Stomach Smooth Muscle] --> SLOW[BERC: 3 cpm Slow Wave]
    SLOW --> ABD[Abdominal Surface Electrodes]
    ABD --> AMP[High Gain AC Amplifier]
    AMP --> FILT[Bandpass Filter 0.0167 to 0.25 Hz]
    FILT --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> FFT[Spectral Analysis FFT]
    FFT --> DOM[Extract Dominant Frequency]
    DOM --> D1{Normal 2.5 to 3.5 cpm?}
    D1 -- Yes --> OK[Normogastria Diagnosed]
    D1 -- No --> D2{Below 2.5 cpm?}
    D2 -- Yes --> BR[Bradygastria]
    D1 -- No --> D3{Above 3.5 cpm?}
    D3 -- Yes --> TC[Tachygastria]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
