## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are modeled on actual KTU University Examination papers for **PEECT521 – Biomedical Engineering**, Module 2. Mark distribution follows the KTU 2024 Scheme: Part A (3 marks each), Part B (14 marks each, with internal choice). Model answers use the same notation, block names, and analytical depth expected by KTU board examiners.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the major functional blocks of a digital ECG machine in the order of signal flow and state the purpose of any three of them.*

**Model Answer:**

Signal flow: **Electrode → Patient Cable → Defibrillation Protection → Lead Selector → Buffer → Right-Leg Drive → Instrumentation Amplifier → HPF → Main Amplifier → LPF → Notch Filter → Isolation Amplifier → ADC → Microprocessor → Display/Recorder**.

Three purposes:
1. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Provides high CMRR (≥ 100 dB) differential amplification of the sub-millivolt ECG signal (gain ≈ 1000).
2. **Right-Leg Drive:** Actively drives the common-mode voltage at the patient back to zero, reducing mains interference by 20–40 dB.
3. **Isolation Amplifier:** Provides galvanic isolation (≥ 4 kV RMS) between patient and ground-referenced circuitry, ensuring patient leakage current < 10 µA as per IEC 60601-1.

**A2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the role of the Right-Leg Drive (RLD) circuit in an ECG machine? Why is it preferred over a simple driven-ground approach?*

**Model Answer:**

The RLD circuit senses the **common-mode voltage** present on the patient's body (mainly 50 Hz interference) through a summing junction, **inverts and amplifies** it (typically gain ≈ 100), and **drives the resulting signal back into the right-leg electrode**. This creates a negative feedback loop that actively nulls the common-mode voltage at the body, reducing mains interference by **20–40 dB** and improving effective CMRR.

It is preferred over a simple driven-ground because the patient's body is *not* a perfect conductor; without a dedicated drive electrode, the common-mode feedback would have to flow through the measurement electrodes, **degrading CMRR** and injecting interference into the differential signal. The dedicated RLD electrode provides a low-impedance return path for the common-mode current, leaving the measurement electrodes to carry only the differential cardiac signal.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Modified]**

**(a)** Draw the complete block diagram of a modern digital ECG machine and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat circuit, derive the closed-form gain of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier used in the ECG front-end. Compute the gain for **R₁ = 25 kΩ** and **R_g = 50 Ω**, and comment on the suitability of this gain for an ECG signal of amplitude 1 mV. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Function — 7 marks**

Refer to the Master Block Diagram in Section 4.1. The student should draw the diagram and explain the function of **at least eight** blocks, with the **isolation boundary** clearly marked. Key blocks to explain:

| Block | Function (must be written) | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode | Transduces ionic currents from skin to electronic signal using Ag/AgCl | 0.5 |
| Patient cable | Shielded, driven shield, 10 wires for 12-lead | 0.5 |
| Defib protection | Resistor + capacitor + Zener to survive 5 kV pulse | 1.0 |
| Lead selector | Switches among 12 standard leads | 0.5 |
| Buffer amp | Provides high Z_in > 10 MΩ to prevent loading | 0.5 |
| RLD | Common-mode feedback, improves CMRR | 1.0 |
| Instrumentation amp | High CMRR differential gain ≈ 1000 | 1.0 |
| HPF (0.05 Hz) | Removes DC offset and baseline wander | 0.5 |
| LPF (150 Hz) | Limits bandwidth, anti-alias | 0.5 |
| Notch (50 Hz) | Removes mains interference | 0.5 |
| Isolation amp | Patient safety, V_iso > 4 kV | 0.5 |
| ADC | Digitization, 12–16 bits, ≥ 1 kHz | 0.5 |

**(b) Instrumentation Amplifier Gain Derivation — 7 marks**

Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation. The key steps the student must reproduce:

1. **Topology description** — two buffer stages feeding a difference amp.
2. **Identify current through Rg** — I = (V₁ − V₂)/R_g.
3. **Output of A1, A2** — V_o1 = V₁ + IR₁, V_o2 = V₂ − IR₁.
4. **Difference stage output** — V_out = (1 + 2R₁/R_g)(V₁ − V₂).
5. **Final gain formula** — **G = 1 + 2R₁/R_g**.

**Numerical substitution:**

$$
G = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25\,000}{50} = 1 + 1000 = 1001
$$

**Comment:** A 1 mV ECG differential input produces:

$$
V_{out} = 1001 \times 1\,\text{mV} = 1.001\,\text{V}
$$

This is well within the **±5 V** supply swing of typical op-amps and provides good headroom for further stages (filters, ADC). A gain of 1000 is **ideal for ECG** because:
- Cardiac signals are 0.5 – 5 mV — the gain brings them to 0.5 – 5 V.
- Excessive gain (> 5000) would saturate the op-amps during large motion artifact.
- Insufficient gain (< 100) would not overcome the ADC quantization step (≈ 1.2 mV for 12-bit, 5 V full scale).

**Awarding marks:**
- Circuit diagram: 2 marks
- Derivation steps 1–5: 4 marks
- Numerical substitution + comment: 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice for Question A)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 2, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Defibrillation Protection Circuit** and **Right-Leg Drive Circuit** used in an ECG machine with neat diagrams. Why is galvanic isolation mandatory between the patient-side and line-side circuits? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An ECG machine uses a first-order RC high-pass filter with **C = 1 µF** and a target cutoff of **0.05 Hz**. Compute the required resistor value. If the same machine uses a low-pass filter with **R = 10 kΩ** and the same capacitor value, what is the resulting low-pass cutoff frequency? Comment on whether the machine is suitable for diagnostic or monitoring ECG. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Defibrillation, RLD, and Isolation — 7 marks**

**Defibrillation Protection (2 marks):** A defibrillator delivers a **5 kV, 100–200 J** pulse into the patient's chest. Without protection, the ECG front-end would be destroyed. The protection circuit uses:
- **Current-limiting resistors (R = 10 kΩ)** in each lead to limit fault current.
- **High-voltage capacitors (C = 10–33 nF, 6 kV rating)** that bypass high-frequency pulse energy to a common node.
- **Zener diodes (V_z = 5.6 V)** clamping the residual voltage to ±(V_z + V_supply).
- **Gas-discharge tube (spark gap, V_br = 90 V)** as a final crowbar to earth.

**Right-Leg Drive Circuit (2.5 marks):** As shown in Section 4.2 — the three limb electrodes feed a summing junction whose output is the common-mode voltage V_cm. This is amplified by an inverting amplifier with gain ≈ 100 and fed back to the right-leg electrode. The feedback reduces V_cm to a few millivolts, effectively cancelling 50 Hz mains pickup.

**Galvanic Isolation (2.5 marks):** The patient must be protected from any fault on the line-powered side. A single ground fault on the line side could otherwise drive lethal currents (mA) through the patient. **Galvanic isolation** (transformer, opto-coupler, or capacitive) breaks the conductive path so that no DC current can flow between the patient and mains. Per **IEC 60601-1**, the patient leakage current must be **< 10 µA** (Type CF, cardiac-floating) and the isolation voltage must withstand **≥ 4 kV RMS**. Optical or capacitive digital isolators (e.g., **ADuM1411**) are now standard.

**(b) Filter Calculations — 7 marks**

**HPF calculation (3 marks):**

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3.18\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

**LPF calculation (3 marks):**

$$
f_{c,LPF} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10\,000 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.01} \approx 15.92\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** A low-pass cutoff of **15.9 Hz** is **far too low for diagnostic ECG** (which requires 150 Hz to preserve QRS morphology and high-frequency notches). It is, however, **acceptable for a simple heart-rate monitor** where only the R-R interval is needed and EMG/mains noise is heavily filtered. For diagnostic ECG, increase the LPF cutoff to **150 Hz** by either reducing R to **1.06 kΩ** or reducing C to **0.1 µF**.

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Block diagram is drawn without the isolation boundary.** Marks are docked because the examiner cannot see the safety architecture. Always draw a dashed line or block labelled "ISOLATION" between the patient-side and line-side.
> 2. **Instrumentation amplifier gain is written as `1 + 2R₁/Rg` without the value of R₁ and R_g used in the question.** A 14-mark question that demands a numerical answer will lose 2–3 marks for omitting numerical substitution.
> 3. **CMRR is given as a number, not in dB.** A CMRR of 100,000 means nothing to a clinician; **CMRR = 100 dB** is the correct way to report.
> 4. **Filter cutoff is computed with wrong units (Hz vs rad/s).** ω_c = 1/RC, f_c = 1/(2πRC). Mixing them is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **No mention of IEC 60601-1 or patient-leakage current.** When asked about safety, *always* cite the standard and the **10 µA** limit.
> 6. **"Right-leg drive" confused with "right-leg electrode."** The drive is the *active feedback circuit*; the electrode is the *passive transducer* on the body. KTU examiners explicitly look for this distinction.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] An ECG machine is a **high-gain, low-noise, biopotential amplifier** with bandwidth **0.05 – 150 Hz** and signal amplitude **0.5 – 5 mV**.
- [x] The full block chain is: **Electrode → Cable → Defib Protection → Lead Selector → Buffer → RLD → Instrumentation Amp → HPF → Main Amp → LPF → Notch → Isolation → ADC → µP → Display/Recorder**.
- [x] The **three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** is the canonical ECG front-end; its gain is **G = 1 + 2R₁/R_g** and CMRR is **≥ 100 dB** with laser-trimmed resistors.
- [x] The **Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** uses active common-mode feedback to improve CMRR by **20–40 dB**.
- [x] The **Defibrillation Protection** uses R, C, and Zener/gas-discharge-tube clamping to survive a **5 kV** pulse.
- [x] The **Isolation Barrier (≥ 4 kV RMS)** is mandatory; patient leakage current must be **< 10 µA** (IEC 60601-1, Type CF).
- [x] **Filters**: HPF 0.05 Hz removes baseline drift; LPF 150 Hz is anti-alias; notch 50 Hz kills mains interference.
- [x] **Sampling theorem**: f_s ≥ 2 × 150 Hz = 300 Hz minimum, but **1 kHz** is standard for diagnostic ECG.
- [x] **ADC resolution**: 12 – 16 bits; the **1 mV calibration pulse** must produce a **10 mm** deflection on the recorder (10 mm/mV scale).
- [x] **Recorder speed**: **25 mm/s** standard; **50 mm/s** for pediatric/neonatal ECG.
- [x] **Safety standards**: IEC 60601-1 (general), IEC 60601-2-25 (ECG-specific), AAMI EC11, AHA recommendations.
- [x] The ECG block diagram is the **template** for EEG, EMG, EOG, and ERG signal-acquisition chains.
- [x] Always mark the **isolation boundary** in the block diagram — it is the *first* thing the KTU examiner looks for.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ECG Recording System. The Human Nervous System: Neurons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT521 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** ECG Recording System. The Human Nervous System: Neurons ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Electrocardiogram (ECG) Recording System — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEECT521 / Module 2):** > The **Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG)** is the bioelectric recording of the heart's electrical activity over time, captured non-invasively via skin-surface electrodes and displayed as a voltage-versus-time waveform. The full recording system is the ensemble of **(i) electrodes, (ii) lead wires, (iii) patient cable, (iv) instrumentation amplifier, (v) signal conditioning (filters, baseline correction, notch), (vi) ADC, and (vii) display/recorder.** According to KTU 2024 scheme, the standard diagnostic bandwidth is **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** and the typical signal amplitude at the body surface lies between **0.5 mV and 5 mV**. The reference standard for clinical ECG is the **12-lead system** (3 Einthoven bipolar + 3 Goldberger augmented + 6 Wilson precordial). ### 1.2 The Human Nervous System — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Neuron (KTU Definition):** A *neuron* is the fundamental excitable cell of the nervous system specialized in the reception, integration, and transmission of electrical and chemical signals. The human nervous system contains approximately **86 ± 8 billion neurons** and **~10¹⁴ synapses**. The nervous system is anatomically partitioned into: - **Central Nervous System (CNS):** Brain + Spinal Cord - **Peripheral Nervous System (PNS):** Somatic + Autonomic (Sympathetic/Parasympathetic) And functionally partitioned into: - **Sensory (Afferent) Neurons** — Carry signals *toward* CNS - **Motor (Efferent) Neurons** — Carry signals *away from* CNS - **Interneurons (Association Neurons)** — Local integration ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wiring Diagram" View Think of the body as a **gigantic building**: - The **ECG system** is the *external monitoring device* clipped to a tenant's fuse box. It cannot see the electricity itself; it only senses the *magnetic/voltage leakage* on the wires that comes out at the skin. To reconstruct the heart's full activity, you need a clever arrangement of "listening posts" — these are the **leads**. - A **neuron** is like the building's internal *intercom system* — a battery-powered cable (the axon) that, when poked the right way, fires a *standardized electrical pulse* (action potential) down the cable to deliver a chemical message (neurotransmitter) at the next door (synapse). The strength of the message is encoded **not in pulse size but in pulse frequency** (rate coding). ### 1.4 Standard ECG Signal Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Amplitude/Time Constants (Adult Resting ECG):** > - P wave amplitude: **0.25 mV** (peak) > - R wave amplitude: **1.60 mV** (peak) — dominant positive deflection > - Q wave amplitude: **< 0.25 mV** (negative deflection) > - Heart rate zone: **60–100 bpm** (normal sinus rhythm) > - Sampling rate for digital ECG: **≥ 500 samples/second** ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 ECG System — Block-Level Functional Architecture The full recording chain operates as follows: 1. **Bioelectric Source (Heart)** — Dipole generator inside the thorax; equivalent to a time-varying current dipole of magnitude **~1 μA·m**. 2. **Volume Conductor (Body Tissue)** — Acts as a 3D resistive medium; typical resistivity of blood ≈ **1.6 Ω·m**, myocardium ≈ **5–7 Ω·m**, lung ≈ **20 Ω·m**. 3. **Skin-Electrode Interface** — Forms the **electrode-skin half-cell potential** of **≈ 200–300 mV** DC offset, which must be cancelled by a **high-pass filter (HPF) at 0.05 Hz**. 4. **Lead Configuration** — Selects a specific vector difference of body-surface potentials. 5. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — Provides **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** at 50/60 Hz; gain typically **100–1000**. 6. **Filters**: - **HPF (0.05 Hz)** → removes DC baseline drift - **LPF (150 Hz)** → limits noise/aliasing - **Notch (50/60 Hz)** → removes power-line interference 7. **ADC** — 12-bit minimum (clinical standard **≥ 1 μV/LSB** resolution). 8. **Display/Storage** — Real-time waveform and patient record. ### 2.2 The 12-Lead ECG System — Engineering Breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** A "12-lead ECG" actually uses only **10 electrodes** and computes **12 independent viewing angles** of the cardiac dipole. | Lead Group | Type | Electrodes Used | Vectors | KTU Tag | |------------|------|-----------------|---------|---------| | Einthoven (I, II, III) | Bipolar limb | RA, LA, LL | 3 | Standard | | Goldberger (aVR, aVL, aVF) | Augmented unipolar | RA, LA, LL | 3 | Standard | | Wilson Precordial (V1–V6) | Unipolar chest | + 6 chest | 6 | Precordial | | **Total** | — | **10 electrodes** | **12 leads** | — | **Einthoven's Law** (key KTU relation): $$I + III = II$$ This must hold at every instant; deviations indicate electrode misplacement. ### 2.3 Neuron — Structural & Functional Engineering A neuron is an electrically excitable cell whose membrane behaves as a **non-linear, voltage-dependent, ion-selective conductor**. The plasma membrane has a **resting capacitance C_m ≈ 1 μF/cm²** and a **resting resistance R_m of order 1–10 kΩ·cm²**. **Functional Parts:** - **Dendrites** — High surface-area input zone; graded (analog) potentials. - **Soma (Cell Body)** — Contains nucleus; integration hub. - **Axon Hillock** — Trigger zone for action potential (highest density of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels). - **Axon** — Active cable; conducts action potential at **1–120 m/s** depending on myelination. - **Myelin Sheath** — Schwann cells (PNS) / Oligodendrocytes (CNS); increases conduction velocity via **saltatory conduction** at the **Nodes of Ranvier** (every **1–2 mm**). - **Axon Terminals** — Output zone; convert electrical to chemical signal (synapse). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — ECG & Neuron > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **one-stop revision sheet** for derivations, problems, and viva questions on this topic. All symbols conform to standard bioelectric conventions. | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Typical Value | |---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Lead Vector Magnitude (Einthoven) | $E = \sqrt{E_x^2 + E_y^2 + E_z^2}$ | E in mV | ≤ 5 mV | | 2 | Einthoven Triangle Closure | $I + III = II$ | All in mV | Exact identity | | 3 | Heart Rate from R-R | $HR = \dfrac{60}{R\text{-}R\,[\text{s}]}$ | bpm | 60–100 bpm | | 4 | Cardiac Axis | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{Lead I amplitude}}{\text{Lead aVF amplitude}}$ | degrees | −30° to +110° normal | | 5 | Electrode Half-Cell Potential | $E_{hc} \approx 200\text{–}300$ mV (DC) | mV | Must be HPF blocked | | 6 | Skin-Electrode Impedance | $Z_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C_s}$ | Ω; f in Hz | < 5 kΩ at 10 Hz | | 7 | Instrumentation Amp Gain | $G = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}$ | dimensionless | 100–1000 | | 8 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | dB | ≥ 100 dB | | 9 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ | dB | ≥ 40 dB clinical | | 10 | Nernst Equation (K⁺) | $E_K = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}\right)$ | Volts | ≈ −90 mV (rest) | | 11 | Nernst Equation (Na⁺) | $E_{Na} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[Na^+]_o}{[Na^+]_i}\right)$ | Volts | ≈ +60 mV | | 12 | Resting Membrane Potential (Goldman) | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | Volts | ≈ −70 mV | | 13 | Conduction Velocity (Myelinated) | $v = k\sqrt{d}$ | m/s | 5–120 m/s | | 14 | Conduction Velocity (Unmyelinated) | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{d \cdot r_m}{2 r_i \cdot c_m}}$ | m/s | 0.5–2 m/s | | 15 | Membrane Time Constant | $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ | seconds | 1–10 ms | | 16 | Length Constant | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | cm | 0.1–1.0 mm | | 17 | Hodgkin–Huxley AP Rate | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{C_m}\!\left(I - g_{Na}m^3h(V-E_{Na}) - g_K n^4(V-E_K) - g_L(V-E_L)\right)$ | V/s | — | | 18 | Power Line Interference | $f_{line} = 50$ Hz (India) / 60 Hz (US) | Hz | Notch filter needed | ### 2.5 Engineering Significance - **ECG systems** are the most widely deployed diagnostic instrumentation in clinical medicine — over **300 million ECGs are recorded globally per year**. - **Neural signal models (Hodgkin–Huxley, 1952; Nobel 1963)** form the mathematical basis of modern neuromorphic engineering and brain-computer interfaces. - Both topics converge in **bidirectional neural-recording implants** (e.g., cardiac pacemakers with neural feedback, closed-loop DBS). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Einthoven's Triangle Law ($I + III = II$) **Statement (KTU 2024 — must be memorized for derivations):** The sum of the potentials in Leads I and III equals the potential in Lead II at every instant. **Given:** - $V_{RA}, V_{LA}, V_{LL}$ are the right-arm, left-arm, and left-leg potentials (w.r.t. ground/RLD). - Lead I = $V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ - Lead II = $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ - Lead III = $V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ **Step 1 — Write the three lead expressions:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Lead I} \;&=\; V_{LA} - V_{RA} \\ \text{Lead II} \;&=\; V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\ \text{Lead III} \;&=\; V_{LL} - V_{LA} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Add Lead I and Lead III (algebraic):** $$ \begin{aligned} I + III \;&=\; (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\ &=\; V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} \\ &=\; V_{LL} - V_{RA} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Recognize that the result equals Lead II:** $$ \boxed{\,I + III \;=\; II\,} $$ **Interpretation:** The three Einthoven leads are not independent — only two are required to fully reconstruct the frontal-plane cardiac vector. This is why Einthoven's triangle is a *closed loop* (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law analogue in volume conductor theory). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heart Rate from R-R Interval **Given:** The QRS complex (dominant R wave) repeats with period $T_{RR}$ (seconds). **Step 1 — Number of beats in 1 minute:** If one beat occurs every $T_{RR}$ seconds, then the number of beats in 60 seconds is: $$ HR = \dfrac{60 \text{ s/min}}{T_{RR} \text{ s/beat}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute typical values:** If $T_{RR} = 0.8$ s (a normal sinus rhythm of 75 bpm): $$ HR = \dfrac{60}{0.8} = 75 \text{ bpm} $$ **Step 3 — The "1500 method" (clinical):** On standard ECG paper at 25 mm/s and 1 mm = 0.04 s: $$ HR = \dfrac{1500}{\text{number of small boxes between R-R}} $$ **Derivation of 1500:** 60 s × 25 mm/s = 1500 mm; one small box = 1 mm = 1/1500 min. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nernst Equation for the Resting K⁺ Potential **Statement:** The equilibrium potential of a single ionic species across a permeable membrane is given by the Nernst equation. **Derivation — Start from electrochemical potential balance:** The free energy change for moving 1 mole of ion $X^{z+}$ from inside (i) to outside (o): $$ \Delta G = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[X^{z+}]_o}{[X^{z+}]_i}\right) + z F (V_o - V_i) $$ **Step 1 — At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$:** $$ 0 = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[X^{z+}]_o}{[X^{z+}]_i}\right) + z F (V_o - V_i) $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $V_m = V_i - V_o$:** $$ zF(V_o - V_i) = -RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[X^{z+}]_o}{[X^{z+}]_i}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Multiply both sides by −1 and rearrange:** $$ V_m = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[X^{z+}]_o}{[X^{z+}]_i}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Convert to log base 10 (using $2.303 RT/F \approx 61.5$ mV at 37 °C):** $$ V_m = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[X^{z+}]_o}{[X^{z+}]_i}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Apply to K⁺ ($z=+1$, $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM):** $$ \begin{aligned} E_K \;&=\; \dfrac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{+1} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5}{140}\right) \\ &=\; 61.5 \times (-1.447) \\ &=\; -89.0 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\,E_K \;\approx\; -89 \text{ mV}\,} $$ This matches the experimentally observed resting K⁺ Nernst potential, validating the model. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Length and Time Constants of a Passive Cable A neuron's axon is mathematically modeled as a **distributed RC transmission line** (cable equation). **Cable equation (one-dimensional, passive):** $$ \lambda^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - \tau \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} - V = 0 $$ where: - $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ (length constant, cm) - $\tau = r_m c_m$ (time constant, seconds) - $r_m$ = membrane resistance per unit length (Ω·cm) - $r_i$ = intracellular axial resistance per unit length (Ω/cm) - $c_m$ = membrane capacitance per unit length (F/cm) **Step 1 — Steady-state DC solution:** Set $\partial V/\partial t = 0$: $$ \lambda^2 \dfrac{d^2 V}{d x^2} = V \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V(x) = V_0 e^{-x/\lambda} $$ **Step 2 — Physical meaning of $\lambda$:** At $x = \lambda$, the voltage has decayed to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its source value. For a typical unmyelinated axon, $\lambda \approx 1$ mm — so the passive signal dies out very fast, which is **why active regeneration (action potential) is necessary**. **Step 3 — Time constant $\tau$:** $\tau$ sets the *temporal* spread. Typical $\tau \approx 5$ ms. This is why an EPSP takes a few milliseconds to peak and decay. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Saltatory Conduction Velocity (Myelinated Axon) **Model:** Conduction hops between Nodes of Ranvier spaced distance $L$ apart. **Step 1 — Time to charge one node:** $\Delta t \approx \tau_m = R_m C_m$ **Step 2 — Current spreads passively through internode (resistance $R_i$):** $$ V(L) = V_0 \, e^{-L/\lambda} $$ For reliable propagation, $L \leq 1.5\,\lambda$. **Step 3 — Effective conduction velocity:** $$ v \;\approx\; \dfrac{L}{\tau_m} $$ **Step 4 — Substituting typical values** $L = 1$ mm, $\tau_m = 0.1$ ms (myelinated): $$ v = \dfrac{10^{-3} \text{ m}}{10^{-4} \text{ s}} = 10 \text{ m/s} $$ Faster large-diameter myelinated fibers reach **120 m/s**, enabling the human reaction time of **~150–200 ms** to be dominated by central processing, not peripheral conduction. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — ECG vs. Neuron (Engineering Comparison) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Insight:** Both ECG and neuronal action potentials are governed by the same Hodgkin–Huxley-style voltage-gated ion channel dynamics. The ECG is essentially a *spatially averaged, far-field* projection of millions of neuronal-style action potentials in the cardiac myocytes. | Parameter | Cardiac Myocyte (ECG source) | Neuron | |-----------|-------------------------------|--------| | Resting V_m | −90 mV | −70 mV | | Peak V_m (AP) | +30 mV | +40 mV | | AP duration | **200–300 ms** (plateau phase!) | 1–2 ms | | Conduction velocity | 0.3–4 m/s | 0.5–120 m/s | | Key plateau ion | L-type **Ca²⁺** channel (Phase 2) | None — fast Na⁺/K⁺ only | | Refractory period | ~250 ms (absolute) | ~1–2 ms | | Myelination | None | Yes (PNS/CNS) | | Trigger zone | SA node (pacemaker) | Axon hillock | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ECG Recording System — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[HEART
cardiac dipole] --> B[BODY TISSUE
volume conductor] B --> C[ELECTRODES
10 placed on skin] C --> D[PATIENT CABLE
shielded leadwires] D --> E[RIGHT LEG DRIVE
active ground] D --> F[INSTRUMENTATION AMP
CMRR 100 dB] F --> G[HPF 0.05 Hz
blocks DC] G --> H[LPF 150 Hz
anti alias] H --> I[NOTCH 50 Hz
line reject] I --> J[ADC 12 bit
500 sps] J --> K[MICROCONTROLLER
DSP filter] K --> L[DISPLAY
LCD or monitor] K --> M[STORAGE
SD or cloud] L --> N[CLINICIAN
interpretation] M --> N ``` **Engineering Reading Guide:** - The **HPF (0.05 Hz)** stage is non-negotiable; without it, the 200–300 mV half-cell offset would saturate the INA. - The **RLD (Right-Leg Drive)** is *not* a passive ground; it actively drives common-mode noise back into the body to improve CMRR. - The **ADC sampling rate (≥ 500 sps)** is dictated by the Nyquist criterion for the highest signal frequency (~150 Hz). ### 4.2 Lead Placement — 12-Lead ECG Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LIMBS RA[RA right arm
red] LA[LA left arm
yellow] LL[LL left leg
green] RL[RL right leg
black ground] end subgraph CHEST V1[V1 sternal 4th ICS] V2[V2 4th ICS left] V3[V3 between V2 and V4] V4[V4 5th ICS midclav] V5[V5 anterior axillary] V6[V6 midaxillary] end RA --> LEAD1[Einthoven I] LA --> LEAD1 RA --> LEAD2[Einthoven II] LL --> LEAD2 LA --> LEAD3[Einthoven III] LL --> LEAD3 RA --> AVR[aVR] LA --> AVL[aVL] LL --> AVF[aVF] V1 --> WP[Wilson Central Terminal] V2 --> WP V3 --> WP V4 --> WP V5 --> WP V6 --> WP WP --> PREC[Precordial V1 to V6] ``` ### 4.3 Neuron — Structural Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB DEN[Dendrites
graded input zone] --> SOM[Soma
cell body nucleus] SOM --> AH[Axon Hillock
trigger zone] AH --> AX[Axon
active cable] AX --> M1[Myelin Sheath
Schwann cell] M1 --> NR1[Node of Ranvier 1] NR1 --> M2[Myelin segment] M2 --> NR2[Node of Ranvier 2] NR2 --> M3[Myelin segment] M3 --> AT[Axon Terminals] AT --> SYN[Synapse
neurotransmitter release] SYN --> POST[Postsynaptic Neuron
graded PSP] ``` ### 4.4 Cardiac Action Potential Phases — 5-Phase Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[Resting -90 mV] --> P1[Phase 0
fast Na+ influx
depolarization] P1 --> P2[Phase 1
Na+ inactivation
K+ efflux brief] P2 --> P3[Phase 2
PLATEAU
Ca2+ influx] P3 --> P4[Phase 3
K+ efflux
repolarization] P4 --> P5[Phase 4
resting -90 mV
automatic in SA node] P5 --> P0 ``` ### 4.5 ECG Waveform — Correspondence to Cardiac Phases ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Atrial depolarization
SA node fires] --> PW[P wave] PW --> PR[PR segment
AV nodal delay 100 ms] PR --> QRS[QRS complex
Ventricular depolarization] QRS --> ST[ST segment
Ventricular plateau Phase 2] ST --> TW[T wave
Ventricular repolarization] TW --> U[U wave
Purkinje repolarization] U --> REST[isoelectric baseline] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mnemonic:** *"**P**lease **Q**uite **R**emove **S**illy **T**oys **U**nder bed"* — sequence of ECG waves and their origin. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) #### **Question 1 (3 Marks): Define the Einthoven triangle. State the law that governs the relationship between its three leads and write the expression.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PEECT521]` **Model Answer:** The Einthoven triangle is an *equilateral* triangle formed by connecting the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) on the body, treating the cardiac dipole as located at the centroid. The law is **Einthoven's Law**: $$ I + III = II $$ where $I, II, III$ are the three bipolar limb lead potentials in mV. This is a direct consequence of Kirchhoff's voltage law applied to the closed loop of the volume conductor. **(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 law + 1 expression)** --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks): Differentiate between graded potential and action potential in neurons.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series-2]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Graded Potential | Action Potential | |---------|------------------|------------------| | Amplitude | Variable (analog) | All-or-none (fixed) | | Propagation | Decremental (passive) | Non-decremental (active) | | Location | Dendrites / Soma | Axon hillock / Axon | | Refractory period | None | Yes (absolute + relative) | | Threshold | No threshold | Sharp threshold (~−55 mV) | | Summation | Spatial + Temporal | None (only frequency coded) | **(3 marks: 1 mark for any three correct distinctions with values)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of an ECG recording system. State the typical specifications for each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Nernst equation and compute the equilibrium potential for K⁺ across a neuronal membrane at 37 °C, given $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution — Part (a) The ECG recording system has the following functional blocks (refer to **Section 4.1 diagram**): 1. **Bioelectric Source:** Heart acts as a time-varying current dipole. Typical dipole moment: **1 μA·m**, frequency content 0.05–150 Hz, surface amplitude **0.5–5 mV**. 2. **Electrodes (10 nos.):** Ag/AgCl disposable, half-cell potential **≈ 200–300 mV DC**, skin-electrode impedance **< 5 kΩ**, placed on limbs and 6 precordial positions. 3. **Patient Cable & Defibrillation Protection:** Shielded, with series resistor (≥ 10 kΩ) and gas-discharge tube / MOV for protection against defibrillator shock (5 kV, ~50 A). 4. **Right-Leg Drive (RLD) Circuit:** An *active* common-mode feedback loop that senses common-mode voltage and drives an opposing current back through the right-leg electrode. Improves **CMRR by ~20 dB**. 5. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** 3-OP-AMP topology, gain **G = 1 + 2R₁/R_g = 100–1000**, **CMRR ≥ 100 dB at 50 Hz**, input impedance **> 10 MΩ**, input bias current **< 50 pA**. 6. **Filters:** - HPF at **0.05 Hz** (single-pole RC, time constant ~3.2 s) → blocks half-cell DC. - LPF at **150 Hz** (2nd-order Butterworth) → limits noise and prepares for ADC. - Notch at **50 Hz** (Q ≈ 8, twin-T) → removes mains interference. 7. **ADC:** ≥ **12-bit** resolution (1 LSB ≈ 1.22 μV if range is ±2.5 mV with gain 1000), sampling rate **≥ 500 sps**, anti-aliasing built into the LPF stage. 8. **Display / Storage / DSP:** Real-time LCD or PC monitor, with digital filters (e.g., 0.5–40 Hz bandpass for clean clinical view), and storage in SCP-ECG or HL7-aECG format. **(7 marks: 1 block diagram + 5 marks for specifications + 1 mark for flow summary)** ##### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Statement of the Nernst Equation:** The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium membrane potential for a single ionic species $X^{z+}$ that is in electrochemical equilibrium across a permeable membrane: $$ E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[X^{z+}]_o}{[X^{z+}]_i}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Constants at body temperature (T = 37 °C = 310 K):** $$ \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \;\approx\; 61.5 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 3 — Plug in values for K⁺ (z = +1):** $$ E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5 \text{ mM}}{140 \text{ mM}}\right) \text{ mV} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm:** $$ \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5}{140}\right) = \log_{10}(0.03571) = -1.447 $$ **Step 5 — Final result:** $$ E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.447) = -89.0 \text{ mV} $$ $$ \boxed{\,E_K \;\approx\; -89 \text{ mV at 37 degrees C\,} $$ **Interpretation:** This value is the **K⁺ Nernst potential** and is close to the *resting* potential of neurons and cardiac myocytes, confirming that the membrane at rest is dominantly K⁺-selective. The slight deviation (~ −70 mV in real neurons) is due to small but non-zero Na⁺/Cl⁻ permeabilities handled by the **Goldman equation** (refer Section 2.4). **(7 marks: 2 marks derivation + 1 mark constants + 2 marks substitution + 2 marks final numerical value with units)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Draw a labelled diagram of a typical multipolar neuron and explain the function of each part. What is the significance of the **Nodes of Ranvier** in neural conduction? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A patient ECG has an R-R interval of 0.75 seconds. Calculate the heart rate. The patient's Lead I amplitude is +1.2 mV and Lead aVF amplitude is +0.8 mV. Compute the cardiac axis in degrees and comment on its normality. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution — Part (a) **Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.3** Mermaid schematic. **Functions of each part (each 0.5 mark):** - **Dendrites** — Branched extensions; receive synaptic input; generate graded (local) potentials. - **Soma (Cell Body)** — Houses the nucleus and Nissl bodies; integrates incoming PSPs. - **Axon Hillock** — Region of lowest threshold; trigger zone for the action potential. - **Axon** — Long projection; conducts APs to terminals. - **Myelin Sheath** — Insulating layer; increases conduction speed and reduces metabolic cost. - **Nodes of Ranvier** — ~1 μm gaps between myelin segments; densely packed voltage-gated Na⁺ channels; site of AP regeneration. - **Axon Terminals** — Release neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft. **Significance of Nodes of Ranvier (3 marks):** 1. **Saltatory Conduction:** AP "jumps" from node to node (Latin *saltare* = to leap), increasing velocity by **5–50×** compared to unmyelinated fibers of the same diameter. 2. **Energy Efficiency:** Ion exchange (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pumping) is needed **only at nodes**, not along internodes — saving ~100× metabolic ATP. 3. **Clinical Relevance:** Demyelinating diseases (e.g., **Multiple Sclerosis**) cause conduction failure at these nodes, producing neurological deficits. **(7 marks: 2 marks diagram + 2 marks part functions + 3 marks Nodes of Ranvier significance)** ##### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Heart Rate Calculation:** $$ HR = \dfrac{60 \text{ s/min}}{T_{RR}} = \dfrac{60}{0.75} = 80 \text{ bpm} $$ $$ \boxed{\,HR = 80 \text{ bpm} \quad \text{(within normal range 60 to 100)}\,} $$ **Step 2 — Cardiac Axis Calculation:** The mean electrical axis $\theta$ in the frontal plane is given by: $$ \tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{Lead I amplitude}}{\text{Lead aVF amplitude}} $$ $$ \tan\theta = \dfrac{+1.2 \text{ mV}}{+0.8 \text{ mV}} = 1.50 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the angle:** $$ \theta = \tan^{-1}(1.50) = 56.3^{\circ} $$ **Step 4 — Determine the quadrant:** Both Lead I and Lead aVF are **positive** → the axis lies in the **normal quadrant (0° to +90°)**. **Step 5 — Normal Range Reference:** - Normal axis: −30° to +90° - Right axis deviation: +90° to +180° - Left axis deviation: −30° to −90° **Comment:** $$ \boxed{\,\text{Cardiac axis} = +56.3^{\circ}\ \text{(Normal Sinus Axis, no deviation)}\,} $$ **(7 marks: 2 marks HR + 1 mark formula + 2 marks axis calculation + 2 marks quadrant comment with clinical reference)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ECG & Neuron Questions (KTU 2024):** > > 1. **Forgetting the sign of the Nernst potential.** Many students compute $E_K = +89$ mV instead of **−89 mV**. The sign depends on the ratio $[X]_o/[X]_i$; remember that K⁺ is **higher inside** the cell. > > 2. **Mixing Einthoven sign convention.** Some students write Lead I = $V_{RA} - V_{LA}$ — the **KTU-marked convention** is $I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$. **One mark lost per wrong sign.** > > 3. **Incomplete block diagrams.** A block diagram must show *all 7 functional blocks* (Electrode → INA → Filters → ADC → DSP → Display → RLD) to earn the full block-diagram mark. Omitting RLD costs 1 mark. > > 4. **Heart rate units.** Always write **bpm** (beats per minute). Writing only "80" is worth 0 marks. > > 5. **Cardiac axis quadrant.** Stating only the angle without identifying the quadrant and commenting on normalcy loses 1 mark. > > 6. **Drawing the neuron without labels.** A bare diagram with no part-names = **0 marks** for the diagram. Use the **Mermaid/SVG** style format with explicit labels. > > 7. **Units in derivation.** KTU strictly checks that the final answer has correct **units (mV, m/s, etc.)**. An answer "−89" with no "mV" is **−0.5 mark**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — ECG Recording & Human Neuron (Module 2):** - ✅ ECG records the **electrical activity of the heart** through skin electrodes; signal amplitude **0.5–5 mV**, bandwidth **0.05–150 Hz**. - ✅ **Einthoven's Triangle** uses RA, LA, LL; governed by the law **I + III = II**. - ✅ **Goldberger Augmented leads** (aVR, aVL, aVF) reference the *Wilson Central Terminal*. - ✅ **Wilson Precordial leads (V1–V6)** are unipolar chest leads; require the WCT as the indifferent electrode. - ✅ **Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** is an *active* common-mode feedback — not a passive ground. - ✅ The **HPF at 0.05 Hz** is essential to block the **Ag/AgCl half-cell potential (≈ 250 mV DC)**. - ✅ **CMRR of the INA must be ≥ 100 dB** for clinical-grade ECG. - ✅ A **neuron** has dendrites (input), soma (integration), axon hillock (trigger), axon (conduction), and terminals (output). - ✅ **Resting potential ≈ −70 mV** (neuron) / **−90 mV** (cardiac myocyte). - ✅ **Action potential** is all-or-none, follows the **all-or-none law**, and is regenerated at **Nodes of Ranvier** in myelinated fibers. - ✅ **Saltatory conduction** speeds up nerve impulses by **5–50×** and saves metabolic energy. - ✅ **Nernst equation** gives the equilibrium potential of a single ion; **Goldman equation** extends it to multiple ions. - ✅ Standard clinical **heart rate range = 60–100 bpm**; calculated as **HR = 60 / R-R(s)**. - ✅ **Cardiac axis** is computed from Lead I and Lead aVF using $\tan\theta = I / aVF$; normal range is **−30° to +90°**. - ✅ **Hodgkin–Huxley model (1952, Nobel 1963)** mathematically describes the action potential using **gating variables m, h, n**. - ✅ The **cardiac action potential has a plateau (Phase 2)** due to **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** — this is what makes the cardiac AP last 200–300 ms (vs. 1–2 ms in neurons).

topics.title: Action Potential of Brain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT521 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Action Potential of Brain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🧠 1.1 What is the Action Potential of the Brain?

The **Action Potential (AP) of the Brain** refers to the rapid, transient, all-or-none electrical signal generated by excitable neural cells (primarily neurons) in the central nervous system, in which the electrical membrane potential rapidly rises and falls following a consistent trajectory. It is the fundamental unit of information transfer within the brain's neural circuitry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An action potential is a self-propagating, stereotypic waveform of **rapid depolarization** (driven by Na⁺ influx) followed by **repolarization** (driven by K⁺ efflux) along the axonal membrane of a neuron, with a typical amplitude of approximately **+30 mV to +40 mV** and a duration of **1–2 milliseconds**, originating from a resting membrane potential of approximately **−70 mV**.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Brain's Electrical Domino Effect"

Imagine a row of dominoes standing in a long corridor. If you push the **first domino** with enough force (above a *threshold*), it falls and knocks over the next, which knocks over the next, creating a **self-propagating wave**. The action potential works exactly like this:

- Each **ion channel** = a domino waiting to fall.
- The **threshold stimulus** = the initial push of the first domino.
- The **refractory period** = the brief delay before a fallen domino can be set up again.
- The **saltatory conduction** (in myelinated neurons) = the wave skipping between dominoes placed farther apart (jumping between Nodes of Ranvier).

Once started, the wave travels in **one direction only**, never backward, because the section behind it is temporarily "exhausted" (refractory).

### 🔑 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Tested Values)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** | **−70 mV** | Baseline polarized state of neuron |
| **Threshold Potential** | **−55 mV** | Trigger point for AP firing |
| **Peak Depolarization** | **+30 mV to +40 mV** | Maximum Na⁺ driven overshoot |
| **AP Duration** | **1–2 ms** | Total spike time |
| **Absolute Refractory Period** | **~1 ms** | No re-trigger possible |
| **Nerve Impulse Velocity** | **1–120 m/s** | Depends on axon diameter and myelination |
| **Body Temperature** | **37 °C (310 K)** | Reference temperature for Nernst calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for Biomedical Engineers?**
> Action potentials are the foundation of all **neurodiagnostic equipment** — EEG (Electroencephalography), EMG (Electromyography), ECG, deep brain stimulators, and neural prosthetics all operate on the principles derived from neural action potential generation and propagation.

---

### 🧬 1.2 The Cellular Players Involved

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "BIG FOUR" Ions of Neural Signaling:**
> 1. **Na⁺ (Sodium)** — 140 mM outside, 10 mM inside → Influx causes **depolarization**
> 2. **K⁺ (Potassium)** — 140 mM inside, 4 mM outside → Efflux causes **repolarization**
> 3. **Ca²⁺ (Calcium)** — Triggers neurotransmitter release at synapses
> 4. **Cl⁻ (Chloride)** — Maintains inhibitory potential equilibrium

The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** continuously maintains this 10:1 concentration gradient using **1 ATP** to pump **3 Na⁺ out** and **2 K⁺ in** against their gradients.

# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ⚙️ 2.1 The Five Phases of a Brain Action Potential

The action potential is not a single event — it is a choreographed sequence of **five distinct ionic phases**. Each phase is governed by specific voltage-gated ion channels that open and close in response to membrane voltage changes.

### 📋 Phase-by-Phase Breakdown

**Phase 1 — Resting State (Polarization)**
- Membrane potential held at **−70 mV** by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump**.
- Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are **closed** (but available).
- Voltage-gated K⁺ channels are **closed**.
- Leak channels maintain the baseline permeability.

**Phase 2 — Depolarization (Rising Phase)**
- A stimulus (synaptic input, sensory receptor) brings membrane to **−55 mV (threshold)**.
- Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open rapidly** → massive Na⁺ influx down electrochemical gradient.
- Membrane potential rapidly rises toward **Na⁺ equilibrium potential (E_Na ≈ +60 mV)**.
- This is a **positive feedback loop**: more Na⁺ in → more depolarization → more Na⁺ channels open.

**Phase 3 — Repolarization (Falling Phase)**
- At peak (+30 mV), **Na⁺ channels inactivate** (fast inactivation gate closes).
- **Voltage-gated K⁺ channels open** (delayed rectifier) → K⁺ efflux.
- Membrane potential falls back toward **K⁺ equilibrium potential (E_K ≈ −90 mV)**.

**Phase 4 — Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)**
- K⁺ channels close slowly, causing brief overshoot below RMP.
- Membrane dips to approximately **−80 mV to −90 mV**.
- This is the **relative refractory period**.

**Phase 5 — Return to Resting Potential**
- Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump restores ionic gradients.
- Membrane returns to **−70 mV**.
- Ion channels reset to their resting configurations.

### 🧮 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — The "Must-Memorize" Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** These four equations appear in nearly every KTU numerical and derivational question. Memorize them with units and assumptions.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation (Single Ion)** | $E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{out}}{[\text{ion}]_{in}}$ | $E$ = equilibrium potential (V); $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K); $T$ = temperature (K); $z$ = ion valence; $F$ = 96,485 C/mol | Reversal potential for **one** ion |
| 2 | **Nernst at 37°C (Simplified)** | $E = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{out}}{[\text{ion}]_{in}}$ mV | Uses log₁₀, $T=310$ K | Quick exam calculation |
| 3 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | $V_m$ = membrane potential (V); $P$ = permeability (m/s) | **Resting membrane potential** considering multiple ions |
| 4 | **Cable Equation (Length Constant)** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | $r_m$ = membrane resistance (Ω·cm); $r_i$ = intracellular resistance (Ω/cm) | How far a signal travels passively |
| 5 | **Time Constant** | $\tau = r_m \cdot c_m$ | $c_m$ = membrane capacitance (F/cm²) | How fast membrane voltage changes |
| 6 | **Conduction Velocity (Myelinated)** | $v \propto \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{R \cdot C_m})}$ | $d$ = axon diameter | **Saltatory conduction** speed |
| 7 | **Threshold Criterion** | $V_{threshold} = -55$ mV | Fixed biological value | All-or-none firing trigger |

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EEG Signal Processing:** The summed action potentials of millions of cortical pyramidal neurons produce measurable scalp voltages (10–100 μV), which EEG machines detect.
- **Neural Implants:** Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) electrodes for Parkinson's disease deliver **130 Hz pulses** to modulate action potential firing.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Decode motor cortex action potential patterns to control prosthetic limbs.
- **Anesthetics:** Most general anesthetics work by **blocking voltage-gated Na⁺ channels**, preventing action potential generation.

---

### 🔄 2.3 Refractory Periods — The "Single-Lane Highway Rule"

> [!NOTE]
> This is **HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU question territory.** Understand both periods.

| Property | Absolute Refractory Period | Relative Refractory Period |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | ~1 ms (during depolarization + most of repolarization) | ~2–4 ms (during hyperpolarization) |
| **Na⁺ Channel State** | Inactivated (cannot be re-opened) | Recovered from inactivation, but closed |
| **Can AP fire?** | **No — impossible regardless of stimulus strength** | **Yes — but requires stronger-than-normal stimulus** |
| **Function** | Ensures unidirectional propagation | Limits maximum firing frequency |
| **Analogy** | Highway under construction — absolutely no entry | Highway with roadworks — slow, but passable with care |

**Maximum Firing Frequency:**
$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\text{AP duration} + \text{ARP}} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 \text{ ms}} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

In practice, neurons fire at **100–200 Hz maximum** due to additional biophysical limits.

# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧪 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Nernst Equation

The Nernst Equation gives the **equilibrium (reversal) potential** for a single ion species — the membrane voltage at which there is **zero net ionic current** for that ion.

### 🔢 Starting Point: Thermodynamic Equilibrium

Consider an ion $X^{z+}$ with charge $z$ and valence, separated by a permeable membrane. Two opposing forces act on the ion:

1. **Chemical Diffusion Force** — drives ion down its concentration gradient.
2. **Electrical Force** — drives ion down its electrical potential gradient.

At equilibrium, these two forces are exactly equal and opposite.

**Step 1: Set up the electrochemical potential equality**

The free energy change of moving 1 mole of ion from outside (o) to inside (i) is:

$$\Delta G = RT \ln \dfrac{[X^{z+}]_i}{[X^{z+}]_o} + zF(E_i - E_o)$$

**Step 2: Apply the equilibrium condition**

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ (no net driving force):

$$RT \ln \dfrac{[X^{z+}]_i}{[X^{z+}]_o} + zF(E_i - E_o) = 0$$

**Step 3: Define membrane potential**

Let $E = E_i - E_o$ be the membrane potential (inside relative to outside):

$$zFE = -RT \ln \dfrac{[X^{z+}]_i}{[X^{z+}]_o}$$

**Step 4: Invert the log ratio using log identity**

Using the property $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$:

$$E = -\dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X^{z+}]_i}{[X^{z+}]_o} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X^{z+}]_o}{[X^{z+}]_i}$$

**Final Nernst Equation:**

$$\boxed{E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{out}}{[\text{ion}]_{in}}}$$

**Step 5: Conversion to base-10 logarithm**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$ and $T = 310$ K (37°C):

$$E = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot 310}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i}$$

$$E = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 310}{z \cdot 96485} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i}$$

$$E = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i} \text{ mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **61.5 mV constant** is the most-tested number in KTU numericals. It is derived from $2.303 RT/F$ at body temperature.

---

### 📊 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Calculate the Nernst equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37°C given $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM. Take $z = +1$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify variables**
- $z = +1$
- $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM
- $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM
- $T = 310$ K

**Step 2: Apply simplified Nernst equation**

$$E_K = \dfrac{61.5}{1} \log_{10} \dfrac{4}{140}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the ratio**

$$\dfrac{4}{140} = 0.02857$$

**Step 4: Take logarithm**

$$\log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.544$$

**Step 5: Final calculation**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.544) = -94.96 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -95 \text{ mV}}$$

This matches the experimentally observed K⁺ reversal potential in real neurons.

---

### 🧠 3.3 Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation — Membrane Potential Under Multiple Permeabilities

Unlike Nernst (which assumes only one permeable ion), the **GHK equation** calculates the actual resting membrane potential when **multiple ions** are permeable.

**Full Form:**

$$V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$

**Where:**
- $V_m$ = membrane potential (V)
- $P_{ion}$ = permeability of membrane to that ion
- $[ion]_o$ = extracellular concentration
- $[ion]_i$ = intracellular concentration

**Key Insight — Permeability Determines the Voltage:**

At rest, the typical relative permeabilities are:
- $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$

Because K⁺ permeability dominates, $V_m$ sits close to $E_K$ (≈ −70 mV), not at the weighted average of all three Nernst potentials.

**During the peak of the action potential**, Na⁺ permeability surges to ~600× its resting value, pulling $V_m$ toward $E_{Na}$ (+60 mV) — this is the depolarization overshoot.

---

### ⏱️ 3.4 Cable Theory Essentials — Length and Time Constants

**Length Constant ($\lambda$):**
The distance over which a passively-spreading voltage decays to **37%** (1/e) of its original value.

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i + r_o}}$$

For intracellular signals where $r_o$ is negligible:

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$$

- Higher $r_m$ (better insulation, e.g., myelin) → larger $\lambda$ → signal travels further.
- Typical values: **0.1–1.0 mm** in unmyelinated axons; **up to 10 mm** in myelinated axons.

**Time Constant ($\tau$):**
The time required for the membrane voltage to reach **63%** (1 − 1/e) of its final value in response to a current step.

$$\tau = r_m \cdot c_m$$

- Typical $\tau$ ≈ **10–50 ms** for neuronal membranes.
- Determines how rapidly a neuron can respond to synaptic inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Myelination increases $\lambda$ (by increasing $r_m$) and **decreases** $c_m$, which is why myelinated axons conduct **100× faster** than unmyelinated axons of the same diameter via **saltatory conduction** (jumping between Nodes of Ranvier).

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the term "Action Potential" as applied to the brain. List any four key characteristics of a neural action potential.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **action potential** is a rapid, transient, all-or-none electrical signal generated by excitable neural cells in the brain, in which the membrane potential rapidly depolarizes and then repolarizes, propagating along the axon.

**Four key characteristics:**
1. **All-or-none response** — Once threshold (−55 mV) is reached, the AP fires at full amplitude; sub-threshold stimuli produce no AP.
2. **Self-propagating** — The AP regenerates itself along the axon without amplitude decay.
3. **Fixed amplitude and duration** — Typically +30 mV peak, 1–2 ms duration, regardless of stimulus strength.
4. **Unidirectional conduction** — The refractory period prevents backward propagation.

**Mark distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + 4 characteristics (0.5 each = 2 marks).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Distinguish between absolute refractory period and relative refractory period.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Absolute Refractory Period | Relative Refractory Period |
|---|---|---|
| **Na⁺ channel state** | Inactivated (cannot reopen) | Recovered (closed, but reopenable) |
| **AP generation possible?** | No, regardless of stimulus strength | Yes, but requires stronger-than-normal stimulus |
| **Timing** | During depolarization + early repolarization | During hyperpolarization (undershoot) |
| **Function** | Ensures unidirectional propagation | Limits maximum firing frequency |
| **Duration** | ~1 ms | ~2–4 ms |

(1.5 marks for table comparison + 1.5 marks for physiological explanation)
