Calculate the axial length of the eye. **Solution:** Depth from cornea to retina (subtract transducer-cornea time $t_1$ as reference): $$\Delta t = t_4 - t_1 = 30.5 - 0 = 30.5 \,\mu s$$ $$d_{eye} = 0.077 \times 30.5 = 2.35 \text{ cm} = 23.5 \text{ mm}$$ This is a **normal adult human eye axial length** (typical range 22–25 mm). ### 3.2 Derivation: Axial (Range) Resolution Axial resolution is the **minimum distance** between two reflectors along the beam axis that can be distinguished as separate echoes. **Step 1:** Two reflectors will be resolved only if the echo from the **deeper** reflector returns to the transducer **after** the echo from the **shallower** reflector has completely died out. **Step 2:** If the transmitted pulse has a spatial pulse length $SPL$ (in meters), then for two echoes to be separable, the second reflector must be at least $SPL/2$ deeper than the first. **Step 3:** The SPL is the product of the speed of sound and the pulse duration $\tau$: $$SPL = c \cdot \tau$$ **Step 4:** Therefore, the axial resolution is: $$\text{Resolution}_{axial} = \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$ **Step 5:** Substituting $c = 1540$ m/s and a typical pulse duration $\tau = 1 \,\mu s$: $$\text{Resolution}_{axial} = \frac{1540 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.77 \text{ mm}$$ This is the **theoretical best** axial resolution in soft tissue — outstanding for ophthalmic use where ocular structures are only a few mm apart. > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Higher frequency transducers (e.g., 20 MHz) allow shorter pulses, improving axial resolution but at the cost of reduced penetration. This is the classic **resolution-vs-penetration trade-off** in ultrasound. ### 3.3 Derivation: Reflection Coefficient and Echo Amplitude The amplitude of the A-Scan spike is governed by the mismatch in acoustic impedance at each tissue interface. **Step 1:** Acoustic impedance is the product of tissue density $\rho$ and sound velocity $c$: $$Z = \rho \cdot c$$ **Step 2:** Typical values (memorize these — frequently tested): | Tissue | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $c$ (m/s) | $Z$ (× 10⁶ Rayl) | |---|---|---|---| | Air | 1.2 | 330 | 0.0004 | | Fat | 950 | 1450 | 1.38 | | Water | 1000 | 1480 | 1.48 | | Soft tissue (avg) | 1060 | 1540 | 1.63 | | Muscle | 1080 | 1580 | 1.70 | | Lens (eye) | 1140 | 1641 | 1.87 | | Bone | 1900 | 4080 | 7.75 | **Step 3:** For an ultrasound beam incident perpendicular to an interface between media 1 and 2: $$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$ **Worked Example — Cornea-to-Aqueous Humor interface (eye):** Assume $Z_{cornea} \approx 1.87 \times 10^6$ Rayl and $Z_{aqueous} \approx 1.48 \times 10^6$ Rayl. $$R = \left(\frac{1.87 - 1.48}{1.87 + 1.48}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.39}{3.35}\right)^2 = (0.1164)^2 \approx 0.0136$$ So only **~1.36%** of the intensity is reflected at the cornea-aqueous interface. The rest transmits into the eye — this is why A-Scan can visualize deeper structures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **R (intensity reflection coefficient)** with the **pressure reflection coefficient** $r_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$. The intensity version is **squared** because intensity is proportional to pressure squared. ### 3.4 Attenuation Analysis Ultrasound loses intensity as it propagates due to: 1. **Absorption** — conversion to heat (dominant in soft tissue). 2. **Scattering** — redirection by small inhomogeneities (Rayleigh scattering). 3. **Reflection** — at macroscopic interfaces. The intensity at depth $x$ follows an exponential decay: $$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-2\alpha x}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **amplitude attenuation coefficient** (Np/cm), and the factor of **2** arises from the round trip in pulse-echo mode. In practice, attenuation is reported in **dB**: $$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot x$$ For soft tissue, the rule of thumb is: $$\text{Attenuation} \approx 1 \text{ dB/cm/MHz}$$ So a 5 MHz beam suffers **5 dB loss per cm** of travel. This is why TGC must increase gain by approximately 1 dB/cm/MHz to compensate. > [!NOTE] > **TGC Slope Setting:** In clinical A-Scan, the TGC slope is user-adjustable. For ophthalmic use, a slope of ~0.5 dB/cm/MHz is typical; for abdominal imaging, ~0.7 dB/cm/MHz; for cardiac, ~0.3 dB/cm/MHz. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — A-Scan System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pulser
High Voltage Pulse Generator] -->|Excitation Pulse| B[Transducer
Piezoelectric Crystal] B -->|Ultrasound Beam| C[Tissue Medium
With Acoustic Interfaces] C -->|Echoes| B B -->|Received RF Signal| D[TR Switch
Transmit Receive] D -->|Echo Signal| E[TGC Amplifier
Depth Dependent Gain] E -->|Compensated Signal| F[Bandpass Filter
Noise Reduction] F -->|Filtered RF| G[Envelope Detector
and Amplifier] G -->|Video Signal| H[Display Unit
CRT or LCD] I[Time Base Generator
Synchronized Sweep] -->|Horizontal Sweep| H H -->|Spike Display| J[Clinician
Interprets Echoes] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style I fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style J fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` **Reading the diagram (left to right, top-down):** - Pulser fires → Transducer emits ultrasound → Ultrasound enters tissue → Echoes return → T/R Switch routes echoes → TGC compensates → Filter cleans signal → Detector extracts envelope → Display draws spikes → Clinician reads. ### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Typical Ophthalmic A-Scan Display ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EyeCross[Human Eye Cross Section] direction LR C[Cornea] --> A[Aqueous Humor] --> L[Lens] --> V[Vitreous Humor] --> R[Retina] end EyeCross -.Reflected Echoes.-> Display subgraph Display[A-Scan Display] direction LR T0[T0 Cornea Spike
Tall] --> T1[T1 Anterior Lens
Medium] T1 --> T2[T2 Posterior Lens
Medium] T2 --> T3[T3 Retina Spike
Tall with TGC] end T0 -.t = 0 us.-> T1 T1 -.t approx 4.5 us.-> T2 T2 -.t approx 9 us.-> T3 T3 -.t approx 30.5 us.-> EndAxis[Total Axial Length approx 23.5 mm] style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style L fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 style V fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style R fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 style T0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style T1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style T2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style T3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style EndAxis fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Interpretation of A-Scan Echoes ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([A-Scan Echo Spike Analysis]) --> Q1{Spike Height?} Q1 -->|Very Tall| Tall[Strong Reflector
Large Impedance Mismatch
e.g. Bone Retina Interface] Q1 -->|Medium| Med[Moderate Reflector
e.g. Lens Surfaces] Q1 -->|Small| Small[Weak Reflector
Small Mismatch or Deep
Check TGC] Start --> Q2{Spike Position?} Q2 -->|Left of Display| Shallow[Superficial Structure
Near Transducer] Q2 -->|Right of Display| Deep[Deep Structure
Far from Transducer] Start --> Q3{Multiple Spikes?} Q3 -->|Yes at expected positions| Normal[Normal Anatomy
Measure Spacing] Q3 -->|Spike missing| Abnormal[Possible Pathology
e.g. Retinal Detachment] Q3 -->|Extra spike| Extra[Foreign Body or
Tumor or Cyst] style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style Tall fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 style Med fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 style Small fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style Shallow fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style Deep fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 style Normal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style Abnormal fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 style Extra fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Clinical Pearl (KTU Examiner loves this!):** In **retinal detachment**, the retina lifts off the choroid. On A-Scan, an extra echo spike appears between the vitreous and the sclera — this is a **diagnostic hallmark** of retinal detachment. Similarly, in **foreign body detection**, a high-amplitude spike appears at the depth of the metallic or glass fragment. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > **Q1. Define A-Scan. Mention any two applications of A-Scan in biomedical engineering.** **Model Answer:** A-Scan (Amplitude Mode Scan) is a one-dimensional ultrasonic diagnostic display technique in which **echoes returning from acoustic interfaces within biological tissues are displayed as vertical spikes** whose amplitude is proportional to the strength of the reflected echo, plotted against a horizontal time base representing depth of tissue penetration. The horizontal axis is calibrated using the average velocity of ultrasound in soft tissue (**c = 1540 m/s**) so that it directly reads depth in centimeters. **Two applications:** 1. **Ophthalmic biometry** — measurement of axial length of the eye for IOL power calculation prior to cataract surgery. 2. **Echoencephalography** (historical) — detection of midline shift in the brain; also useful for detecting intracranial hemorrhage in neonates. > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]** > **Q2. What is Time Gain Compensation (TGC)? Why is it necessary in A-Scan?** **Model Answer:** **TGC (Time Gain Compensation)** is a depth-dependent amplification technique in ultrasound receivers where the gain of the amplifier is progressively increased with time (and therefore with depth) to compensate for the attenuation of ultrasound as it propagates through tissue. **Necessity:** - Ultrasound intensity decreases exponentially with depth due to absorption, scattering, and reflection. - Without TGC, deep echoes would appear artificially small compared to shallow echoes of the same size. - TGC ensures that **echoes from reflectors of identical acoustic properties appear as equal-height spikes** on the display, regardless of their depth — making the image a true representation of tissue interfaces rather than depth-biased. - In ophthalmic A-Scan, TGC is critical to ensure that the **retinal spike** (deep) is as prominent as the **corneal spike** (shallow) for accurate biometric measurement. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) #### ⭐ INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer ANY ONE ⭐ --- ##### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]** **Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **A-Scan ultrasonic diagnostic system**. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An A-Scan examination of the eye shows the following spike positions on a calibrated time base (13 μs/cm): - Spike 1 (Cornea): 0 μs - Spike 2 (Anterior lens): 5.85 μs - Spike 3 (Posterior lens): 10.4 μs - Spike 4 (Retina): 32.5 μs Calculate: (i) Axial length of the eye in mm (ii) Anterior chamber depth (iii) Lens thickness (iv) Vitreous chamber depth Assume c = 1540 m/s. **(7 Marks)** --- **MODEL SOLUTION:** **(a) Block Diagram & Working Principle (7 Marks):** The A-Scan system operates on the **pulse-echo principle** and consists of the following blocks: 1. **Pulser:** Generates a short, high-voltage electrical pulse (100–400 V, ~1 μs duration) that excites the piezoelectric transducer. 2. **Transducer (Probe):** A piezoelectric crystal (e.g., PZT, lead zirconate titanate) that converts the electrical pulse into an ultrasound burst and, after a brief interval, converts returning echoes back into electrical signals. 3. **T/R Switch (Transmit-Receive Switch):** During transmission, it disconnects the sensitive receiver to prevent damage. After transmission, it switches to receive mode and routes the returning echoes to the receiver. It uses diodes and a damping network. 4. **Time Gain Compensator (TGC):** A depth-controlled amplifier that increases gain linearly or non-linearly with time to compensate for tissue attenuation. 5. **RF Amplifier & Bandpass Filter:** Amplifies the weak RF echo and filters out noise outside the transducer bandwidth. 6. **Envelope Detector:** Extracts the envelope of the high-frequency RF echo (using diode rectification and low-pass filtering) to produce a slowly varying video signal. 7. **Video Amplifier:** Further amplifies the video signal to a level suitable for display. 8. **Time Base Generator:** Generates a synchronized horizontal sweep voltage that moves the display beam from left to right, calibrated so 1 cm on the screen = 13 μs = 1 cm of tissue depth. 9. **Display (CRT or LCD):** Shows the final trace with vertical spikes (echo amplitudes) against the horizontal depth axis. **Working Principle:** The pulser excites the transducer → ultrasound pulse enters tissue → at each acoustic interface (different Z), a portion of the wave reflects back → returning echoes are detected by the same transducer → routed through T/R switch → amplified with TGC → envelope detected → displayed as spikes on the time base. The **time position** of each spike indicates **depth**, and the **height** indicates **echo strength**. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** Using $d_{cm} = 0.077 \times t_{\mu s}$ (derived from $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ with $c = 1540$ m/s): **(i) Axial Length of Eye** (cornea to retina): $$\Delta t_{eye} = t_4 - t_1 = 32.5 - 0 = 32.5 \,\mu s$$ $$d_{eye} = 0.077 \times 32.5 = 2.5025 \text{ cm} = \mathbf{25.03 \text{ mm}}$$ **(ii) Anterior Chamber Depth** (cornea to anterior lens): $$\Delta t_{AC} = t_2 - t_1 = 5.85 - 0 = 5.85 \,\mu s$$ $$d_{AC} = 0.077 \times 5.85 = 0.4504 \text{ cm} = \mathbf{4.50 \text{ mm}}$$ **(iii) Lens Thickness** (anterior lens to posterior lens): $$\Delta t_L = t_3 - t_2 = 10.4 - 5.85 = 4.55 \,\mu s$$ $$d_L = 0.077 \times 4.55 = 0.3504 \text{ cm} = \mathbf{3.50 \text{ mm}}$$ **(iv) Vitreous Chamber Depth** (posterior lens to retina): $$\Delta t_V = t_4 - t_3 = 32.5 - 10.4 = 22.1 \,\mu s$$ $$d_V = 0.077 \times 22.1 = 1.7017 \text{ cm} = \mathbf{17.02 \text{ mm}}$$ **Verification:** $4.50 + 3.50 + 17.02 \approx 25.02$ mm ≈ 25.03 mm ✓ (matches axial length, small rounding error) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = c \cdot t / 2$. The ultrasound travels DOWN and BACK, so the total path is 2d, not d. > 2. **Using wrong velocity:** Always use $c = 1540$ m/s for soft tissue; $c = 1641$ m/s for lens (if specifically asked). Mixing these gives wrong lens thickness. > 3. **Unit errors:** Converting μs correctly. $1 \mu s = 10^{-6}$ s. The factor $0.077$ cm/μs is derived assuming $c = 1540$ m/s and depth in cm — do not mix this with $d = 770 t$ (which gives meters when t is in seconds). > 4. **Subtracting the wrong reference spike:** Always use the FIRST spike as the time origin (transducer-cornea contact). All measurements are $\Delta t$ from this reference. --- ##### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]** **Q. (a)** Derive the **time-of-flight equation** used in A-Scan to determine the depth of a reflecting interface. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the significance of **acoustic impedance** and the **reflection coefficient** in ultrasonic A-Scan imaging. An ultrasound beam is normally incident on the interface between fat ($Z_1 = 1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl) and muscle ($Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl). Calculate the percentage of intensity reflected and transmitted. **(7 Marks)** --- **MODEL SOLUTION:** **(a) Derivation of Time-of-Flight Equation (7 Marks):** **Assumptions:** 1. The velocity of ultrasound in soft tissue is uniform and equal to the standard $c = 1540$ m/s. 2. The beam travels in a straight line perpendicular to the reflecting interface (normal incidence). 3. The interface is much larger than the ultrasound wavelength (specular reflection). 4. Attenuation effects are compensated by TGC and do not affect time-of-flight measurement. **Derivation:** Let the transducer be at position $x = 0$ and the reflecting interface be at depth $d$ below it. **Step 1:** The ultrasound pulse travels from the transducer to the interface — a one-way distance of $d$ meters. $$\text{Time for one-way travel} = t_1 = \frac{d}{c}$$ **Step 2:** The reflected echo travels back from the interface to the transducer — another one-way distance of $d$ meters. $$\text{Time for return travel} = t_2 = \frac{d}{c}$$ **Step 3:** Total round-trip time of flight: $$t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}$$ **Step 4:** Solving for depth $d$: $$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}}$$ **Step 5:** For soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s) and time in microseconds: $$d_{cm} = \frac{1540 \times t_{\mu s} \times 10^{-6}}{2} \times 10^2 = 0.077 \cdot t_{\mu s} \text{ cm}$$ **Step 6:** Calibration constant — time required for ultrasound to traverse 1 cm of soft tissue and return: $$t_{1cm} = \frac{2 \times 0.01}{1540} = 12.99 \approx 13 \,\mu s/\text{cm}$$ This is the **standard time-base calibration** for all clinical A-Scan instruments. **(b) Acoustic Impedance and Reflection Coefficient (7 Marks):** **Acoustic Impedance (Z):** Acoustic impedance is the product of tissue density ($\rho$) and the velocity of ultrasound ($c$) in that tissue: $$Z = \rho \cdot c \quad [\text{units: Rayl} = \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}]$$ **Significance in A-Scan:** - It is the **fundamental tissue property** that determines whether a useful echo will be produced at an interface. - The **difference in Z values** between two adjacent tissues governs the strength of the reflected echo. - A large Z mismatch → strong echo (tall spike). Examples: soft tissue–bone (Z mismatch ≈ 6.12 × 10⁶) and soft tissue–air (Z mismatch ≈ 1.63 × 10⁶) produce the strongest echoes in the body. - A small Z mismatch → weak echo. Examples: muscle–fat, lens–vitreous produce smaller spikes. - This is why **coupling gel** is used between the transducer and skin — to eliminate the air gap (Z mismatch would be enormous and almost all energy would be reflected, preventing imaging). **Reflection Coefficient (R):** For normal incidence of an ultrasound wave on a plane interface between two media: $$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$ R is the **fraction of incident intensity** that is reflected. It is dimensionless, ranging from 0 to 1. **Numerical Calculation:** Given: $Z_1 = 1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl (fat), $Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl (muscle). **Step 1:** Compute the impedance difference: $$Z_2 - Z_1 = (1.70 - 1.38) \times 10^6 = 0.32 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the impedance sum: $$Z_2 + Z_1 = (1.70 + 1.38) \times 10^6 = 3.08 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the pressure reflection coefficient: $$r_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{0.32}{3.08} = 0.1039$$ **Step 4:** Compute the intensity reflection coefficient: $$R = r_p^2 = (0.1039)^2 = 0.0108 = \mathbf{1.08\%}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the intensity transmission coefficient: $$T = 1 - R = 1 - 0.0108 = 0.9892 = \mathbf{98.92\%}$$ **Interpretation:** - Only about **1% of the ultrasound intensity** is reflected at the fat-muscle boundary — the A-Scan spike will be **relatively small** for this interface. - About **99% of the intensity** is transmitted into the muscle, allowing imaging of deeper structures. - This explains why muscle-fat boundaries are visible but not as prominent as, say, soft tissue-bone boundaries. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Squaring error:** Students often write $R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ (forgetting the square). Remember: the formula with the square gives the **intensity** reflection coefficient. Without the square, it gives the **pressure** amplitude reflection coefficient. > 2. **Sign of $Z_2 - Z_1$:** The reflection coefficient uses the **square** of the difference, so sign doesn't matter — but students should still write it correctly. > 3. **Forgetting to express in percentage:** The examiner expects "1.08%" not just "0.0108" — always state the final answer in a meaningful unit. > 4. **Confusing T and R:** $T = 1 - R$ only when there is no absorption at the interface. For a perfect interface, this holds. Always show this step explicitly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — MUST MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM** - [ ] **A = Amplitude** (one-dimensional, spikes vs. time) - [ ] **Standard velocity in soft tissue: c = 1540 m/s** (used universally for depth calibration) - [ ] **Master equation: $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$** (factor of 2 is for round trip) - [ ] **Time calibration: 1 cm of tissue depth = 13 μs of round-trip time** - [ ] **Acoustic impedance: $Z = \rho \cdot c$** (in Rayl) - [ ] **Reflection coefficient (intensity): $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$** - [ ] **Attenuation rule of thumb: ~1 dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue** → drives TGC slope - [ ] **Axial resolution: $\frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$** — shorter pulse = better resolution - [ ] **Higher frequency → better resolution, less penetration** (classic trade-off) - [ ] **TGC** compensates for depth-dependent attenuation — must understand "why" not just "what" - [ ] **Ophthalmic A-Scan frequency: 10 MHz** — gives ~0.077 mm axial resolution - [ ] **Eye axial length: 22–25 mm (normal adult)** — 4 characteristic spikes: Cornea, Ant. Lens, Post. Lens, Retina - [ ] **Retinal detachment diagnosis:** extra spike in vitreous region on A-Scan - [ ] **Coupling gel eliminates air-tissue interface** (air has Z = 0.0004 × 10⁶ — near-total reflection) - [ ] **Acoustic impedance values to memorize:** Air (0.0004), Fat (1.38), Water (1.48), Soft tissue (1.63), Muscle (1.70), Lens (1.87), Bone (7.75) — all in 10⁶ Rayl - [ ] **Clinical applications:** Eye biometry, echoencephalography, foreign body detection, retinal detachment - [ ] **T/R Switch** protects receiver during high-voltage transmission - [ ] **Piezoelectric effect** is the transducer's operating principle (PZT most common crystal) > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Final Tip:** When answering KTU questions, **always draw the block diagram** — it is worth 2-3 marks by itself in most 14-mark questions. Also, when stating formulas, **always mention the units** of each variable. This is the single most common differentiator between an 8-mark and a 12-mark answer in KTU board exams. ---

topics.title: B-Scan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT521 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** B-Scan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**B-Scan (Brightness Scan) Ultrasonography** is a two-dimensional (2-D) diagnostic ultrasound imaging modality in which the magnitude of returning acoustic echoes is displayed as proportional pixel intensity (brightness) on a raster-scanned cathode-ray tube (CRT) or flat-panel display. Each displayed vertical column in the image represents a single **A-line (A-scan trace)** captured at a different lateral position of the transducer. The horizontal axis corresponds to the lateral position of the transducer aperture, while the vertical axis encodes the axial depth derived from the round-trip time-of-flight of the pulsed acoustic wave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT521 – Module 4):** B-scan imaging is a cornerstone topic under *Diagnostic Ultrasound Imaging Systems*. The student is expected to clearly distinguish A-mode, B-mode, M-mode, and TM-mode displays, and explain the role of the scan converter, time-gain compensation (TGC), and grayscale mapping.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a completely dark room holding a flashlight. You sweep the beam from left to right across the wall. At every instant, the dust particles in the air reflect a tiny amount of light back to you. Now imagine a *magical* flashlight that can measure the **distance** of the reflecting dust from you (using the time the light took to return) and the **brightness of the reflection** (how much came back). You then *paint* the wall:
- **Horizontal position** of the paint stroke = where your flashlight was pointing (left/right).
- **Vertical position** of the dot on the wall = how far the dust was from you.
- **Brightness of the dot** = how strongly the dust reflected the light.

Sweep the flashlight left-to-right quickly, and you get a 2-D grayscale **map of dust density** in the dark room. **That map IS a B-scan image.** Replace the light with **ultrasound** (typically **5 – 10 MHz** in ophthalmology, **2 – 5 MHz** in abdominal/obstetric imaging), replace "dust" with **tissue interfaces (cornea, lens, retina, organ boundaries, tumors)**, and you have a clinical B-scan ultrasound.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Clinical Use:** B-scan is **indispensable in ophthalmology** for imaging the eye when the ocular media is opaque (e.g., cataract, vitreous hemorrhage, corneal opacification). It is also the foundation of obstetric, abdominal, vascular, and cardiac ultrasound.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $c \approx 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ (assumed constant by all clinical scanners).
- **Typical B-scan frequency (ocular):** **8 – 10 MHz**.
- **Typical B-scan frequency (abdominal/obstetric):** **2 – 5 MHz**.
- **Display dynamic range:** **40 – 60 dB** mapped to 256 grayscale levels (8 bits/pixel).
- **Frame rate (real-time B-scan):** **15 – 60 frames/sec** depending on depth and line density.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and the "How"

The B-mode display is built by concatenating a large number of individual A-mode (amplitude-mode) traces side by side, in a controlled geometric order. The complete operational logic unfolds in the following structured steps:

1. **Pulse Generation:** A high-voltage (≈ 100 – 200 V) narrow pulse (≈ 0.5 – 2 µs) is applied to the piezoelectric transducer by the pulser, exciting a short burst of ultrasound.
2. **Wave Propagation:** The acoustic wave propagates through tissue at $c \approx 1540 \, \text{m/s}$, undergoing partial reflection, scattering, and attenuation at every tissue interface.
3. **Echo Reception (T/R Switch):** The same transducer is switched to *receive* mode by a Transmit/Receive (T/R) switch. Returning echoes (microvolt-level signals) are amplified.
4. **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC):** Deeper echoes are weaker because of attenuation. TGC applies a **time-varying amplification** that progressively increases gain with depth, equalizing the apparent echo strength and producing a uniform image.
5. **Envelope Detection / Log Compression:** The amplified RF echo is rectified and log-compressed (typically 40 dB → 5-bit or 8-bit grayscale code) so that the wide dynamic range of acoustic echoes fits the limited dynamic range of the display.
6. **Scan Conversion:** Each A-line (a 1-D vector of grayscale samples vs. depth) is written into a digital **scan-converter memory** at a memory address corresponding to its transducer lateral position. The scan converter then *reads out* the memory in standard **TV raster format** (e.g., 525/625-line interlaced, or progressive) for display.
7. **Mechanical/Electronic Sweeping:** The transducer is swept (mechanically with a motor, or electronically with a phased-array or linear-array) so that adjacent A-lines correspond to adjacent lateral positions.
8. **Display:** A 2-D grayscale picture is shown, where **brightness ∝ echo amplitude** and **position ∝ (lateral, depth) coordinates**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it called "Brightness" mode?** Because the only piece of echo information **modulating** the display is the *brightness* (luminance) of each pixel — unlike A-mode (where echo amplitude deflects a trace vertically) or M-mode (where amplitude modulates brightness but time is the horizontal axis).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Value / Note |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | Depth of reflector | $d = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$ | m | $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ in soft tissue; division by 2 due to round-trip |
| 2 | Round-trip time for depth $d$ | $t = \dfrac{2 d}{c}$ | s | Used to set PRF maximum |
| 3 | Maximum Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) | $\text{PRF}_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 d_{\max}}$ | Hz | Avoid range ambiguity |
| 4 | Axial (range) resolution | $\delta_{ax} = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | m | Depends on transducer *bandwidth* $\Delta f$, NOT on $f_0$ alone |
| 5 | Pulse spatial length | $L_{pulse} = n \, \lambda = \dfrac{n \, c}{f_0}$ | m | $n$ = number of cycles in pulse (typically 2 – 4) |
| 6 | Axial resolution (cycle-based) | $\delta_{ax} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_0}$ | m | Used for narrowband approximation |
| 7 | Lateral resolution | $\delta_{lat} \approx \lambda \cdot \dfrac{F}{D} = 1.22 \, \lambda \cdot \dfrac{F}{D}$ | m | $F$ = focal distance, $D$ = aperture diameter |
| 8 | Frame rate | $\text{FR} = \dfrac{\text{PRF}}{N_{\text{lines}}}$ | fps | $N_{\text{lines}}$ = lines per frame |
| 9 | Total time per frame | $T_f = N_{\text{lines}} \cdot \dfrac{2 d_{\max}}{c}$ | s | Reciprocal of FR |
| 10 | Attenuation in tissue | $A = \alpha \, f \, d$ | dB | $\alpha \approx 0.5 \text{ – } 1.0 \, \text{dB/cm/MHz}$ |
| 11 | Reflection coefficient (normal incidence) | $R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | – | $Z$ = acoustic impedance $\rho c$ |
| 12 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho \, c$ | Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) | Soft tissue ≈ 1.63 × 10⁶ Rayl |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rendering Notice:** In the formulas above, the absolute-value / division notation uses `\dfrac{}{}` or `\vert` rather than literal `|` characters to prevent breakage of the markdown table syntax. Always write `c \, t \, / \, 2` style fractions in KTU exam scripts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

B-scan engineering directly enables:
- **Ophthalmic biometry** (axial length measurement, intraocular lens power calculation).
- **Detection of retinal detachment, vitreous hemorrhage, intraocular tumors, and orbital masses.**
- **Obstetric imaging** (fetal biometry, anomaly screening).
- **Image-guided interventions** (biopsy needle tracking in real time).
- **Elastography & Doppler overlays** as add-on modalities on the same B-mode platform.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Axial (Range) Position of a Reflector

The transducer emits an ultrasound pulse at time $t = 0$. The pulse travels into tissue at speed $c$ and strikes a reflector at depth $d$. The echo returns to the transducer at time $t_1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance traveled by pulse (down and up)} &= c \cdot t_1 \\
\text{Distance from transducer to reflector (one way)} &= \frac{c \cdot t_1}{2} \\
\therefore d &= \frac{c \cdot t_1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of conversion:** Because the pulse must traverse the depth $d$ **twice** (forward to the reflector, then back to the receiver), we divide the total path length $c t_1$ by 2 to recover the one-way depth.

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)

A new transmit pulse cannot be fired until the echo from the *deepest* expected reflector (at $d_{\max}$) has returned. Otherwise a second-pulse echo would be misinterpreted as a first-pulse echo from a *shallower* depth — the **range-ambiguity artifact**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Round-trip time to depth } d_{\max} &: \quad t_{\max} = \frac{2 \, d_{\max}}{c} \\
\text{To avoid ambiguity, period between pulses } T &\geq t_{\max} \\
T_{\min} &= \frac{2 \, d_{\max}}{c} \\
\therefore \text{PRF}_{\max} &= \frac{1}{T_{\min}} = \frac{c}{2 \, d_{\max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** PRF is bounded by the longest possible round-trip travel time. Higher PRFs (used in shallow imaging) are safe; lower PRFs (deep imaging) are mandated.

### 3.3 Derivation of Frame Rate for a Sector B-Scan

A sector B-scan forms one complete image (frame) by acquiring $N_{\text{lines}}$ A-lines, each line requiring a round-trip to depth $d_{\max}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time per A-line} &= \frac{2 \, d_{\max}}{c} \\
\text{Time per frame} \quad T_f &= N_{\text{lines}} \times \frac{2 \, d_{\max}}{c} \\
\text{Frame Rate} \quad \text{FR} &= \frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{c}{2 \, d_{\max} \, N_{\text{lines}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Evaluation (worked example):** Let $d_{\max} = 15 \, \text{cm} = 0.15 \, \text{m}$, $N_{\text{lines}} = 120$, $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 120 \times \frac{2 \times 0.15}{1540} \\
&= 120 \times \frac{0.30}{1540} \\
&= 120 \times 1.948 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s} \\
&= 2.338 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{s} = 23.38 \, \text{ms} \\
\text{FR} &= \frac{1}{0.02338} \approx 42.8 \, \text{fps}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with **real-time B-mode** (≥ 30 fps).

### 3.4 Derivation of Axial Resolution

Axial resolution is the **minimum axial separation** at which two reflectors lying along the beam axis can be distinguished as separate echoes. It is set by the **spatial pulse length (SPL)** — the physical length of the acoustic pulse in tissue.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPL} &= n \cdot \lambda = \frac{n \cdot c}{f_0} \\
\text{To resolve two echoes, the pulse must fully return} & \text{ from reflector 1 before reaching reflector 2.}\\
\therefore \text{Axial resolution} \quad \delta_{ax} &= \frac{\text{SPL}}{2} = \frac{n \cdot c}{2 \, f_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a *broadband* short pulse (typical in modern scanners, $n \approx 1\text{ – }2$ cycles, large $\Delta f$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{ax} = \frac{c}{2 \, \Delta f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:** For $f_0 = 10 \, \text{MHz}$, $n = 2$, $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$:

$$
\delta_{ax} = \frac{2 \times 1540}{2 \times 10 \times 10^6} = 1.54 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.154 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Logic:** Higher frequency → shorter wavelength → shorter pulse → finer axial resolution. **This is why ocular B-scan uses 10 MHz** (sub-millimetre detail in a 24-mm-long eye) and abdominal scans use 3 – 5 MHz (deeper penetration needed, resolution sacrificed).

### 3.5 Derivation of Lateral Resolution

Lateral resolution depends on the **beam width at the focal zone**. For a circular focused aperture of diameter $D$ at focal distance $F$:

$$
\delta_{lat} \approx 1.22 \, \lambda \cdot \frac{F}{D}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:** For $F = 5 \, \text{cm}$, $D = 1.2 \, \text{cm}$, $f_0 = 5 \, \text{MHz}$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.308 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\delta_{lat} = 1.22 \times 0.308 \times \frac{50}{12} = 1.565 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Logic:** A *wider* aperture (larger $D$) or a *shorter* wavelength (higher $f$) gives a tighter focus and better lateral resolution.

### 3.6 Time-Gain Compensation (TGC) — Mathematical Justification

Tissue attenuation at frequency $f$ over depth $d$ is $A(d) = \alpha f d$ in dB. To compensate, the receiver must apply gain:

$$
G(d) = \alpha \, f \, d \quad \text{(in dB, linearly ramped with time)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Since } d &= \frac{c \, t}{2}, \\
G(t) &= \frac{\alpha \, f \, c}{2} \, t = k \, t, \quad k = \frac{\alpha f c}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **linear-in-time** gain ramp restores echoes to a uniform display intensity. (TGC is a **controllable** slope, set by the sonographer via "near gain" and "far gain" knobs.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a B-Scan Ultrasound Imaging System

The following **Mermaid block diagram** captures the full signal path from pulse generation to raster display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clk[Master Clock and Timing Generator] --> pulser[Pulser / High Voltage Transmitter]
    clk --> scanConv[Scan Converter and Display Controller]
    pulser --> trSwitch[T/R Switch]
    trSwitch --> transd[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    transd -->|Echoes Return| trSwitch
    trSwitch --> rxPre[Low Noise Preamplifier]
    rxPre --> tgcVar[TGC Variable Gain Amplifier]
    tgcVar --> demod[Envelope Detector and Log Compressor]
    demod --> a2d[A/D Converter]
    a2d --> scanConv
    scanConv --> dsp[Digital Signal Processing and Grayscale LUT]
    dsp --> mon[Grayscale Display Monitor]
    scanConv --> mon
    trSwitch --> transd
    trSwitch --> trSwitch
    mech[Mechanical Sector Scanner Motor or Phased Array Beamformer] --> transd
```

**Textual walk-through of the architecture:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Master Clock | Provides the system-wide timing reference; synchronizes transmit, receive, scan conversion, and display raster. |
| Pulser | Generates a brief high-voltage RF burst that excites the piezoelectric crystal. |
| T/R Switch | Routes the transmit pulse to the transducer and routes returning microvolt-level echoes to the receiver; protects the sensitive receiver from the high-voltage transmit pulse. |
| Preamplifier | First-stage low-noise amplification of the echo signal. |
| TGC | Time-varying gain amplifier that compensates for depth-dependent attenuation. |
| Envelope Detector & Log Compressor | Rectifies the RF echo, extracts its envelope, and compresses ~100 dB dynamic range into ~40 dB display range. |
| A/D Converter | Digitizes the compressed envelope samples. |
| Scan Converter | Stores the digital echo samples in a memory matrix at addresses corresponding to (lateral, axial) coordinates; reads them out in TV raster order. |
| DSP & LUT | Applies grayscale look-up tables, persistence filtering, edge enhancement, and post-processing. |
| Display | Raster-scanned grayscale monitor showing the 2-D B-mode image. |
| Mechanical / Phased-Array Scanner | Provides the lateral sweeping of the acoustic beam across the tissue. |

### 4.2 Comparison of A-mode, B-mode, and M-mode Displays

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A_Mode["A-Mode Amplitude Display 1D"]
        A1[Time of Flight] --> A2[Echo Amplitude as Vertical Deflection]
        A2 --> A3[Single Stationary Trace]
    end
    subgraph B_Mode["B-Mode Brightness Display 2D"]
        B1[Sweep Transducer Laterally] --> B2[Acquire Many A-Lines]
        B2 --> B3[Map Echo Amplitude to Grayscale Intensity]
        B3 --> B4[2D Image: Brightness vs Lateral x Depth]
    end
    subgraph M_Mode["M-Mode Motion Display"]
        M1[Hold Transducer Stationary] --> M2[Plot Echo Brightness vs Time]
        M2 --> M3[Used for Moving Structures e.g Heart Valves]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Note (Fallback Used):** A *physical* cross-sectional eye drawing (cornea, lens, vitreous, retina, sclera, optic nerve) is the standard textbook figure for ocular B-scan. Because pure Mermaid cannot render such an anatomical diagram faithfully, the **block-level functional architecture** above is provided. In the KTU answer script, a student should **redraw the labeled anatomical eye** (cornea → anterior chamber → lens → vitreous → retina → choroid → sclera → optic nerve) and overlay the ultrasound beam path with **echo spikes at each interface**.

### 4.3 Real-Time B-Scan Image Formation — Pixel Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    beam[Focused Ultrasound Beam Sweeps Tissue] --> reflect[Echoes Return from Multiple Interfaces]
    reflect --> aLine[Each Position Produces One A-Line Vector of Echo Amplitudes]
    aLine --> pixMap[Each A-Line Sample = One Pixel Column Brightness]
    pixMap --> image[Full Frame = 2D Grayscale Image]
    image --> orient[X-axis = Lateral Position Y-axis = Depth Brightness = Echo Strength]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**

> **Q1.** What is B-scan imaging? Why is it called "brightness" mode? Mention any two clinical applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
B-scan (Brightness-scan) is a 2-D ultrasound imaging technique in which the amplitude of returning echoes is displayed as the **brightness (luminance)** of pixels on a raster display, with one axis representing lateral transducer position and the other representing depth of the reflector. It is called *brightness* mode because **echo amplitude modulates the brightness** of the displayed spot, in contrast to A-mode where amplitude deflects a vertical trace. Two clinical applications: **(i) ophthalmic imaging** of the eye in opaque media (e.g., vitreous hemorrhage, retinal detachment) and **(ii) obstetric imaging** for fetal biometry and anomaly screening. (3 marks)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

> **Q2.** What is the role of the **scan converter** in a B-mode ultrasound system? Why is it required?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A scan converter is a digital memory device that **stores echo data in acquisition coordinates** (lateral position vs. depth) but **reads them out in TV raster format** (line-by-line, top-to-bottom) suitable for the display monitor. It is required because the ultrasound beam acquires data sequentially in polar or sector coordinates (one A-line at a time, in arbitrary angular order) while the display is a **fixed Cartesian raster** that must be refreshed at 25 – 60 Hz; the scan converter bridges these two timing/geometry domains. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions of 14 marks are split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, OR a single 14-mark sub-divided question. Both alternatives A and B are given below; the student attempts ONE.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Block Diagram + Working + Calculations*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Q3, 14 marks]**

> **Q3 (a).** Draw the **block diagram of a B-scan ultrasound imaging system** and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Block | Function (1-line each) |
|-------|------------------------|
| Master Clock / Timing Generator | Provides synchronized timing pulses for transmit, receive, scan conversion, and display raster. |
| Pulser / Transmitter | Generates a high-voltage short-duration RF pulse (≈ 100 – 200 V, ≈ 0.5 – 2 µs) that excites the piezoelectric transducer. |
| T/R (Transmit–Receive) Switch | Routes the high-voltage transmit pulse to the transducer and protects the sensitive low-noise receiver; routes returning microvolt-level echoes to the receiver. |
| Piezoelectric Transducer | Converts electrical pulse → acoustic wave on transmit, and acoustic echoes → electrical signals on receive. |
| Preamplifier | First-stage low-noise amplification of weak echo signals. |
| TGC (Time-Gain Compensation) | Applies depth-dependent gain ramp to compensate for tissue attenuation. |
| Envelope Detector & Log Compressor | Rectifies the RF, extracts the envelope, and compresses ~100 dB dynamic range to ~40 dB for display. |
| A/D Converter | Digitizes the compressed envelope samples at a rate of ≥ 2 × bandwidth. |
| Scan Converter | Stores digital samples in a 2-D memory matrix and reads them out in TV raster order. |
| Display Monitor | Displays the 2-D grayscale B-mode image in real time. |
| Mechanical / Phased-Array Scanner | Provides lateral sweeping of the ultrasound beam across the tissue region of interest. |

(7 marks — full marks awarded for a labelled diagram with one-line functions of each block.)

---

> **Q3 (b).** In an ocular B-scan, the maximum imaging depth is **$d_{\max} = 30 \, \text{mm}$** and each frame consists of **$N = 120$** A-lines. The speed of sound in ocular tissue is **$c = 1500 \, \text{m/s}$**. Calculate:
> (i) the maximum permissible pulse repetition frequency,
> (ii) the frame rate, and
> (iii) the round-trip time for a reflector at the maximum depth. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum PRF** — to avoid range ambiguity, the pulse period must be ≥ round-trip time to $d_{\max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\max} &= \frac{2 \, d_{\max}}{c} = \frac{2 \times 30 \times 10^{-3}}{1500} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{s} = 40 \, \mu\text{s} \\
\text{PRF}_{\max} &= \frac{1}{t_{\max}} = \frac{1}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 25\,000 \, \text{Hz} = 25 \, \text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Frame Rate** — each of the 120 A-lines occupies one PRF period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FR} &= \frac{\text{PRF}_{\max}}{N} = \frac{25\,000}{120} = 208.3 \, \text{fps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Round-trip time for $d_{\max} = 30 \, \text{mm}$:**

$$
t_{\max} = \frac{2 \times 30 \times 10^{-3}}{1500} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{s} = 40 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

**(1 mark)** — *and the student should note that 40 µs is exactly the time bound that defined PRF in (i).*

**(Conclusion: 2 marks)** Real-time B-scan is comfortably achieved (> 30 fps), confirming the system can display smooth motion of ocular structures.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Principle + Resolution + TGC*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Q4, 14 marks]**

> **Q4 (a).** Explain the **principle of B-scan image formation** with reference to A-line composition, scan conversion, and grayscale mapping. Compare A-mode, B-mode, and M-mode displays in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Principle of B-scan image formation (4 marks):**
1. The transducer emits a short ultrasound pulse and receives the echoes from tissue interfaces along one line — this is the **A-line** (amplitude vs. depth trace).
2. The transducer is **swept laterally** (mechanically or electronically) to acquire many A-lines at different lateral positions; each A-line is stored as a vertical column in the scan-converter memory.
3. The scan converter reads out the 2-D memory matrix in standard TV raster format at 25 – 60 Hz.
4. Each echo amplitude is **log-compressed and mapped to a grayscale level (0 – 255)**, so that the brightness of a pixel on the screen is proportional to the strength of the corresponding echo.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | A-Mode | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|
| Dimension | 1-D | 2-D | 1-D (time-motion) |
| Echo display | Vertical deflection (amplitude vs. time) | Grayscale brightness (intensity) | Brightness of dots over time |
| Axes | Time (≡ depth) vs. Amplitude | Lateral vs. Depth | Time vs. Depth |
| Information | Echo amplitude | Spatial map of tissue | Motion of structures |
| Typical use | Eye-biometry, echoencephalography | Ophthalmology, obstetrics, abdomen | Cardiac valve motion |
| Transducer | Stationary | Sweeping | Stationary |

---

> **Q4 (b).** Explain **axial and lateral resolution** in B-mode ultrasound. For a **10 MHz** transducer with **$n = 2$** cycles per pulse in tissue ($c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$), calculate the axial resolution. What is the role of TGC? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Axial resolution (3 marks):** The minimum depth separation at which two reflectors lying along the beam axis can be distinguished. It equals half the spatial pulse length (SPL). Higher frequency and shorter pulses give better axial resolution.

$$
\delta_{ax} = \frac{n \, c}{2 f_0}
$$

**Lateral resolution (1 mark):** The minimum lateral separation of two reflectors perpendicular to the beam axis that can be distinguished, set by the beam width at the focal zone. A larger aperture and shorter wavelength improve it.

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{ax} &= \frac{n \, c}{2 f_0} = \frac{2 \times 1540}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{3080}{2 \times 10^{7}} \\
&= 1.54 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.154 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of TGC (1 mark):** Time-Gain Compensation applies a time-varying amplification that increases with depth to compensate for the depth-dependent attenuation of ultrasound in tissue, equalising the apparent echo brightness and producing a uniform image.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (loses 1 – 2 marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = ct/2$ (echo travels round-trip). This is the most common error — KTU examiners award 0 for the entire depth calculation if 2 is dropped.
> 2. **Confusing axial resolution with lateral resolution** — axial depends on SPL / frequency; lateral depends on beam width / aperture / focal length.
> 3. **Writing $c = 1500 \, \text{m/s}$ in ocular tissue** — the standard textbook value is $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ in soft tissue; the eye is sometimes approximated at 1500 m/s. Either is acceptable **only if you state the assumption clearly**. Mixing both in one derivation will be penalised.
> 4. **Omitting the units in the final answer** — always write **mm** for depth and **µs / ms** for time.
> 5. **Drawing a B-scan block diagram without TGC, scan converter, or T/R switch** — these three blocks are *mandatory* in any KTU answer; a generic "transmitter → receiver → display" diagram loses 2 – 3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **B-scan = 2-D grayscale ultrasound image**; brightness of each pixel = echo amplitude.
- ✅ **Axes:** X = lateral transducer position; Y = depth; pixel intensity = echo strength.
- ✅ **Core depth equation:** $d = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$, with $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ in soft tissue (1500 m/s acceptable in eye).
- ✅ **PRF constraint:** $\text{PRF}_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 d_{\max}}$ — prevents range-ambiguity artifacts.
- ✅ **Frame rate:** $\text{FR} = \dfrac{c}{2 d_{\max} N_{\text{lines}}}$ — typically 15 – 60 fps for real-time imaging.
- ✅ **Axial resolution:** $\delta_{ax} = \dfrac{n \, c}{2 f_0} = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ — set by pulse length, improved by higher frequency.
- ✅ **Lateral resolution:** $\delta_{lat} \approx 1.22 \, \lambda \cdot F/D$ — set by beam width, improved by wider aperture and higher frequency.
- ✅ **Scan converter** is essential to map polar/sector acquisition coordinates to Cartesian TV raster display.
- ✅ **TGC** = linear-in-time gain ramp compensating attenuation $A = \alpha f d$; controlled by near/far gain knobs.
- ✅ **Block diagram must include:** Master Clock → Pulser → T/R Switch → Transducer → Preamplifier → TGC → Envelope Detector/Log Compressor → A/D → Scan Converter → DSP/LUT → Display, with mechanical/phased-array sweeping feedback.
- ✅ **Comparison with A-mode and M-mode** is a *guaranteed* KTU question — memorise the comparison table.
- ✅ **Ocular B-scan frequencies (8 – 10 MHz)** vs **abdominal (2 – 5 MHz)** — higher frequency = better resolution but shallower penetration.
- ✅ **Reflection coefficient** $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ — large impedance mismatches (lens/vitreous, tissue/bone, tissue/air) produce strong echoes that define tissue boundaries on the B-mode image.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
