topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT521 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** M-Scan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# M-Scan (Motion-Mode Ultrasound) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *M-Scan (Motion-Mode Ultrasonography)* is a diagnostic ultrasound display modality in which the reflected echoes from a stationary, single ultrasonic beam (aligned along a chosen axis of interest) are plotted as a function of time, thereby producing a continuous, one-dimensional graphical record of the motion of moving anatomical interfaces (such as cardiac walls, heart valves, and pulsatile vessels) along the beam's axis.

In M-mode, the **horizontal axis represents time**, while the **vertical axis represents the depth (distance) of the reflecting structure from the transducer face**. Brightness of the dot indicates echo amplitude. Unlike B-scan which builds a 2-D static image, M-mode sacrifices spatial cross-sectional detail to achieve an extremely **high temporal resolution** (often 1000+ frames/s internally, though display is usually limited to a few hundred sweeps/s).

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Edge-On Movie of a Flickering Candle"**
> Imagine you are standing in a dark room holding a single laser pointer (the ultrasound beam) aimed horizontally at a candle flame. The flame flickers and sways. If you only record the *single line* where the laser passes through the flame using a long-exposure film strip, you will capture a record of *how the flame moved along that line over time*. The strip of film represents time, and the position along the line represents distance. This is exactly what M-mode does to the heart: it watches one chosen "line" through the moving cardiac structures and lays out the motion chronologically.

- **Transmitted frequency used clinically:** typically **2 MHz to 10 MHz**.
- **Standard speed of ultrasound in soft tissue:** **c = 1540 m/s** (an internationally accepted biological average).
- **Typical M-mode sweep speed (chart paper):** **25 mm/s, 50 mm/s, or 100 mm/s**.
- **Time-axis precision:** A single sweep spans 2 to 10 seconds depending on display settings.

> [!TIP]
> **Why M-mode matters in KTU examinations:** Questions on M-mode usually test the link between the *time-amplitude display*, the *depth calculation using c* and the *pulse round-trip time*, and the *clinical application to cardiac motion* (mitral valve, aortic root, left ventricular wall thickness).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — How M-Mode Builds a Trace

The M-mode acquisition process follows a strict, repeated cycle:

1. **Pulse Generation:** The piezoelectric transducer emits a short, high-frequency ultrasound pulse (typically 2–3 cycles long) along a single fixed scan line.
2. **Echo Reception:** The same transducer (acting in pulse-echo mode) listens for returning echoes from each tissue interface along the line.
3. **Time-Gated Depth Mapping:** Each returning echo is placed on the display's **vertical axis** at a depth proportional to its round-trip time:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where `d` is depth, `c` is the speed of sound in tissue, and `t` is the round-trip time. The factor of **½** appears because the pulse travels to the target *and back* in time `t`.

4. **Modulation of Brightness:** The amplitude of the echo modulates the brightness of the corresponding point on the display — this is why it is fundamentally a *brightness-modulated* display.
5. **Slow Horizontal Sweep:** A new pulse is fired at a high **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)**. The display is swept horizontally at a much slower rate (chart-paper speed), so successive echoes lay down a continuous track showing how each interface moves over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of M-mode's existence:** Cardiac structures move at speeds up to ~100 mm/s during systole. B-mode (with typical frame rates of 30–60 fps) smears or aliases this motion. M-mode can fire pulses at PRF = 1000–5000 Hz, capturing *every millisecond* of motion without aliasing — making it the gold standard for measuring *rates of change* like mitral valve E-F slope, wall thickening, and chamber dimensions over the cardiac cycle.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Governing Equation | Symbols & Units | Typical Value / Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Depth of reflecting interface | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | d [m], c [m/s], t [s] | 0.05 – 0.25 m in cardiac work | Converts echo time-of-flight to anatomical distance |
| 2 | Round-trip time | $t = \dfrac{2d}{c}$ | t [s], d [m], c [m/s] | 65 – 325 µs typical | Determines max unambiguous depth |
| 3 | Maximum unambiguous range (depth) | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$ | $d_{max}$ [m] | 0.077 – 0.385 m for PRF 2–10 kHz | Sets operating PRF |
| 4 | Pulse Repetition Frequency | $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot d_{max}}$ | PRF [Hz] | 1 – 10 kHz cardiac | Higher PRF → higher temporal resolution |
| 5 | Axial (range) resolution | $\Delta d = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | $\Delta d$ [m], $\tau$ [s] (pulse duration) | 0.5 – 2 mm | Minimum resolvable structure separation |
| 6 | Lateral resolution (at depth d) | $\Delta \ell = \lambda \cdot \dfrac{d}{D}$ | $\lambda$ [m], D [m] (aperture) | 2 – 5 mm | Sideways resolution along beam line |
| 7 | Sampling rate needed (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ [Hz], $f_{max}$ [Hz] | $\geq$ 20 Hz for 10 Hz heart motion | Prevents aliasing of motion |
| 8 | Velocity of sound in tissue | $c = 1540$ m/s | c [m/s] | Standardized value | Used in all depth calculations |
| 9 | Heart rate from M-mode | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{\text{R–R interval in s}}$ | HR [bpm] | 60 – 100 bpm adult | Direct clinical readout |
| 10 | M-mode chart speed scaling | $\text{time/screen} = \dfrac{\text{screen width}}{\text{chart speed}}$ | time [s], chart speed [mm/s] | 25, 50, 100 mm/s | Determines temporal resolution on paper |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** M-mode is the foundational technology behind modern **Tissue Doppler Imaging (TDI)**, **M-mode color flow**, and **Speckle-Tracking Echocardiography**. The same depth-time-amplitude relationship underlies elastography and intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) pullback recordings.

## 2.3 Clinical / Engineering Significance

- **Cardiology:** Assessment of mitral valve motion (E-F slope in mitral stenosis), LV wall thickness, fractional shortening, pericardial effusion.
- **Obstetrics:** Fetal heart rate (M-mode through fetal heart) — historically the most accurate method before Doppler.
- **Ophthalmology:** Measurement of eye-wall thickness and vitreous membrane mobility.
- **Vascular:** Plaque motion characterization in carotid and peripheral arteries.
- **Research:** High-temporal-resolution studies of rapidly oscillating biological interfaces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth from Round-Trip Time

**Given:** An ultrasound pulse is transmitted, reflects from an interface, and returns to the transducer.

**Step 1 — Define the round-trip time.**
Let `t` be the time elapsed between transmission and reception of the echo. This time is the *total* time for the wave to travel *out* and *back*.

**Step 2 — Express total path length.**
The pulse travels a total distance `L_total` at the speed of sound `c`:

$$L_{total} = c \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Relate total path to target depth.**
Since the pulse travels to the target and returns, the one-way distance to the reflecting interface (the depth `d`) is half the total path:

$$d = \frac{L_{total}}{2} = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Final Result:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination tip:** The factor **½** is the single most-frequently forgotten element in KTU derivations. Always justify it with the words *"pulse travels to the interface and back in time t"*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Unambiguous Depth

**Given:** Successive pulses cannot be transmitted until the previous pulse's echoes have returned, otherwise range ambiguity occurs.

**Step 1 — Maximum allowable time between pulses.**
The interval between two transmitted pulses is `T = 1 / \text{PRF}`. The pulse-echo must complete its round trip in *less than or equal to* `T`:

$$t_{max} = T = \frac{1}{\text{PRF}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into depth equation.**
Using the round-trip equation solved for `t`:

$$t_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot d_{max}}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve.**
Set the two expressions for `t_max` equal:

$$\frac{2 \cdot d_{max}}{c} = \frac{1}{\text{PRF}}$$

Solving for `d_max`:

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nyquist Sampling Criterion for M-Mode Motion

**Given:** An anatomical structure oscillates with maximum frequency component `f_max` (Hz). To reconstruct its motion faithfully, the M-mode sampling rate (PRF) must obey the Nyquist theorem.

**Step 1 — Statement of Nyquist criterion.**
A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency `f_max` must be sampled at a rate at least twice that frequency:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Identify sampling rate in M-mode context.**
In M-mode, the effective sampling rate of the moving structure is the **PRF** (number of pulses per second directed at the scan line):

$$\text{PRF} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Combine with depth constraint.**
Substituting `PRF = c / (2 d_{max})`:

$$\frac{c}{2 \cdot d_{max}} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

Rearranging for `d_max`:

$$d_{max} \leq \frac{c}{4 \cdot f_{max}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Deeper imaging **reduces** the allowable PRF, which **reduces** the maximum motion frequency that can be sampled. This is why very deep structures (e.g., adult trans-thoracic 30 cm) alias faster motion than shallow structures (e.g., pediatric 8 cm).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A 3 MHz cardiac M-mode transducer (c = 1540 m/s) detects a mitral valve echo returning 130 µs after pulse transmission. The PRF is 2 kHz. Determine:
(a) the depth of the mitral valve,
(b) whether this depth is within the maximum unambiguous range, and
(c) the axial resolution if the pulse duration is 1.5 µs.

### Part (a) — Depth Calculation

Given: `t = 130 µs = 130 × 10^{-6} s`, `c = 1540 m/s`.

Substitute into the depth equation:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2002 \text{ m}$$

Divide by 2:

$$d = \frac{0.2002}{2} = 0.1001 \text{ m} \approx 0.10 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ cm}$$

**Answer (a):** The mitral valve echo originates at a depth of approximately **10 cm**.

### Part (b) — Maximum Unambiguous Range

Given: `PRF = 2 kHz = 2000 Hz`, `c = 1540 m/s`.

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 2000} = \frac{1540}{4000}$$

$$d_{max} = 0.385 \text{ m} = 38.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Comparison:** 10 cm < 38.5 cm.

**Answer (b):** Yes, the mitral valve at 10 cm is well within the maximum unambiguous range. No range ambiguity will occur.

### Part (c) — Axial Resolution

Given: `τ = 1.5 µs = 1.5 × 10^{-6} s`, `c = 1540 m/s`.

$$\Delta d = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$\Delta d = \frac{2310 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 1155 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 1.16 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer (c):** The axial resolution is approximately **1.16 mm** — small enough to distinguish mitral valve leaflet structures from adjacent tissue.

## 3.5 Heart Rate from M-Mode — Step-by-Step

**Step 1.** Identify two consecutive R-wave peaks (or two consecutive identical valve events) on the M-mode trace.

**Step 2.** Measure the horizontal distance `Δx` between them on the display.

**Step 3.** Convert this distance to time using the chart speed `v` (mm/s):

$$\Delta t = \frac{\Delta x}{v}$$

**Step 4.** Compute heart rate:

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{\Delta t} \text{ [beats per minute]}$$

**Numerical:** If Δx = 25 mm and v = 25 mm/s, then Δt = 1 s, and HR = **60 bpm**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 M-Mode Acquisition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Clock Generator 1 to 10 kHz PRF"]
    nodeB["Pulser HV Transmitter"]
    nodeC["Piezoelectric Transducer Single Element"]
    nodeD["Tissue Volume Scan Line with Moving Interfaces"]
    nodeE["TGC Amplifier Time Gain Compensation"]
    nodeF["Envelope Detector and Log Compressor"]
    nodeG["Display Memory Depth vs Time Matrix"]
    nodeH["Slow Sweep Horizontal Display 25 to 100 mm per s"]
    nodeI["M Mode Trace on Monitor or Chart Paper"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

## 4.2 M-Mode Display Coordinate System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["VERTICAL AXIS Depth in cm 0 to 25"]
    nodeB["HORIZONTAL AXIS Time in seconds 0 to 5 s typical"]
    nodeC["Brightness Echo Amplitude in dB"]
    nodeD["Cardiac Wall Trace Wavy Curve"]
    nodeE["Mitral Valve Trace M Shape"]
    nodeF["Pericardial Effusion Echo Free Space"]
    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeA --- nodeE
    nodeB --- nodeE
    nodeC --- nodeE
    nodeA --- nodeF
    nodeB --- nodeF
    nodeC --- nodeF
```

## 4.3 M-Mode vs B-Mode Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Single Scan Line M Mode 1D Over Time"]
    nodeB["Multiple Scan Lines B Mode 2D Cross Section"]
    nodeC["High Temporal Resolution 1000 fps effective"]
    nodeD["Lower Temporal Resolution 30 to 60 fps"]
    nodeE["Excellent for Motion Analysis"]
    nodeF["Excellent for Anatomical Mapping"]
    nodeG["Used in Cardiology Valves Wall Motion"]
    nodeH["Used in Radiology Abdomen OB General"]
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeH
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow from Pulse to M-Mode Pixel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Step 1 Transmit 2 cycle 3 MHz pulse"]
    nodeB["Step 2 Wait for echo round trip t = 2d over c"]
    nodeC["Step 3 Receive echo amplitude A"]
    nodeD["Step 4 Convert time t to depth d = ct over 2"]
    nodeE["Step 5 Place pixel at depth d on vertical axis"]
    nodeF["Step 6 Sweep horizontally at chart speed"]
    nodeG["Step 7 Repeat at PRF"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
**Q:** Define M-scan ultrasound. What does the vertical and horizontal axis represent in an M-mode display?

**Model Answer:**
M-scan or Motion-mode ultrasonography is a single-line, time-motion display of ultrasound echoes. The **vertical axis represents the depth** of the reflecting interface (computed from round-trip time), and the **horizontal axis represents time**. Brightness of the trace encodes echo amplitude. It is used primarily in cardiology to study rapidly moving structures such as heart valves and cardiac walls. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for axes, 1 for application)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q:** State the standard speed of ultrasound in soft tissue and the depth equation used in M-mode.

**Model Answer:**
The standard speed of ultrasound in soft tissue is **c = 1540 m/s**. The depth equation is:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where `d` is the depth of the reflecting interface, `c` is the speed of sound in tissue, and `t` is the round-trip time of the echo. *(3 marks: 1 for c value, 2 for equation with factor-½ justification)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor ½** in depth equations is the most common mistake — it costs **2 full marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing PRF with frequency** — these are two entirely different quantities. PRF is the *pulse* rate (kHz); frequency is the *carrier* ultrasound (MHz).
> 3. **Mixing up axial and lateral resolution definitions** — axial is along the beam, lateral is perpendicular.
> 4. **Not stating the units** in final numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Skipping the Nyquist justification** in sampling problems — just writing `PRF ≥ 2 f_max` without derivation loses 1 mark.

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle of M-mode ultrasound display. How does it differ from B-mode display?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An M-mode ultrasound system operating at 3 MHz with c = 1540 m/s detects an echo returning at 78 µs. The PRF is 5 kHz and the pulse duration is 1 µs. Calculate:
  (i) Depth of the reflecting interface.
  (ii) Maximum unambiguous depth.
  (iii) Axial resolution.
  (iv) Whether the depth falls within the unambiguous range.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Principle & Difference

**Principle (4 marks):**
M-mode ultrasonography works on the **pulse-echo principle**. A single piezoelectric transducer emits a short, focused ultrasound pulse along one fixed scan line. The same transducer receives returning echoes. Each echo is converted into a brightness-modulated dot placed on the display's **vertical axis at a depth proportional to its round-trip time**:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

Successive pulses are transmitted at a high PRF (1–10 kHz). The display is swept horizontally at a much slower chart speed (25, 50, or 100 mm/s), so that over time, the moving interface traces out a continuous curve representing its motion along the beam axis.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Diagram 4.1** above (Clock → Pulser → Transducer → Tissue → TGC Amplifier → Envelope Detector → Display Memory → Slow Sweep → M-mode Display).

**Difference from B-mode (1 mark):**

| Aspect | M-Mode | B-Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Lines of sight | One (1-D) | Many forming 2-D cross-section |
| Axes | Depth (Y) vs Time (X) | Depth (Y) vs Lateral position (X) |
| Temporal resolution | Very high (effective kHz) | Limited (30–60 fps) |
| Best use | Motion analysis | Anatomical mapping |

#### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:** `f = 3 MHz`, `c = 1540 m/s`, `t = 78 µs = 78 × 10^{-6} s`, `PRF = 5 kHz = 5000 Hz`, `τ = 1 µs = 1 × 10^{-6} s`.

**(i) Depth:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 78 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.12012}{2} = 0.06006 \text{ m} \approx 6.0 \text{ cm}$$

**(ii) Maximum unambiguous depth:**

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 5000} = \frac{1540}{10000} = 0.154 \text{ m} = 15.4 \text{ cm}$$

**(iii) Axial resolution:**

$$\Delta d = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.00154}{2} = 0.00077 \text{ m} = 0.77 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Comparison:** 6.0 cm < 15.4 cm → **Yes, the depth is within the unambiguous range.** No range ambiguity.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the depth equation and the maximum unambiguous range equation for an M-mode ultrasound system. State the Nyquist sampling criterion in this context and explain what happens if it is violated.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An M-mode trace shows two consecutive R-wave peaks separated by 20 mm on a chart recorder running at 50 mm/s. Calculate the heart rate. If a structure oscillates at 15 Hz, what is the minimum PRF required to avoid aliasing? Comment on the trade-off between imaging depth and PRF.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivations

**Depth equation (3 marks):** See Derivation 3.1.

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Maximum unambiguous range (2 marks):** See Derivation 3.2.

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$$

**Nyquist criterion in M-mode (1 mark):**

$$\text{PRF} \geq 2 \cdot f_{motion,max}$$

**Consequence of violation (1 mark):** If the Nyquist criterion is violated, the motion of the structure will be **aliased** — i.e., the apparent direction and frequency of motion on the M-mode trace will be incorrect. The structure may appear to move in the wrong direction or at a much lower frequency than it actually does.

#### Part (b) — Numerical & Conceptual

**Given:** `Δx = 20 mm`, `v = 50 mm/s`, `f_motion = 15 Hz`.

**(i) Heart rate (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Time between R peaks:

$$\Delta t = \frac{\Delta x}{v} = \frac{20 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm/s}} = 0.4 \text{ s}$$

Step 2 — Heart rate:

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{0.4} = 150 \text{ bpm}$$

**(ii) Minimum PRF (2 marks):**

$$\text{PRF}_{min} = 2 \times 15 = 30 \text{ Hz}$$

**Note:** This is the *minimum* PRF for motion sampling. In practice, PRF is set much higher (kHz range) for adequate depth coverage.

**(iii) Depth–PRF trade-off (2 marks):**
The two equations

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{PRF} \geq 2 f_{max}$$

are **inversely linked**. Increasing depth requires *decreasing* PRF, which in turn *reduces* the maximum motion frequency that can be sampled without aliasing. Conversely, increasing PRF allows higher-frequency motion to be captured but restricts maximum imaging depth. Operators must therefore **balance depth and temporal resolution** based on the clinical target.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — M-Scan**

- **M-mode = Motion mode** — single-line, time-depth display with brightness-modulated echoes.
- **Vertical axis = depth** (from round-trip time); **horizontal axis = time**; **brightness = echo amplitude**.
- **Standard speed of sound in soft tissue:** `c = 1540 m/s` — memorize this.
- **Depth equation:** `d = c·t / 2` — the **factor ½ is mandatory** (pulse travels to and from the interface).
- **Round-trip time:** `t = 2d / c`.
- **Maximum unambiguous depth:** `d_max = c / (2·PRF)`.
- **PRF definition:** number of ultrasound pulses fired per second along the scan line (typically 1–10 kHz in cardiac M-mode).
- **Axial resolution:** `Δd = c·τ / 2` (where τ is pulse duration) — *smaller pulse = better resolution*.
- **Lateral resolution:** `Δℓ = λ·d / D` (λ = wavelength, D = transducer aperture).
- **Nyquist sampling rule for motion:** `PRF ≥ 2·f_motion,max`; otherwise **aliasing** of motion occurs.
- **Depth–PRF trade-off:** deeper imaging ⇒ lower PRF ⇒ lower max motion frequency sampled.
- **Typical clinical frequencies:** 2–10 MHz; **typical chart speeds:** 25, 50, 100 mm/s.
- **Primary clinical use:** cardiology — mitral valve motion (E-F slope), LV wall thickness, pericardial effusion, fetal heart rate.
- **Key advantage:** extremely high temporal resolution (effective kHz-range) — captures rapid motion that B-mode cannot.
- **Key limitation:** only one scan line — no anatomical cross-section.
- **M-mode forms the basis** for modern Tissue Doppler, Speckle Tracking, and IVUS pullback displays.
- **Common exam trap:** confusing PRF (kHz) with transducer frequency (MHz) — they are unrelated quantities.
- **Heart rate calculation from M-mode:** `HR = 60 / (Δx / v)`, where Δx is R–R distance and v is chart speed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT521 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biomedical Engineering (BME) Applications** refer to the systematic deployment of engineering principles, mathematical modelling, and modern technology to **diagnose, treat, monitor, and rehabilitate** human physiological conditions. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEECT521), the "Applications" module consolidates the theoretical concepts learned in Modules 1–3 into practical, real-world medical and healthcare systems.

The four pillars of BME Applications as defined by the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework are:

| Pillar | Engineering Domain | Medical Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Diagnostic** | Signal processing, imaging physics | Radiology, Pathology |
| **Therapeutic** | Control systems, power electronics | Cardiology, Oncology |
| **Restorative** | Materials science, robotics | Rehabilitation, Prosthetics |
| **Preventive/Monitoring** | IoT, biosensors, telemetry | Public Health, Wellness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The "Applications" module is **integrative** — questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE) frequently require a student to map a given clinical problem to its underlying engineering principle (e.g., "Which transducer principle does a CT scanner use?"). Mastering this **reverse-mapping skill** is essential for scoring above 80%.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Translator"

Imagine a BME engineer as a **universal translator sitting between two foreign diplomats**:
- **Diplomat A (The Human Body):** Speaks the language of bioelectric potentials, chemical concentrations, mechanical pressures, and thermal gradients.
- **Diplomat B (The Electronic Device/Computer):** Speaks the language of voltages, currents, bits, and pixels.

Just as a translator must understand *both* languages and the *context* of the conversation, a biomedical engineer must understand physiology *and* electronics, then design hardware/software that converts one domain's signal into the other without distortion or harm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Every BME application can be reduced to a **transduction chain**: Biological Variable → Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Processor → Actuator/Display. Memorize this chain — it is the skeleton of nearly every 14-mark question in this module.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in BME Applications

- **Normal Resting Heart Rate:** **60–100 bpm** (beats per minute)
- **Normal Body Temperature:** **37 °C ± 0.5 °C** (98.6 °F)
- **Standard Resting Blood Pressure:** **120/80 mmHg**
- **Speed of Ultrasound in Soft Tissue:** **1540 m/s** (industry standard reference value)
- **Diagnostic X-ray Energy Range:** **20 keV – 150 keV**
- **Static Magnetic Field of Clinical MRI:** **1.5 T – 3 T** (Tesla)
- **Refractive Index of Human Cornea:** **1.376**
- **Acoustic Impedance of Soft Tissue:** **1.63 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** (Rayl)
- **Normal Arterial Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂):** **95 % – 100 %**
- **Body Surface Area (DuBois formula):** **≈ 1.73 m²** (standard adult)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of BME Applications — Structured Logic

### A. Diagnostic Imaging Applications
- **Radiographic (Ionising Radiation)**
  - **X-ray Radiography:** Uses differential absorption of X-rays by tissues (bone vs. soft tissue vs. air).
  - **Computed Tomography (CT):** Cross-sectional imaging via rotating X-ray source and reconstruction algorithms.
  - **PET (Positron Emission Tomography):** Detects gamma photons from positron-emitting radiotracers (e.g., ¹⁸F-FDG).
  - **SPECT:** Uses single-photon-emitting tracers (e.g., ⁹⁹ᵐTc).
- **Non-Ionising**
  - **MRI:** Exploits nuclear magnetic resonance of hydrogen protons.
  - **Ultrasound Imaging:** Uses reflected high-frequency sound waves (2–18 MHz).
  - **Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT):** Uses low-coherence interferometry for retinal imaging.

### B. Bioelectric Signal Monitoring Applications
- **ECG (Electrocardiogram):** Records cardiac electrical activity (10 mV order, 0.05–150 Hz bandwidth).
- **EEG (Electroencephalogram):** Records cortical brain activity (10–100 µV, 0.5–100 Hz).
- **EMG (Electromyogram):** Records skeletal muscle activity (50 µV – 5 mV, 20 Hz – 5 kHz).
- **EOG (Electrooculogram):** Records eye-movement potentials (0.05–3.5 mV).

### C. Therapeutic Device Applications
- **Cardiac:** Pacemakers (fixed-rate, demand, programmable), Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillators (ICD).
- **Renal:** Haemodialysis machines, peritoneal dialysis systems.
- **Respiratory:** Mechanical ventilators, CPAP/BiPAP machines.
- **Surgical:** Electrosurgical units, surgical lasers (CO₂, Nd:YAG), da Vinci surgical robot.
- **Drug Delivery:** Insulin pumps, implantable drug infusion pumps.

### D. Prosthetic & Assistive Applications
- **Cochlear Implants:** Convert sound to electrical stimulation of the auditory nerve.
- **Retinal Prosthesis (Argus II / Bionic Eye):** Stimulate retinal ganglion cells.
- **Powered Exoskeletons:** Assist locomotion in spinal cord injury patients.
- **Myoelectric Limbs:** Prosthetic arms controlled by EMG signals from residual muscles.

### E. Emerging Applications
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** EEG-driven communication for ALS patients.
- **3D Bioprinting:** Printing living tissue scaffolds.
- **Wearable Health Tech:** Smartwatches with SpO₂, ECG, body-temperature monitoring.
- **AI-Assisted Diagnosis:** Deep-learning CAD systems for mammography, CT lung nodule detection.
- **Telemedicine:** Real-time remote consultation using IoMT (Internet of Medical Things).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** A typical 14-mark question asks: *"Discuss the working principle and applications of [device]"*. Students who follow a **structured template** (Working Principle → Block Diagram → Clinical Application → Safety/Ethical Concern) consistently score 12+/14.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application Domain | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | X-ray Attenuation (Beer-Lambert Law) | $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹), $x$ = thickness (cm) |
| 2 | $HU = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}} - \mu_{\text{air}}}$ | CT Hounsfield Unit | Dimensionless; Water = 0 HU, Air = −1000 HU |
| 3 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 \, f_0 \, v \, \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler Ultrasound Shift | $f_0$ = transmitted freq, $v$ = blood velocity, $\theta$ = beam angle, $c$ = 1540 m/s |
| 4 | $f_{\text{Larmor}} = \gamma \, B_0$ | MRI Precession Frequency | $\gamma/2\pi$ for H¹ = **42.58 MHz/T** |
| 5 | $Z = \rho \, c$ | Acoustic Impedance | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $c$ = sound speed (m/s) |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{4 \, a}{\pi \, r^4} \cdot \eta \cdot L$ | Hagen-Poiseuille Flow Resistance | $a$ = constant; used in cardiovascular modelling |
| 7 | $V_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\int_0^T V(t) \, dt}{T}$ | ECG Mean Voltage Calculation | For numerical integration in DSP labs |
| 8 | $F = m \cdot a$ (Newton) — applied as $F = \Delta P \cdot A$ | Ventilator Pressure Delivery | $\Delta P$ in cmH₂O, $A$ in m² |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{P}{A}$ (Ultrasonic Intensity) | Ultrasound Dosimetry | $P$ = acoustic power (W), $A$ = beam area (cm²); limit **720 mW/cm²** (FDA) |
| 10 | $D = \Gamma \cdot t$ (Optical Density) | Pulse Oximetry (Beer-Lambert at 660 nm & 940 nm) | Ratio used to compute SpO₂ |
| 11 | $P = \dfrac{V^2}{Z}$ (Power delivered to tissue) | Surgical Diathermy / RF Ablation | $V$ = voltage, $Z$ = tissue impedance |
| 12 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | AI Diagnostic Model Evaluation | Used in KTU machine-learning applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Formula 1:** The Beer-Lambert law ($I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$) is the **single most tested formula** in BME applications. Variants appear in X-ray imaging, optical oximetry, OCT, and spectrophotometry. Master it with its logarithmic form: $\mu x = \ln(I_0 / I)$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Sub-Field | Application Leveraged | Industry Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **VLSI / Embedded Systems** | Pacemaker ASICs, neural implants | $4.2 B market (2024, Medtronic) |
| **Robotics & Mechatronics** | da Vinci robot, exoskeletons | Surgical precision 10× human hand |
| **Image Processing / AI** | CT-MRI reconstruction, CNN-based CAD | Google Health, IBM Watson Oncology |
| **Materials Science** | Biocompatible Ti-alloy implants, hydrogels | Reduces rejection rates below 5% |
| **Wireless Communications** | Implant telemetry, telemedicine | 5G enables remote robotic surgery |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beer-Lambert Law for X-ray Attenuation

### Problem Statement
A monoenergetic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a tissue slab of thickness $x$. Derive an expression for the transmitted intensity $I$ in terms of the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define an infinitesimally thin slice of tissue.**
Consider a tissue slice of thickness $dx$. The probability of a photon being absorbed or scattered in this slice is proportional to $dx$.

$$dI = -\mu \, I \, dx$$

This means the *change* in intensity $dI$ is **negative** (attenuation) and proportional to the current intensity $I$ and slice thickness $dx$. The constant of proportionality $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient, which has units of cm⁻¹.

**Step 2: Separate variables to obtain a differential equation.**

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

This is a standard first-order separable ODE. The negative sign indicates that intensity *decreases* as the beam travels through more tissue.

**Step 3: Integrate both sides from the entry point to the exit point of the beam.**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = \int_{0}^{x} -\mu \, dx$$

The left-hand side integrates to a natural logarithm; the right-hand side integrates to a linear function of $x$.

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals.**

$$[\ln I]_{I_0}^{I} = -\mu \, [x]_{0}^{x}$$

Substituting the limits:

$$\ln I - \ln I_0 = -\mu \, x$$

Using the logarithm identity $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu \, x$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate both sides to obtain the final Beer-Lambert form.**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The intensity drops **exponentially** with depth. Doubling the thickness does *not* halve the intensity — it squares the attenuation factor. This is why thick body parts (abdomen) require higher X-ray doses than thin parts (hand).

**Step 6: Apply the half-value layer (HVL) concept.**
The HVL is the thickness required to reduce $I$ to $I_0 / 2$. Setting $I = I_0/2$:

$$\frac{I_0}{2} = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \, x_{1/2}}$$

Solving:

$$x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Doppler Shift for Diagnostic Ultrasound

### Problem Statement
Derive the frequency shift observed when an ultrasound beam reflects off red blood cells moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ to the beam axis.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the geometry.**
Let the transducer emit ultrasound at frequency $f_0$ and wavelength $\lambda$. A red blood cell moving with velocity $v$ acts as a **moving reflector**.

**Step 2: Frequency observed by the moving red blood cell (receiver in motion).**
Using the Doppler effect for a moving observer:

$$f' = f_0 \left(\frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

The factor $\cos\theta$ accounts for the angle between the velocity vector and the beam axis. Here $c$ is the speed of sound in soft tissue (1540 m/s).

**Step 3: Frequency of the reflected wave observed by the stationary transducer (source in motion).**
The blood cell now re-emits (reflects) at frequency $f'$, but the cell is moving away as a *source*. The transducer receives:

$$f_r = f' \left(\frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta}\right)$$

The denominator is $(c - v\cos\theta)$ because the source (cell) is moving *toward* the transducer when blood flows *toward* the probe (positive Doppler).

**Step 4: Substitute $f'$ into the equation for $f_r$.**

$$f_r = f_0 \left(\frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c}\right) \left(\frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta}\right)$$

**Step 5: Simplify the expression.**

$$f_r = f_0 \left(\frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}\right)$$

**Step 6: Apply the clinically valid approximation $v \ll c$.**
Since blood velocity (≈ 0.1–1 m/s) is much smaller than $c$ (1540 m/s), we can linearise using the binomial identity:

$$\frac{1}{1 - \frac{v\cos\theta}{c}} \approx 1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c}$$

Therefore:

$$f_r \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c}\right)\left(1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

**Step 7: Drop the second-order $v^2/c^2$ term.**

$$f_r \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v\cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

**Step 8: Define the Doppler shift $\Delta f = f_r - f_0$.**

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{2 \, f_0 \, v \, \cos\theta}{c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Application:** In foetal heart monitors, an $f_0 = 2$ MHz probe at $\theta = 0°$ gives $\Delta f \approx 260$ Hz for a peak foetal heart velocity of 0.1 m/s — well within audible range, which is why the *whoosh-whoosh* of the foetal heart is audible on the loudspeaker.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hounsfield Unit (CT Number)

### Step 1: Define the linear attenuation coefficient of water as the reference.**
By definition, water has a linear attenuation coefficient $\mu_{\text{water}}$.

**Step 2: Define the CT (Hounsfield) number for any tissue.**

$$HU = K \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the conventional constant $K = 1000$.**

$$HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

**Step 4: Verify boundary conditions.**
- For **water:** $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = \mu_{\text{water}} \Rightarrow HU = 0$. ✓
- For **air:** $\mu_{\text{air}} \approx 0 \Rightarrow HU \approx -1000$. ✓
- For **dense bone:** $\mu_{\text{bone}} \approx 2 \mu_{\text{water}} \Rightarrow HU \approx +1000$. ✓

**Step 5: Sample values (memorise for KTU exams):**

| Tissue | Approx. HU |
|---|---|
| Air | −1000 |
| Fat | −100 to −50 |
| Water | 0 |
| CSF | +15 |
| White matter | +20 to +30 |
| Grey matter | +35 to +45 |
| Blood (clotted) | +60 to +80 |
| Bone (cortical) | +700 to +3000 |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application Mapping

| Clinical Problem | BME Solution | Underlying Principle | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cardiac arrhythmia | Pacemaker / ICD | Pulse generation circuit + electrode-tissue interface | Battery life (≈ 7 yrs) |
| End-stage renal failure | Haemodialysis | Semi-permeable membrane diffusion (Fick's law) | Infection risk, time cost |
| Sensorineural deafness | Cochlear implant | Electrical stimulation of CN VIII | Limited frequency resolution |
| Type-1 diabetes | Insulin pump | Continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion | Sensor accuracy drift |
| Paralysis (spinal injury) | Exoskeleton / FES | EMG-driven actuators | Fatigue, cost |
| Brain tumour | Gamma Knife / LINAC | Focused ionising radiation | Radiation safety |
| Blindness (retinal) | Argus II retinal implant | Electrical stimulation of retinal cells | Low visual acuity (~20/1200) |
| COVID-19 respiratory failure | Ventilator | Positive-pressure airflow + PEEP control | Barotrauma risk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Biomedical Application Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS"]

    ROOT --> DIAG["DIAGNOSTIC APPLICATIONS"]
    ROOT --> THER["THERAPEUTIC APPLICATIONS"]
    ROOT --> REST["RESTORATIVE APPLICATIONS"]
    ROOT --> MON["MONITORING APPLICATIONS"]
    ROOT --> EMRG["EMERGING APPLICATIONS"]

    DIAG --> IMG["Medical Imaging"]
    DIAG --> SIG["Bioelectric Signal Recording"]
    DIAG --> LAB["In-vitro Diagnostic Devices"]

    IMG --> XR["X-Ray and CT"]
    IMG --> MRI["MRI"]
    IMG --> US["Ultrasound"]
    IMG --> NM["Nuclear Medicine PET SPECT"]
    IMG --> OCT["Optical Coherence Tomography"]

    SIG --> ECG["ECG"]
    SIG --> EEG["EEG"]
    SIG --> EMG["EMG"]
    SIG --> EOG["EOG"]

    THER --> CARD["Cardiac Pacemaker and ICD"]
    THER --> RENL["Dialysis Machine"]
    THER --> RESP["Ventilator"]
    THER --> SURG["Surgical Robot and Laser"]
    THER --> DRUG["Insulin Pump and Infusion"]

    REST --> COCH["Cochlear Implant"]
    REST --> RET["Retinal Prosthesis"]
    REST --> EXO["Powered Exoskeleton"]
    REST --> MYO["Myoelectric Limb"]

    MON --> ICU["ICU Patient Monitor"]
    MON --> FET["Fetal Heart Rate Monitor"]
    MON --> WEAR["Wearable Health Tracker"]
    MON --> TELE["Telemedicine IoMT"]

    EMRG --> BCI["Brain Computer Interface"]
    EMRG --> AIDX["AI Assisted Diagnosis"]
    EMRG --> BIO3D["3D Bioprinting"]
    EMRG --> NANO["Nanomedicine and Drug Delivery"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Generic BME Application Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BIO["Biological Variable"]:::bio
    SENS["Sensor or Transducer"]:::sens
    COND["Signal Conditioner"]:::cond
    PROC["Processor or DSP Unit"]:::proc
    DSP["Display or Actuator"]:::disp
    FB["Feedback Loop"]:::fb

    BIO -->|Physiological Signal| SENS
    SENS -->|Electrical Signal| COND
    COND -->|Filtered Amplified| PROC
    PROC -->|Decision or Image| DSP
    DSP -.->|Control Action| FB
    FB -.-> BIO

    classDef bio fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#0040a0,color:#000
    classDef cond fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#0a7a0a,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a07000,color:#000
    classDef disp fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#5a00a0,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#a05500,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Pacemaker (Therapeutic Application Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENS["Intracardiac Sense Electrode"]:::sense
    AMP["Sense Amplifier"]:::amp
    COMP["Comparator and Threshold Detector"]:::comp
    LOGIC["Timing and Control Logic"]:::logic
    PULSE["Pulse Generator Circuit"]:::pulse
    PACE["Pacing Electrode to Myocardium"]:::pace
    BAT["Lithium Iodine Battery"]:::bat
    PROG["External Programmer Telemetry"]:::prog

    SENS --> AMP
    AMP --> COMP
    COMP --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> PULSE
    PULSE --> PACE
    BAT -.->|Powers| LOGIC
    BAT -.->|Powers| PULSE
    PROG -.->|Wireless Adjust| LOGIC

    classDef sense fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef pulse fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    classDef pace fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    classDef bat fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef prog fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d0099,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A pacemaker continuously monitors the heart's intrinsic rhythm. If the *sense amplifier* fails to detect a natural beat within a programmed interval (typically 1 second → 60 bpm), the *timing logic* fires the *pulse generator*, which delivers a **2–5 V, 0.5 ms** pulse to the myocardium via the *pacing electrode*. The lithium-iodine battery typically lasts **7–10 years** before surgical replacement.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]
**List any three therapeutic applications of biomedical engineering and state the physiological parameter each one controls.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Therapeutic Device | Physiological Parameter Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cardiac Pacemaker | Heart rate (beats per minute) |
| 2 | Insulin Pump | Blood glucose concentration |
| 3 | Haemodialysis Machine | Blood urea, creatinine, electrolyte levels |
| 4 | Mechanical Ventilator | Tidal volume, airway pressure, oxygen partial pressure |

(Any three are sufficient for 3 marks.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]
**Differentiate between diagnostic and therapeutic applications of biomedical engineering with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Diagnostic | Therapeutic |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To *identify* a disease or physiological state | To *treat* or *cure* a disease |
| **Energy transfer to patient** | Minimal (non-invasive) | Often significant (invasive) |
| **Output** | Image, waveform, or numerical reading | Drug delivery, electrical stimulation, mechanical support |
| **Example** | MRI scanner | Cardiac pacemaker |
| **Example (alternate)** | ECG machine | Insulin pump |

(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for example, 1 for valid differentiation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q4 Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a **cardiac pacemaker**. Explain the difference between a *fixed-rate (asynchronous)* and a *demand (synchronous)* pacemaker. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Doppler shift equation** used in diagnostic ultrasound. A 5 MHz transducer is used to measure blood flow in a superficial artery at an angle of 30° to the flow direction. If the Doppler shift observed is 1.5 kHz, calculate the blood flow velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A cardiac pacemaker is an implantable electronic device that delivers precisely timed electrical pulses to the heart muscle (myocardium) to maintain an adequate heart rate in patients with bradyarrhythmia or AV block.

**2. Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
Refer to the Functional Architecture diagram in Section 4.3. The major blocks are:
- **Lithium-iodine battery:** Energises the entire circuit (typical voltage 2.8 V, capacity 1–2 Ah).
- **Sense amplifier:** Amplifies the intrinsic R-wave (≈ 5–10 mV) detected by the intracardiac electrode.
- **Comparator + threshold detector:** Compares sensed R-wave against a programmed threshold; if absent, triggers the timing circuit.
- **Timing/control logic:** Implements the escape interval (e.g., 1000 ms → 60 bpm) and refractory period (≈ 300 ms) to prevent T-wave sensing.
- **Pulse generator:** Produces a **2–5 V, 0.5–1.0 ms** constant-voltage or constant-current pulse.
- **Pacing electrode (cathode):** Delivers the pulse to the endocardial tissue; the anode is the pacemaker can.
- **Telemetry/Programmer:** Allows non-invasive adjustment of rate, amplitude, pulse width, and sensitivity post-implant.

**3. Fixed-Rate vs Demand Pacemaker (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fixed-Rate (Asynchronous, VOO) | Demand (Synchronous, VVI) |
|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic sensing | **None** — fires continuously | **Continuous** — senses R-wave |
| Trigger condition | Free-running timer | Absence of intrinsic beat for > escape interval |
| Risk of competition with native rhythm | **High** — can induce ventricular fibrillation | **Low** — inhibited when intrinsic rhythm is normal |
| Battery consumption | Higher (always fires) | Lower (suppressed when not needed) |
| Clinical use today | Obsolete | Standard of care |

**Conclusion (0 mark, free):** Demand pacemakers are universally preferred because they avoid *pacemaker-mediated arrhythmia*.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. State the Doppler equation (1 mark):**

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 \, f_0 \, v \, \cos\theta}{c}$$

**2. Substitute the given values (3 marks):**
- $f_0 = 5 \text{ MHz} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $\Delta f = 1.5 \text{ kHz} = 1.5 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$
- $\theta = 30°$, so $\cos 30° = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$
- $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$ (speed of ultrasound in soft tissue)

**3. Rearrange to solve for $v$ (1 mark):**

$$v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 \, f_0 \cdot \cos\theta}$$

**4. Substitute and compute (1.5 marks):**

$$v = \frac{1.5 \times 10^3 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.8660}$$

Calculate the numerator:
$$1.5 \times 10^3 \times 1540 = 2.31 \times 10^6$$

Calculate the denominator:
$$2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.8660 = 8.66 \times 10^6$$

Final velocity:
$$v = \frac{2.31 \times 10^6}{8.66 \times 10^6} \approx 0.2667 \text{ m/s}$$

**5. Final answer (0.5 mark):**

$$\boxed{v \approx 0.267 \text{ m/s} \approx 26.7 \text{ cm/s}}$$

This value is consistent with normal arterial blood flow velocity (peak systolic 0.2–1.0 m/s).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q5 Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** with a block diagram. Define **Larmor frequency** and compute the Larmor frequency for a hydrogen proton in a 1.5 T MRI system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **Beer-Lambert law**, derive the expression for the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ of an X-ray beam passing through soft tissue. An X-ray beam of intensity 100 units passes through 5 cm of muscle tissue and emerges with intensity 20 units. Calculate the linear attenuation coefficient. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Working Principle of MRI (3 marks):**
MRI exploits the **nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR)** of hydrogen protons (¹H) abundant in body water and fat. The patient is placed in a strong static magnetic field $B_0$ (typically 1.5 T or 3 T), which aligns the proton spins either parallel (low energy) or anti-parallel (high energy) to $B_0$. A **radiofrequency (RF) pulse** at the Larmor frequency tips the net magnetisation vector into the transverse plane. When the RF pulse is switched off, the protons relax, emitting RF signals that are detected by receiver coils. **Gradient magnetic fields** in x, y, z directions encode spatial position. A computer reconstructs cross-sectional images via the **Fourier transform** (k-space filling).

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Main Magnet B0 1.5T"]:::main
    G["Gradient Coils X Y Z"]:::grad
    RF["RF Transmit Coil"]:::rf
    RC["RF Receive Coil"]:::rc
    P["Preamplifier and ADC"]:::pre
    CPU["Computer with Fourier Reconstruction"]:::cpu
    MON["Display Monitor"]:::mon
    COOL["Cryocooler for Superconducting Magnet"]:::cool

    M -.->|Static Field| PAT["Patient"]
    G -.->|Spatial Encoding| PAT
    RF -.->|Excite Protons| PAT
    PAT -.->|Emitted Signal| RC
    RC --> P
    P --> CPU
    CPU --> MON
    COOL -.->|Cooling| M

    classDef main fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#800020,color:#000
    classDef grad fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#800020,color:#000
    classDef rf fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298,color:#000
    classDef rc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298,color:#000
    classDef pre fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    classDef cpu fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#000
    classDef mon fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#3d0a7a,color:#000
    classDef cool fill:#dee2e6,stroke:#495057,color:#000
```

**3. Larmor Frequency Calculation (2 marks):**
The Larmor frequency is the precession frequency of a proton's magnetic moment in a static field $B_0$:

$$f_{\text{Larmor}} = \gamma \, B_0$$

For hydrogen ¹H, the gyromagnetic ratio is:

$$\gamma/2\pi = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T}$$

Substituting $B_0 = 1.5$ T:

$$f_{\text{Larmor}} = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T} \times 1.5 \text{ T} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{f_{\text{Larmor}} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}}$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. State Beer-Lambert law (1 mark):**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

**2. Rearrange to solve for $\mu$ (2 marks):**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

Divide both sides by $-x$:

$$\mu = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \frac{1}{x} \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**3. Substitute the given values (1 mark):**
- $I_0 = 100$ units
- $I = 20$ units
- $x = 5$ cm

**4. Compute the log ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{I_0}{I} = \frac{100}{20} = 5$$

$$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$

**5. Final calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$\mu = \frac{1.6094}{5 \text{ cm}} = 0.3219 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\mu \approx 0.322 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$

This value is consistent with soft tissue attenuation at diagnostic X-ray energies (~60–80 keV).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Applications" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in Doppler / Larmor calculations** (–1 mark). Always convert MHz → Hz, cm → m, etc., before substituting.
> 2. **Using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in ultrasound problems** (–1 mark). Ultrasound uses **1540 m/s** in soft tissue, *not* the speed of light.
> 3. **Confusing MRI Larmor frequency with RF heating frequency** (–1 mark). Larmor frequency is for *precession* of protons, not for therapeutic RF ablation (which is in the 100 kHz – 1 MHz range).
> 4. **Stating "pacemaker" without specifying the demand mode** (–0.5 mark). Always specify VVI / DDD / VOO codes from the NBG pacemaker code.
> 5. **Omitting the cos θ factor** in Doppler equations (–1 mark). KTU examiners *always* test the angle dependence.
> 6. **Forgetting Hounsfield reference** (–0.5 mark). CT number is defined *with respect to water*, not air.
> 7. **Not drawing arrows or signal-flow direction** in block diagrams (–0.5 mark). Arrows are mandatory in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Applications**

- ✅ **Master the 5 application pillars:** Diagnostic, Therapeutic, Restorative, Monitoring, Emerging.
- ✅ **Memorise the Beer-Lambert equation** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ and its logarithmic form.
- ✅ **Be fluent in unit conversions:** 1 MHz = 10⁶ Hz; 1 T = 10⁴ Gauss; 1 mmHg ≈ 133.3 Pa.
- ✅ **Know the four standard clinical constants by heart:** $c_{\text{ultrasound}} = 1540$ m/s, $\gamma/2\pi (^1H) = 42.58$ MHz/T, HU$_{\text{water}} = 0$, HU$_{\text{air}} = -1000$.
- ✅ **Doppler equation** always has the factor $2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ — never omit the 2 or cos θ.
- ✅ **MRI Larmor frequency** for 1.5 T ≈ 63.87 MHz; for 3 T ≈ 127.74 MHz.
- ✅ **Pacemaker code VVI** = Ventricular pacing, Ventricular sensing, Inhibited response (memorise).
- ✅ **Hounsfield values** (rough memory): Air = −1000, Fat = −100, Water = 0, Brain = 20–45, Bone = +700 to +3000.
- ✅ **Acoustic impedance** of soft tissue ≈ 1.63 × 10⁶ Rayl; mismatch at air-tissue interface causes near-total reflection (why ultrasound gel is used).
- ✅ **CT, MRI, PET** = no moving parts in patient; **X-ray, ultrasound** = real-time imaging; **ECG, EEG, EMG** = electrical recording, not imaging.
- ✅ **FDA acoustic intensity limit for diagnostic ultrasound = 720 mW/cm²** (Spatial Peak Temporal Average).
- ✅ **Pacemaker battery life ≈ 7–10 years**; **Cochlear implant** bypasses damaged hair cells and stimulates the auditory nerve directly.
- ✅ **Wearable health tech** is a *monitoring* application, not a *therapeutic* one.
- ✅ **Emerging applications** (BCI, bioprinting, AI) are tested for 3–5 marks as "trend" questions.
- ✅ **Always answer "Applications" questions in this order:** (1) Working Principle → (2) Block Diagram → (3) Clinical Use → (4) Limitations / Safety.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-time Ultrasonic Imaging Systems and Probes. Magnetic Resonance Imaging - Basic NMR Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT521 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-time Ultrasonic Imaging Systems and Probes. Magnetic Resonance Imaging - Basic NMR Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Real-time Ultrasonic Imaging Systems — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-time Ultrasonic Imaging (B-mode / 2D Gray-scale Imaging)** is a diagnostic ultrasound modality in which a 2-D cross-sectional image of tissues is constructed and refreshed continuously (typically **15–60 frames per second**) by rapidly sweeping the ultrasound beam through a planar region of the body, allowing the operator to observe motion (heart, fetus, blood flow) as it happens.

Unlike static B-scan imaging (which acquires one frame and then "freezes" the image), real-time systems use **electronically steered or mechanically swept multi-element transducers** that fire pulse sequences so quickly that the displayed image is perceived as a *live movie* of internal anatomy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under *Diagnostic & Therapeutic Equipment – Part II*. The expected cognitive level is **Apply (L3) / Analyse (L4)** — students must be able to identify the components, explain beam-formation, and discuss the relationship between frame rate, line density, and depth.

---

## 1.2 The Probe (Transducer) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An ultrasound **probe** (or **transducer**) is a hand-held device containing one or more piezoelectric elements that **convert electrical pulses into mechanical (acoustic) pressure waves** and **convert returning echoes back into electrical signals**, enabling the pulse-echo principle that underlies all diagnostic ultrasound.

### Types of Probes Used in Real-time Imaging

| Probe Type | Shape of Image | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Array** | Rectangular | Vascular, musculoskeletal, superficial structures |
| **Convex (Curvilinear) Array** | Truncated sector / fan | Abdominal, obstetrics |
| **Phased Array (Sector)** | Pie-slice fan | Cardiac (echocardiography), intercostal |
| **Mechanical Sector** | Fan (swept by motor) | Cardiac (older systems) |
| **Endocavitary** | Convex / fan | Transvaginal, transrectal |
| **3-D / Matrix Array** | Pyramid / volume | 3-D/4-D obstetric, cardiac |

> [!NOTE]
> In real-time systems, *electronic* steering (phased arrays) and *electronic* focusing (delays) have largely replaced mechanical scanning because they are faster, more reliable, and silent.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sonar Flashlight"

Imagine you are standing in a dark room holding a **flashlight that you can switch on and off in microsecond pulses**, and you have **supernatural hearing** that lets you listen to the *echoes* bouncing off invisible objects:

- When the light pulse hits a wall, the echo tells you how far the wall is.
- If you *tilt* the flashlight quickly, you map out the shape of the room.
- If you do this **fifty times a second**, you essentially *see* the room "live" — that is exactly how real-time ultrasound works, except:
  - The "light" is **sound at 2–15 MHz** (inaudible to humans).
  - The "listening" is the same piezoelectric element acting in reverse.
  - The "tilt" is done by **electronically delaying** which elements of the array fire first.

This is the *pulse-echo + beam-steering* principle. It is the same idea used by **bats (echolocation)** and **submarines (SONAR)**, scaled up for medical imaging.

---

## 1.4 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** is a non-ionizing, tomographic imaging modality that exploits the quantum-mechanical property of **nuclear spin** of hydrogen protons (¹H) in body tissues. When placed in a strong static magnetic field **B₀** and excited by a radio-frequency (RF) pulse at the **Larmor frequency**, the protons absorb and then re-emit energy. The spatial encoding of this emitted signal allows reconstruction of detailed 2-D and 3-D images reflecting tissue chemistry.

---

## 1.5 Basic NMR Components — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** is the physical phenomenon underlying MRI: certain atomic nuclei (¹H, ¹³C, ³¹P, ¹⁹F) possess a non-zero **spin quantum number (I = ½)** and therefore a magnetic moment. When placed in an external magnetic field, they precess at a characteristic frequency (Larmor frequency) and can be tipped away from equilibrium by an RF pulse at that frequency, then detected as they relax back.

The **Basic NMR / MRI System** comprises five functional sub-systems:

1. **Main (Static) Magnet** — produces the strong, uniform **B₀** field.
2. **Gradient Coil System** — produces spatially varying **Gx, Gy, Gz** fields for slice selection and spatial encoding.
3. **Radio-Frequency (RF) Transmit Coil / Transmitter** — delivers the **B₁** excitation pulse at the Larmor frequency.
4. **RF Receive Coil / Receiver** — detects the faint NMR signal (Free Induction Decay, FID).
5. **Computer / Image Reconstruction System** — Fourier-transforms the k-space data into a viewable image.

---

## 1.6 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Compass in a Magnetic Storm"

Picture a child's **spinning top** (the spinning proton) placed inside a **giant invisible magnetic field** (the MRI scanner bore). What happens?

- The top's axis slowly *wobbles* (precesses) around the magnetic field lines at a precise frequency.
- If you **tap** the top with a tiny hammer (the RF pulse) at exactly the *right rhythm* (Larmor frequency), the wobble grows dramatically — **resonance** occurs.
- When the tapping stops, the top's wobble gradually dies down, and the **ringing sound** (the NMR signal) can be heard and recorded.
- By making the magnetic field *slightly different in different parts of the room* (gradient coils), the same rhythm corresponds to *different positions* — so you can figure out **where in the body** the sound came from.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Larmor Frequency** is the "magic" frequency at which a nucleus in a magnetic field absorbs and re-emits RF energy. For ¹H in a **1.5 T** field it is approximately **63.87 MHz**; in a **3 T** field it is **~127.74 MHz**.

> [!WARNING]
> **No ionising radiation** is used in MRI. Safety hazards are *mechanical* (projectile risk of ferromagnetic objects), *cryogenic* (liquid helium), and *RF heating* (SAR limits). KTU students are routinely examined on these hazards.

---

## 1.7 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- Speed of sound in soft tissue: **c ≈ 1540 m/s**
- Diagnostic ultrasound frequency range: **2 – 15 MHz**
- Acoustic impedance of soft tissue: **Z ≈ 1.63 × 10⁶ kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹** (Rayl)
- Gyromagnetic ratio of ¹H: **γ = 42.58 MHz/T**
- MRI field strengths in clinical use: **0.5 T, 1.5 T, 3 T** (research: 7 T and above)
- Typical Larmor frequency at 1.5 T: **63.87 MHz** (in the **FM radio band**)

---

## 1.8 Section-1 Quick Recall Diagram (Textual Mind-Map)

```
REAL-TIME ULTRASONIC IMAGING        |        MRI / NMR
--------------------------------    |    -----------------------
• Pulse-echo (A → M → B → Real-2D)  |    • Strong B₀ magnet
• Multi-element array probe         |    • Gradient coils (Gx,Gy,Gz)
• Electronic beam-steering          |    • RF transmit (B₁) coil
• Electronic beam-focusing          |    • RF receive coil + FID
• Frame rate ≈ 15–60 fps            |    • Larmor frequency
• Sector / linear / convex images   |    • T1, T2 relaxation
• No ionising radiation             |    • No ionising radiation
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Real-time Ultrasonic Imaging — Operational Pipeline

The complete signal-flow of a real-time B-mode scanner can be broken into **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Pulse Generation (TX beamformer)**
   - A short high-voltage pulse (typically **± 50 to ± 200 V**, duration ~ 0.1–1 µs) is applied to the piezoelectric elements.
   - The transmit beamformer introduces **precise time delays** to each element so that the acoustic wavefronts constructively interfere along a chosen line in space → **electronic steering and focusing**.

2. **Acoustic Propagation in Tissue**
   - The beam travels at **c ≈ 1540 m/s** in soft tissue.
   - At each acoustic-impedance discontinuity, a fraction of the energy is reflected back (echo) and the rest continues.

3. **Echo Reception (RX beamformer)**
   - The same (or adjacent) elements receive the returning echoes.
   - The receive beamformer applies **dynamic focusing delays** to the received signals and sums them coherently → improves lateral resolution.

4. **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)**
   - Deeper echoes are weaker because of attenuation (~ **0.5 dB/cm/MHz**, rule of thumb).
   - TGC applies a **time-varying gain** that increases with depth to equalise image brightness.

5. **Envelope Detection**
   - The high-frequency RF echo is rectified and low-pass filtered to extract the envelope (amplitude vs. time).

6. **Log Compression**
   - The envelope is logarithmically compressed (~ 40–60 dB dynamic range) so that weak and strong echoes can be displayed simultaneously in 8-bit gray-scale.

7. **Scan Conversion**
   - The acquired line data is re-mapped from polar coordinates (r, θ) to Cartesian pixel coordinates (x, y) for display on a rectangular monitor.

8. **Display**
   - Final image is shown on a monitor, refreshed at the **frame rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frame rate** is the single most important performance parameter of a real-time scanner. It must be high enough to *fool the human visual system* (persistence of vision ~ 50 ms) into perceiving a moving image.

---

## 2.2 Transducer (Probe) — Detailed Anatomy

A real-time ultrasound probe is *not* a single crystal but a **multi-element assembly**:

- **Piezoelectric elements** — typically lead zirconate titanate (**PZT**) or relaxor-PT single crystals (e.g., **PMN-PT**). Each element is ~ **0.2–0.5 mm wide**.
- **Backing (damping) material** — high-attenuation epoxy-rubber composite that absorbs backwards-radiated energy, shortening the pulse (improving axial resolution).
- **Matching layer(s)** — quarter-wavelength impedance-matching layers (Z ≈ √(Z_tissue × Z_PZT)) that maximise transmission of acoustic energy into tissue.
- **Lens (acoustic lens)** — focuses the beam in the **elevation (out-of-plane)** direction (the lens cannot be electronically focused; only the in-plane scan-plane can be).
- **Cable / connector** — typically **64, 96, 128, 192, or 256** active channels.

> [!NOTE]
> **PZT-5H, PZT-5A** are common piezoelectric ceramics. **PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride)** is used in some high-frequency intravascular probes because it is flexible and broadband.

---

## 2.3 Beam-forming: Steering & Focusing — The Heart of Real-time Imaging

### 2.3.1 Steering

In a linear array, all elements fire together → beam is **perpendicular to the array face**.

In a **phased array**, each element is fired with a small, progressive **time delay Δτ**:

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{d \, \sin\theta}{c}$$

where:
- *d* = element pitch (centre-to-centre spacing)
- *θ* = steering angle from the array normal
- *c* = speed of sound

By changing Δτ, the beam can be swept through a sector (typically **± 45°**) in microseconds — that is the entire basis of the *sector* cardiac probe.

### 2.3.2 Focusing

To focus at depth *F*, an additional **quadratic delay** is added so that the wavefronts from all elements arrive at the focal point in phase:

$$\tau_n = \frac{1}{c}\left(F - \sqrt{F^2 + x_n^2}\right) + \frac{x_n \sin\theta}{c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> On *receive*, dynamic focusing updates the delays continuously as echoes return from deeper depths — this is called **dynamic receive focusing** and is essentially free (digital delays). On *transmit*, only a single fixed focus can be applied per transmit event, so scanners commonly **transmit multiple lines at different focal depths** and interleave them.

---

## 2.4 Frame Rate — The Fundamental Trade-off

The **maximum achievable frame rate** is set by the time it takes the ultrasound to travel to the deepest point of interest and back:

$$\text{Frame Rate}_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot N \cdot D \cdot \cos\theta}$$

where:
- *c* = speed of sound (1540 m/s)
- *N* = number of scan lines per frame
- *D* = maximum imaging depth (m)
- *θ* = steering angle (for sector)

> [!TIP]
> A typical adult echo at 15 cm depth with 128 lines and cos θ ≈ 0.7 gives ~ **40 frames/s** — comfortably above the flicker threshold.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \frac{2d}{c}$ | Round-trip time of an echo from depth *d* | seconds |
| 2 | $f_0 = \frac{c}{\lambda}$ | Relation between frequency, speed, wavelength | Hz |
| 3 | $\Delta\tau = \dfrac{d\sin\theta}{c}$ | Steering delay for an array element | s |
| 4 | $\tau_n = \dfrac{F - \sqrt{F^2 + x_n^2}}{c}$ | Focusing delay (small-angle) | s |
| 5 | $FR_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2ND}$ | Maximum frame rate (linear array) | fps |
| 6 | $Z = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance | Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Pressure reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_t = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Pressure transmission coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\alpha \approx 0.5 \text{ dB/cm/MHz}$ | Attenuation in soft tissue | dB/cm/MHz |
| 10 | $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Pulse Repetition Frequency | Hz |
| 11 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor angular frequency | rad/s |
| 12 | $f_L = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ | Larmor linear frequency | Hz |
| 13 | $M_0 \propto \rho \cdot \gamma^2 \hbar^2 \, B_0 / T_s$ | Equilibrium magnetisation | A/m |
| 14 | $M_z(t) = M_0\!\left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$ | T1 (longitudinal) recovery | A/m |
| 15 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2}$ | T2 (transverse) decay | A/m |
| 16 | $\Delta \omega = \gamma \, \vec{G} \cdot \vec{r}$ | Frequency offset due to gradient | rad/s |
| 17 | $SNR \propto B_0^{1.5 \text{ to } 2}$ | MRI SNR vs field strength | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise items 1, 2, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 14, 15 — these are the equations KTU examiners set numerical problems on.**

---

## 2.5 Real-world Utility of Real-time Ultrasound

- **Obstetrics** — fetal growth monitoring, anomaly scans (4-D imaging).
- **Cardiology** — echocardiography, Doppler assessment of valve function.
- **Emergency medicine** — FAST scans (Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma).
- **Anaesthesia** — guided nerve blocks and central-line placement.
- **Vascular** — carotid intima-media thickness, deep-vein thrombosis screening.

---

## 2.6 MRI — Deeper Look at the Five NMR Sub-systems

### 2.6.1 Main Magnet (B₀)

- **Superconducting** (most clinical 1.5 T and 3 T systems): Nb-Ti or Nb₃Sn wire immersed in liquid **helium at 4.2 K**.
- **Permanent magnets** (open 0.3–0.5 T systems): rare-earth alloys, no cryogen.
- **Resistive electromagnets** (older 0.15–0.4 T systems): large power consumption.
- **Field uniformity** must be < **1 ppm** over a 40-cm DSV (diameter spherical volume) for proper imaging — this is achieved by **passive shimming (iron plates)** and **active shimming (extra coils)**.

### 2.6.2 Gradient Coil System

Three orthogonal coils (**Gx, Gy, Gz**) produce linear field variations of the form:

$$B_z(x,y,z) = B_0 + G_x x + G_y y + G_z z$$

Typical gradient strength: **30–80 mT/m**; slew rate: **150–250 T·m⁻¹·s⁻¹**. They are responsible for:

- **Slice selection** (turning on one gradient during the RF pulse → only one slice resonates).
- **Frequency encoding** (readout gradient during signal acquisition).
- **Phase encoding** (a brief gradient blip between excitation and readout).

### 2.6.3 RF Coils

- **Transmit coil** (often the body-coil built into the bore) produces the B₁ field.
- **Receive coils**: surface coils (small, high SNR, limited depth), phased-array coils (multiple elements combined for parallel imaging — SENSE, GRAPPA).
- Coil tuning is at the **Larmor frequency**; matching is to **50 Ω**.

### 2.6.4 Receiver and Signal Path

The FID is amplified by a **low-noise pre-amplifier (LNA, noise figure < 1 dB)**, then digitised by a fast ADC, then sent to the **reconstruction computer** where an **inverse Fourier transform** converts k-space data into image space.

### 2.6.5 Computer & Console

Runs the pulse sequence (e.g., spin-echo, gradient-echo, EPI, FLAIR), handles DICOM storage, and displays the reconstructed image.

> [!NOTE]
> **K-space** is the raw-data matrix. Each row is filled with one phase-encoding step. The centre of k-space contains low-frequency (contrast) information; the periphery contains high-frequency (edge/detail) information.

---

## 2.7 Real-world Utility of MRI

- **Neurology** — multiple sclerosis, stroke, brain tumours, dementia.
- **Musculoskeletal** — ligament tears, bone marrow oedema.
- **Cardiac** — viability, congenital heart disease, perfusion.
- **Oncology** — staging and treatment response.
- **Angiography (MRA)** — non-contrast renal-artery imaging, carotid plaque characterisation.

---

## 2.8 Clinical Comparison (Why Have Both?)

| Feature | Ultrasound | MRI |
|---|---|---|
| Ionising radiation | **None** | **None** |
| Real-time | **Yes** (15–60 fps) | No (slowest ≈ real-time echo planar) |
| Cost per scan | Low | High |
| Operator dependence | High | Moderate |
| Best for | Soft-tissue motion, vascular flow | Static soft-tissue contrast, brain, marrow |
| Portable / bedside | **Yes** | No (only in specialised units) |
| Contra-indications | None major | Pacemakers, ferromagnetic implants, claustrophobia |
